EcoDiesel: CP4 high-pressure fuel pump failure risk (see Grand Cherokee recall note; same Bosch pump family).

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: Oil per oil-life monitor, up to 10,000 mi/12 mo (severe duty 3,500–5,000 mi); 0W-20 (3.6/2.0T), 0W-40 (392), 5W-40 (EcoDiesel). Spark plugs 3.6: 100,000 mi (then every 50k); 2.0T plugs: shorter interval — verify against owner's manual. Coolant: OAT, 10 yr/150,000 mi. Transmission: "fill for life" under normal duty; severe/off-road/towing service fluid & filter ~60k (ZF and shop practice). All engines timing chain (no belt). Brake fluid every 2–3 years. Axle/transfer-case fluids inspect every 20k, change under severe duty. 4xe adds battery coolant and charging-system checks.

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: 0–60: JL 2.0T ≈6.1–7.0 sec (Road Test Reviews instrumented 6.1 on a 2018 Rubicon 2-dr; TFL ~7.0); JL 3.6 ≈6.8–7.5; 4xe ≈5.5–6.0; Rubicon 392: 4.0 sec (Car and Driver instrumented, 2021; Jeep claims 4.5) with ~12s quarter-mile. JK 3.6: Not verified — needs OEM source (period tests ~8 sec). Rubicon 392 EPA 13/17/14.

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: The largest aftermarket of any US SUV: lift kits (2.5–4.5"), 33–37" tires, regear packages (4.56/4.88/5.13), Dana 44/60 axle upgrades, bumpers/armor, winches, LED lighting, snorkels. Engine mods: cold-air intakes, cat-back exhausts, DiabloSport/JET tuners (JK 3.6), Sprintex/Edelbrock supercharger kits for JK/JL 3.6 (~300+ whp), 392 exhaust/tune work. Factory halo variants: Rubicon, Rubicon X, Willys, Moab, High Tide, Rubicon 392 (incl. 20th Anniversary and Final Edition).

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: JK (2007–2018) kept classic body-on-frame/solid-axle layout and added the first factory 4-door; from 2012 the 3.6 Pentastar replaced the 3.8 pushrod V6 with 285 hp and a 5-speed automatic. JL (2018+) uses a revised lighter frame with aluminum doors/hood/fenders (~200 lb lighter), electrically assisted recirculating-ball steering, and an 8-speed automatic; it added turbo, diesel, PHEV, and V8 powertrains for the first time in Wrangler history. 2024 refresh brought the 12.3" Uconnect 5, full-float Dana 44 rear (Rubicon), 35" tire availability and factory support for 40s. All engines chain-driven, coil-on-plug; 2.0T uses a twin-scroll turbo and water-cooled intercooler.

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: The Wrangler is the center of the Jeep universe — JKs are now the value/cult buy ("2016–2018 Rubicons offer 90% of new capability at ~50% cost"), while JL Rubicons and the 392 are the modern halo. Tuner support is unmatched: Mopar/JPP, AEV, MetalCloak, Rock Krawler, Teraflex all build complete programs. The 4xe is divisive off-road (great low-end electric torque) but its battery recalls have hurt its standing.

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Direct rival is the Ford Bronco (2021+), with the Toyota 4Runner as the durability benchmark. Wrangler beats both off-road (solid axles, lockers, 4:1 case, removable top/doors) and holds best-in-class resale (J.D. Power/ALG awards), but trails the 4Runner badly on long-term reliability and the Bronco on on-road steering refinement. Owns a unique niche — nothing else is a convertible solid-axle 4x4.

jeep-grand-cherokee.subtitle: Mid-size unibody SUV: 2015–2021 (WK2, fourth gen; carried over as "Grand Cherokee WK" through 2022), 2021–2026 (WL, fifth gen — Grand Cherokee L 3-row from 2021, 2-row from 2022; 4xe 2022–2025)

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: WK2: 3.6L Pentastar (ERB) V6 NA; 5.7L HEMI (EZH) OHV V8 with MDS cylinder deactivation; 6.4L (392) HEMI V8 (SRT); 6.2L supercharged HEMI "Hellcat" V8 (Trackhawk, 2018–2021); 3.0L EcoDiesel VM Motori A630 DOHC turbo-diesel V6 (2014–2019). WL: 3.6L Pentastar V6 (eTorque-equipped applications); 5.7L HEMI V8 (Grand Cherokee L only, 2021–2023); 4xe PHEV — 2.0L GME-T4 turbo + 100 kW transmission motor, 17.3 kWh pack (2022–2025); 2.0L Hurricane 4 EVO turbo added for 2026 (details limited — verify per VIN).

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: WK2: 8-speed TorqueFlite 845RE (early V6), 850RE (V6 from ~2018); 8-speed ZF 8HP70 "TorqueFlite 8" (5.7, EcoDiesel, SRT, Trackhawk). WL: 8-speed TorqueFlite 850RE across the range.

horsepower.label: Horsepower
horsepower.text: 3.6: 290 hp (2011–2015) → 295 hp @ 6,400 rpm (2016+, first application of the upgraded Pentastar per WardsAuto); WL 3.6: 293 hp. 5.7: 360 hp (WK2) / 357 hp (WL). 6.4 SRT: 470 hp (2012–2014) → 475 hp (2015+). Trackhawk: 707 hp @ 6,000 rpm. EcoDiesel: 240 hp. WL 4xe: 375 hp combined.

torque.label: Torque
torque.text: 3.6: 260 lb-ft @ 4,000–4,800 rpm. 5.7: 390 lb-ft. 6.4 SRT: 470 lb-ft @ 4,300 rpm. Trackhawk: 645 lb-ft @ 4,800 rpm. EcoDiesel: 420 lb-ft. WL 4xe: 470 lb-ft combined.

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: WK2: RWD standard on V6; 4WD — Quadra-Trac I (single-speed), Quadra-Trac II (2-speed, 2.72:1 low), Quadra-Drive II (adds rear ELSD); SRT/Trackhawk use full-time performance AWD transfer cases. WL: RWD standard (V6); Quadra-Trac I, Quadra-Trac II, Quadra-Drive II with ELSD and available Quadra-Lift air suspension; 4xe is 4WD-only.

dimensions.label: Dimensions
dimensions.text: WK2: WB 114.8 in, L 189.8 in, W 76.3 in, H 69.3 in; curb 4,533 lb (V6 RWD) to ~5,264 lb (V8 4WD), Trackhawk ~5,356 lb. WL 2-row: WB 116.7 in, L 193.5 in; WL L 3-row: WB 121.7 in, L 204.9 in; W 77.9 in, H ~71.5 in; curb 4,513–5,558 lb.

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: Mixed generation-level record. WK2: mid-pack; the 3.6/8-speed drivetrain is well understood and durable with the oil-housing and valvetrain caveats below; SRT/Trackhawk robust but consumable-heavy. WK2 earned high owner-satisfaction; Trackhawk holds value strongly. WL: improved build quality; IIHS crash scores Good on the current structure. Both 4xe models carry the Samsung-cell HV battery recalls (see below). NHTSA complaint data clusters on WK2 electrical/powertrain and on WL/4xe electrical.

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: WK2: monostable electronic shifter rollaway recall (2014–2015, part of a 1.1M-vehicle recall with 41 injuries reported; software fix) — a documented fatality driver; 3.6 oil filter housing/cooler leaks and rocker-arm/lifter tick; Quadra-Lift air suspension compressor and air-spring failures; transfer-case actuator and parking-pawl recalls; 2014–2015 included in the 1.4M-vehicle Uconnect remote-hack recall (Miller/Valasek Cherokee hack). EcoDiesel: EPA/DOJ emissions-cheating consent decree (~$300M penalties, approved emissions modification recall), EGR cooler/soot and oil-cooler failures, bottom-end bearing failures, crankshaft position sensor recall, and Bosch CP4 high-pressure fuel pump self-destruction — 2022 recall of 138,645 (2014–2020 Grand Cherokee, 2014–2019 Ram 1500). WL/4xe: HV battery fire recalls 24V720 (154,032 units) and 25V741 (320,065 units combined Wrangler/GC 4xe, Oct 2025, park-outside order); early Uconnect 5/software glitches.

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: Oil per oil-life monitor, up to 10,000 mi/12 mo; 0W-20 (3.6), 5W-20 (5.7), 0W-40 (6.4/6.2 SC), 5W-40 (EcoDiesel, plus DEF service and diesel fuel filter intervals). Spark plugs 3.6/5.7: 100,000 mi (then every 50k); 392/Trackhawk ~60k — verify per manual. Coolant: OAT 10 yr/150,000 mi. ZF 8HP70/TorqueFlite: "fill for life" normal duty; ZF and shop practice fluid + pan/filter ~60–80k, sooner for towing/track use. All engines timing chain. Brake fluid every 2–3 years; SRT/Trackhawk need frequent fluid and pad/rotor service. Transfer case and axle fluids under severe-duty schedule. EcoDiesel adds EGR/DPF monitoring.

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: 0–60: 3.6 ≈7.5–8.2 (period tests; not freshly verified — treat as approximate); 5.7 ≈6.5–7.0 (approximate); SRT 4.4–4.8 (KBB cites 4.8 for 2018); Trackhawk 3.5 sec with 11.6-sec quarter-mile (multiple instrumented tests; fastest SUV of its era). WL 3.6: Not verified — needs OEM source. WL 4xe: Not verified — needs OEM source (press estimates mid-5s). WK2 towing: up to 6,200 lb (3.6), 7,200 lb (5.7), 7,200 lb (Trackhawk).

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: WK2 has deep Mopar/aftermarket support: DiabloSport inTune/Trinity tuners and PCM swaps (5.7/6.4), cold-air intakes (aFe etc.), long-tube headers and cat-backs for HEMIs, Trackhawk pulley/tune packages pushing 800+ hp, lowering springs and 22" wheels for SRT/Trackhawk; overland-style lifts (~1.5–2"), skid plates and A/T tires on Trailhawk trims (Eibach Pro-Truck kits now exist for WL). Factory performance variants: SRT (2012–2021), Trackhawk (2018–2021), plus Trailhawk off-road trim (2017–2021 WK2; WL Trailhawk 4xe 2022+).

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: WK2 (2011–2022) rides on a Chrysler-developed unibody platform later adopted by the Mercedes-Benz W166 ML/GLE — independent suspension all around, optional Quadra-Lift air springs, and the first Grand Cherokee diesel and 707-hp supercharged variants; 2014 brought the 8-speed and EcoDiesel, 2016 the upgraded 295-hp Pentastar, 2017–2018 the Trailhawk and Trackhawk. WL (2021+) moves to the Giorgio-derived architecture shared with Alfa Romeo Stelvio/Maserati Grecale, adds a 3-row L body, Uconnect 5 with passenger screen, available McIntosh audio, and the 4xe PHEV; the V8 was phased out of the 2-row after 2023.

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: The Trackhawk is a modern cult classic — "the Hellcat you can daily and tow with" — and clean examples command strong money. SRT WK2s are the budget super-SUV. Off-roaders like the WK2/WL Trailhawks for Quadra-Drive II, but hard-core builders stay with Wranglers; tuning culture centers on HEMI power adders rather than rock crawling.

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Rivals: Ford Explorer, Toyota 4Runner/Highlander, Dodge Durango (corporate cousin), BMW X5, Land Rover Defender. Grand Cherokee undercuts the Germans on price with comparable powertrains and beats Explorer/Highlander on capability and V8 availability, but its reliability record (especially EcoDiesel and air suspension) trails Toyota, and WL interior quality, while improved, still meets mixed reviews against the X5/Defender.

jeep-cherokee.subtitle: Compact/mid-size unibody crossover: 2015–2018 (KL, pre-facelift), 2019–2023 (KL facelift; US production ended Feb 28, 2023). Nameplate returns as the 2026 Cherokee (KM) hybrid — all-new model, not covered here; verify separately.

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: 2.4L Tigershark (ED6) MultiAir2 SOHC I4 NA (2014–2021; 2023 Altitude Lux); 3.2L Pentastar (EHB) DOHC V6 NA (2015–2022; standard 2022); 2.0L GME-T4 "Hurricane" turbo I4 (2019+; 2023 Trailhawk). All transverse-mounted; all timing chain.

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: 9-speed ZF 948TE (Chrysler-built ZF 9HP48) automatic on every US-model Cherokee; no manual offered in the US. FWD or 4WD.

horsepower.label: Horsepower
horsepower.text: 2.4 Tigershark: 184 hp (171 lb-ft) — 180 hp quoted on some 2019+ materials; 3.2 V6: 271 hp; 2.0T: 270 hp @ 5,200 rpm.

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: FWD standard; 4WD via three systems — Active Drive I (single-speed PTU with rear-axle disconnect), Active Drive II (two-speed PTU, 2.92:1 low range), Active Drive Lock (Trailhawk; adds mechanical locking rear differential). Selec-Terrain modes on 4WD models.

dimensions.label: Dimensions
dimensions.text: WB 106.3 in, L 182.0 in, W 73.2 in (74.9 in Trailhawk), H 66.2–67.8 in; curb 3,655–4,046 lb.

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: One of FCA's weakest reliability performers in early years: 2014–2015 models drew very high complaint counts (carcomplaints/safecar), almost all transmission-related; Consumer Reports' poor dependability review of the Cherokee and siblings was widely reported (FCA's chief quality officer departed shortly after). Improved markedly after 2016–2017 software and hardware revisions; 2019+ facelift cars are materially better. IIHS 2022: Good in all crash tests; headlights Acceptable/Marginal by trim. Named sources: Consumer Reports, NHTSA complaints via safecar.gov, Car and Driver's 9-speed coverage.

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: (1) ZF 948TE 9-speed — the model's defining defect: harsh/erratic shifts, hesitation, limp mode, unexpected shift-to-neutral; NHTSA recall 16V-529/S55 (2014–2015, TCM/harness crimps), ZF-side recall of ~505,000 units for a control sensor that could drop the trans into neutral, plus a long TSB chain (18-018-17, 21-008-17, multiple TCM flashes) and "snap ring" durability repairs; subject of class-action litigation. (2) 2.4L Tigershark oil consumption — Wood et al. v. FCA class action (final settlement approval Dec 2022; ~1.6M vehicles incl. 2014–2019 Cherokee built before July 2018); defective pistons/rings, oil-control PCV strategy; can stall engines. (3) PTU/rear-drive-module failures on Active Drive II/4WD cars ("service 4x4" warnings; PTU, rear diff, RDM replacement documented). (4) 2014–2015 Uconnect remote-hack recall (1.4M vehicles, Miller/Valasek demonstration). (5) 2019 recall (~86k) for software that could cause stalls.

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: Oil per monitor, up to 10,000 mi/12 mo — on 2.4 Tigershark cars, shops should advise checking oil level monthly and shorter intervals (class-action cars burned a quart per ~1,000 mi in severe cases); 0W-20 (2.4/3.2), 5W-30 (2.0T). Spark plugs 100,000 mi (then every 50k). Coolant: OAT 10 yr/150,000 mi. 948TE fluid: "fill for life" normal duty, but fluid changes ~60k are cheap insurance given the unit's history; requires the ZF quick-learn procedure after service with a capable scan tool. PTU/RDM gear oil on 4WD models per severe-duty schedule. Brake fluid every 2–3 years. All engines timing chain.

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: 0–60: 2.0T ≈7.5 sec (MotorTrend instrumented data via FastestLaps); 3.2 V6 ≈7.2–7.5 (period tests; approximate — not freshly verified); 2.4 ≈8.5–9.5 (approximate — not freshly verified). Best-in-class V6 tow rating at launch: 4,500 lb (2,000 lb on 2.4). No factory performance trim; Trailhawk is the capability flagship.

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: Modest scene: Trailhawk lift kits (~1.5–2", Rocky Road/MFC), 245–265 A/T tires, skid plates, roof racks, light bars. No meaningful engine tuning for the 948TE driveline; the 2.0T responds to intake/exhaust but the transmission limits hard use. Factory capability variants: Trailhawk (2014–2022), Trailhawk L/Elite, plus Overland luxury trim.

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: First Jeep on the FCA Compact US Wide (CUSW) platform shared with the Chrysler 200 — transverse engines, unibody, and the industry's first 9-speed automatic in an SUV, launched weeks late because of transmission software. Segment-first rear-axle disconnect for 4WD fuel economy. The 2019 facelift restyled the polarizing "squint" front end, added the 2.0T, and cut ~150 lb via an aluminum hood/liftgate and composite tailgate. Production moved Toledo → Belvidere in 2017; ended February 2023 when Stellantis idled Belvidere.

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: Enthusiast respect is limited — XJ loyalists rejected the KL's crossover layout, and the 9-speed's reputation stuck. The Trailhawk has a genuine niche following as a capable, compact overlander with a real low range and locker. Tuner support is thin; most community effort goes into making Trailhawks trail-worthy, not fast.

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Rivals: Toyota RAV4, Honda CR-V, Ford Escape, Subaru Forester/Outback. The Cherokee counters with real 4WD hardware (low range, locker, 4,500-lb tow) that none of them offer, but the RAV4/CR-V/Forester all beat it on reliability, resale, and interior packaging; the 2.4/9-speed powertrain also trails on refinement and fuel economy in real-world use.

jeep-compass.subtitle: Compact unibody crossover: 2015–2016 (MK49, first gen; final MKs sold into early 2017), 2017–2026 (MP/552, second gen; 2022 interior/tech facelift; 2.0T powertrain from 2023). Third gen (J4U) unveiled May 2025 but North American production doesn't start until late 2027 (2028 MY) — the MP remains the US-market Compass through at least 2026.

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: MK (2015–2016): 2.0L GEMA "World" DOHC I4 NA (FWD Sport only); 2.4L GEMA "World" DOHC I4 NA. MP (2017–2026): 2.4L Tigershark (ED6) MultiAir2 SOHC I4 NA (2017–2022; 177 hp for 2022); 2.0L GME-T4 turbo I4 (2023+). All timing chain.

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: MK: 5-speed Magna T355 manual; 6-speed Hyundai 6F24 (PowerTech) torque-converter automatic (from 2014); Jatco JF011E CVT2 — mandatory with Freedom Drive II (19:1 simulated low range). MP: 6-speed manual (early Sport), 6-speed Aisin TF-60SN automatic (FWD), 9-speed ZF 948TE automatic (4WD, 2017–2022); 8-speed Hyundai 8F30 automatic (2023+, standard 4WD).

horsepower.label: Horsepower
horsepower.text: MK 2.0: 158 hp; MK 2.4: 172 hp. MP 2.4 Tigershark: 180 hp (177 hp for 2022); MP 2.0T: 200 hp.

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: MK: FWD standard; Freedom Drive I full-time 4WD; Freedom Drive II (CVT-based, off-road package, Trail Rated from 2011). MP: FWD standard (Sport/Latitude); 4WD optional, standard on Trailhawk (Active Drive with low-range simulation via 9-speed's gearing + Selec-Terrain incl. Rock mode); all 2023+ models are 4WD-only.

dimensions.label: Dimensions
dimensions.text: MK: WB 103.7 in, L 173.4 in, W 69.3 in, H 65.2 in; curb 3,219–3,715 lb. MP: WB 103.9 in, L 174.0 in, W 71.7 in, H 65.0 in; curb ≈3,318–3,900 lb (US trims; global figure to 4,266 lb includes Euro 4xe PHEV).

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: MK: below average; CVT cars are the problem children (see below). MP: poor-to-mixed — NHTSA-based analysis counts 1,729 complaints, 14 recalls, and 11 fire reports across 2017–2025, with the 2.4 Tigershark (2018–2022) accounting for ~93% of complaints (engine and electrical split nearly evenly); the 2023+ 2.0T/8-speed reset the powertrain story and is the better bet. IIHS 2022: Good in all crash tests (updated side test Marginal); top trims earned a Top Safety Pick award. Euro NCAP 2 stars (2012 MK) / strong ANCAP 5-star (2017 MP).

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: (1) MK Jatco JF011E CVT2 — overheating, whine, judder, premature failure; FCA faced a class action over the CVT in Compass/Patriot/Caliber. (2) MP 2.4 Tigershark oil consumption — covered by the Wood v. FCA settlement (2017–2018 Compass; same piston/ring defect family as Cherokee, ~1.6M vehicles); PCM reflash recall added limp-mode protection. (3) MP 948TE 9-speed shift-quality complaints and TSB flashes (same ZF family as Cherokee, though lower complaint volume). (4) Early MK TIPM electrical faults; MK suspension wear (front struts, control arms). (5) Various small recalls across MP (backup camera, airbags, software) — 14 total per NHTSA-based count.

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: Oil per monitor, up to 10,000 mi/12 mo; 2.4 Tigershark: check level between services (oil-consumption history), 0W-20; 2.0T: 5W-30. Spark plugs 100,000 mi (then every 50k). Coolant: OAT 10 yr/150,000 mi. CVT2 (MK): fluid every 60k with correct CVTF+4 — critical to survival. 6F24/TF-60SN/948TE/8F30: "fill for life" normal duty; service ~60–120k (Firestone/OEM schedule shows automatic transaxle fluid + filter at 120k normal duty; sooner for severe). Brake fluid every 2–3 years. All engines timing chain (no belt).

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: 0–60 — MK 2.4: Not verified — needs OEM source (period tests ~9–10 sec). MP 2.4: Not verified — needs OEM source (~9+ sec in period tests). MP 2.0T (2023+): Not verified — needs OEM source (notably quicker than the 2.4; press estimates ~7.5 sec). No factory performance trims; Trailhawk is the capability variant.

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: Small but real scene on MP Trailhawk: 1.5–2" lifts, 225–245 A/T tires, skid plates, roof platforms; appearance mods dominate on street trims. MK is largely ignored by enthusiasts. No mainstream ECU tuning for the 2.4; the 2.0T's 8F30 swap is too new for a mature aftermarket.

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: The MK (2007–2017) rode the DaimlerChrysler/Mitsubishi GS (PM/MK) platform shared with the Dodge Caliber — Jeep's first car-based crossover; 2011 brought the Grand Cherokee-style facelift and Trail Rated Freedom Drive II; 2014 replaced most CVTs with the Hyundai 6F24 6-speed. The MP (2017+) sits on the stretched FCA Small Wide LWB platform shared with the Renegade/Fiat 500X/Toro, with 65%+ high-strength steel in the upper structure, Tigershark power, and the Cherokee's 9-speed; the 2022 refresh redid the interior (10.25" cluster, 10.1" Uconnect 5) and made full ADAS standard; 2023 replaced the 2.4/9-speed with the 200-hp 2.0T and Hyundai 8F30 8-speed and standardized 4WD.

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: No cult following — the MK is remembered as the Jeep that wasn't, and the MP is bought on price and looks. The MP Trailhawk earns some respect as a soft-roader with real trail geometry, and 2023+ 2.0T cars are considered "finally adequate" by reviewers. Tuner support is minimal.

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Rivals: Honda CR-V, Toyota RAV4, Mazda CX-5/CX-50, Subaru Crosstrek/Forester, Hyundai Tucson, Kia Sportage. Compass fights with style, Jeep branding, and (Trailhawk) light off-road ability at a discount price, but trails the class leaders on powertrain refinement (2.4/9-speed), reliability history, and resale; the 2.0T narrowed the drivability gap from 2023.

jeep-gladiator.subtitle: Midsize body-on-frame pickup: 2020–2026 (JT, single generation; shares the Wrangler JL's frame, solid axles, powertrains, and removable top/doors with a 5-ft steel bed). 2024 refresh mirrors the JL update (12.3" Uconnect 5, side-curtain airbags); EcoDiesel dropped after 2023, leaving the 3.6 V6 as the sole engine. The only open-air convertible pickup sold in the US.

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: JT (2020–2026): 3.6L Pentastar (ERB) DOHC V6, naturally aspirated (sole engine 2024+); 3.0L EcoDiesel VM Motori L630 DOHC turbo-diesel V6 (2021–2023 only). All timing chain.

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: 6-speed Aisin AL6 manual (3.6 only); 8-speed TorqueFlite 850RE automatic (3.6); 8-speed ZF 8HP75 automatic (EcoDiesel).

horsepower.label: Horsepower
horsepower.text: 3.6 V6: 285 hp @ 6,400 rpm. EcoDiesel: 260 hp.

torque.label: Torque
torque.text: 3.6 V6: 260 lb-ft @ 4,800 rpm. EcoDiesel: 442 lb-ft.

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: 4WD standard (no 2WD variant sold in the US). Sport/Overland/Mojave: Command-Trac part-time (2.72:1 low); Selec-Trac full-time available (Overland/Mojave, 2023+ availability varies by trim); Rubicon: Rock-Trac (4:1 low) with Tru-Lok electronic lockers front/rear and electronic sway-bar disconnect. Mojave is the desert-rated variant: Fox 2.5" internal-bypass shocks, hydraulic jounce bumpers, 33" tires, reinforced frame and cast-iron steering knuckles.

dimensions.label: Dimensions
dimensions.text: L 218.0 in, WB 137.3 in, W 73.8 in, H ~73.1–76.1 in (varies by trim/top; US News lists 75.0 in for a 2025 — exact per-trim heights not verified against OEM sheet). Curb weight: Not verified — needs OEM source (≈4,650–5,050 lb across trims per secondary sources). Bed: 5.0 ft steel box, 35.5 cu ft.

mpg.label: Fuel economy (MPG)
mpg.text: EPA (fueleconomy.gov) — 2025 3.6 auto 4WD 17/22/19; 2022 3.6 manual 16/23/19; 2021 EcoDiesel 4WD 22/28/24 (Rubicon EcoDiesel 21/27/24).

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: Below average in survey data, tracking the Wrangler JL it is based on; early-build 2020–2021 trucks carried the JL's launch-quality problems plus truck-specific drivetrain recalls. Complaint volume is materially lower than the Wrangler's but the defect mix (steering, electrical, driveline) is the same. No independent long-term study exists yet for the EcoDiesel in this application. Not independently crash-tested by IIHS (JL Wrangler small-overlap result was Marginal with a rollover onto the side; the JT adds side-curtain airbags for 2024+).

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: (1) NHTSA 20V-124 — clutch pressure-plate overheating/fracture with fire risk on manual-transmission trucks (shared with JL Wrangler). (2) NHTSA 21V-028 — rear driveshaft separation from an improperly welded slip-yoke (2020–2021). (3) NHTSA 22V-544 — fuel-tank weld defect, potential leak. (4) Sep 2024 recall — instrument-panel display failure (~32,863 units). (5) Shop-documented: front driveshaft CV-joint failure after suspension lifts, rear axle pinion seal leaks, exhaust-manifold tick, steering stabilizer wear/"death wobble" (JL-shared), 3.6 oil filter housing/cooler adapter leaks (JL-shared). EcoDiesel: CP4-family high-pressure fuel pump failure risk (same Bosch pump family as the Grand Cherokee/EcoDiesel recall).

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: Oil per oil-life monitor, up to 10,000 mi/12 mo (severe 3,500–5,000 mi); 0W-20 (3.6), 5W-40 (EcoDiesel). Spark plugs (3.6): 100,000 mi (then every 50k). Coolant: OAT, 10 yr/150,000 mi. Transmissions: "fill for life" normal duty; service fluid & filter ~60k under towing/off-road use (ZF/shop practice). Manual-transmission clutch: inspect per the 20V-124 recall remedy. Axle/transfer-case fluids inspect every 20k, change under severe duty. Brake fluid every 2–3 years. All engines timing chain (no belt).

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: 0–60: 3.6 auto: Not verified — needs OEM source (period Car and Driver instrumented test ~7.2 sec; most secondary tests 7.2–7.6). EcoDiesel: Not verified — needs OEM source. Max towing 7,700 lb (Sport S with Max Tow package; some dealer materials cite 7,650); max payload 1,720 lb. Tow rating drops to ~4,000–6,500 lb on Rubicon/Mojave (heavy suspension hardware). Top speed electronically limited (~110 mph).

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: Shares the Wrangler aftermarket nearly 1:1: 2.5–4" lifts, 35–37" tires (regear strongly recommended — the 3.6 strains on 37s at 5,000+ lb curb), regear packages, bumpers/winches, bed racks, overland camper shells (very popular overlanding platform), AEV/MetalCloak/Teraflex complete kits. Engine mods limited to intakes/exhausts and mild tunes; no mainstream forced induction for the JT 3.6 yet. Mopar/JPP factory accessories include half-doors, tonneau covers, and a 2" lift.

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: The JT (2020+) is a Wrangler JL from the B-pillar forward — same ladder frame (extended rearward), same Dana 44 front/rear axles, same coil-link suspension — with a five-link solid rear axle packaged under a steel 5-ft bed; rear suspension is retuned for payload. Removable roof panels (Freedom Top hardtop or soft top) and removable doors carry over, making it the only convertible pickup on sale. 2024 refresh added the 12.3" Uconnect 5, standard side-curtain airbags front and rear, and Rubicon X trim. EcoDiesel deleted after 2023 as FCA wound down the VM V6.

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: Strong following among overlanders and Wrangler owners who want a bed; the Mojave is the cult pick for desert running ("the truck the Raptor crowd respects"), the Rubicon for rocks. Resale values track Wrangler strength. EcoDiesel trucks are the least-loved (fuel-system risk) but most efficient; 3.6 manuals are connoisseur picks. Tuner support equals the JL's.

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Rivals: Toyota Tacoma, Ford Ranger, Chevrolet Colorado/GMC Canyon, Nissan Frontier. Gladiator is the off-road and open-air king of the segment (solid axles, lockers, convertible) and tops the class on max towing in Max Tow trim, but trails the Colorado and Tacoma on ride/handling refinement, payload on off-road trims, and reliability history; it is also the priciest mainstream midsizer and the only one without a 2WD fleet variant.

jeep-renegade.subtitle: Subcompact unibody crossover: 2015–2023 US (BU/520, single generation; built in Melfi, Italy on the FCA Small Wide 4×4 platform shared with the Fiat 500X). 2019 facelift brought LED lighting, larger grille, updated Uconnect, and the 1.3T engine. Discontinued in the US/Canada after 2023 following years of declining sales; continues abroad (Europe, Latin America) with hybrid-only powertrains from 2024, including a 4xe PHEV never sold in the US.

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: BU US (2015–2023): 1.4L FIRE MultiAir turbo I4 (2015–2018); 2.4L Tigershark (ED6) MultiAir2 SOHC I4 NA (2015–2020 US; dropped for US after 2020); 1.3L GSE-T4 MultiAir3 turbo I4 (2019–2023; sole US engine 2021+). All timing chain except the 2.4 Tigershark (timing chain as well — no belt in the US range).

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: 6-speed Fiat C635 manual (1.4T, 2015–2018); 9-speed ZF 948TE torque-converter automatic (2.4, all 1.3T, and optional on 1.4T). No CVT offered in the US.

horsepower.label: Horsepower
horsepower.text: 1.4T: 160 hp. 2.4 Tigershark: 180 hp. 1.3T: 177 hp.

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: FWD standard on Sport/Latitude/Limited (through 2022); 4WD optional on those trims and standard on Trailhawk — Active Drive (single-speed, disconnecting rear axle) and Active Drive Low (Trailhawk, 20:1 crawl ratio achieved via the 9-speed's ultra-low first gear) with Selec-Terrain including Rock mode and a 0.79-in suspension lift, skid plates, and unique fascias. For 2023 US, FWD and the base Sport trim were dropped — 4WD became standard across the board.

dimensions.label: Dimensions
dimensions.text: WB 101.2 in, L 166.6 in, W 71.1 in, H 66.5 in; curb 3,078–3,549 lb (Wikipedia spec table). US Trailhawk with 4WD and options sits at the top of that range; verify per VIN for quotes.

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: Poor to mixed — the weakest reliability record in the US Jeep lineup alongside the Compass. The 2.4 Tigershark's oil-consumption defect drove the bulk of powertrain complaints (same engine family and class-action settlement as the Cherokee KL/Compass MP); the 948TE 9-speed generated shift-quality complaints and multiple TSB reflashes; electrical and infotainment faults are common. IIHS (2021): Good in small overlap driver, moderate overlap, original side, roof, head restraints; Acceptable passenger small overlap; Poor in the updated moderate-overlap test and Marginal in the updated side test. NHTSA (2016): 4-star overall. Latin NCAP dropped the basic configuration to 1 star in 2023.

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: (1) 2.4 Tigershark excessive oil consumption — covered by the Wood v. FCA settlement (2015–2018 Renegade included, ~1.6M vehicles, final approval Dec 2022; piston/ring defect; PCM reflash added limp-mode protection). NHTSA consumer-safety notification W80 addressed oil consumption on 2015–2018 2.4s. (2) NHTSA 16V-529 / FCA S55 — transaxle wire-harness crimp fault causing sudden loss of propulsion, includes 2015 Renegade 9-speed. (3) 948TE 9-speed harsh/erratic shifting — TSB reflashes (e.g., 18-018-17, 21-008-17) and quick-learn procedures. (4) 1.4T MultiAir actuator faults and ignition-coil failures (top complaint items per 1A Auto's top-5 problems list, along with heater-hose leaks and front CV boots). (5) 1.3T: turbo oil-line and wastegate complaints at higher mileage; some oil-consumption reports (verify per VIN). (6) My Sky removable roof panels — wind noise and water leaks.

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: Oil per oil-life monitor, up to 10,000 mi/12 mo (severe 3,500–5,000 mi); 0W-20 (2.4, 1.3T), 5W-40 (1.4T); on 2.4 cars check level between services (oil-consumption history). Spark plugs: 100,000 mi on 2.4 (then every 50k); 1.4T/1.3T per owner's manual (iridium, verify). Coolant: OAT, 10 yr/150,000 mi. 948TE: "fill for life" normal duty; service ~60k under severe duty. Brake fluid every 2–3 years. All US engines timing chain (no belt).

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: 0–60 — 1.4T 6MT: Not verified — needs OEM source. 2.4 9AT: Not verified — needs OEM source (period tests ~9 sec). 1.3T: Not verified — needs OEM source (press estimates ~9 sec). No factory performance trims; Trailhawk is the capability variant. Max tow rating 2,000 lb (4WD with tow package).

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: Small but active scene on the Trailhawk: 1.5" lifts (AVID, Daystar), 225/65R17 A/T tires, skid plates, roof baskets; the boxy styling drives appearance mods (decals, light bars) on street trims. No meaningful ECU tuning market for any US engine; the 1.4T shared some Fiat 500 Abarth-adjacent parts interest early on. Largely ignored by serious off-road builders.

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: The BU (2015+) was Jeep's first model designed and built entirely outside North America (Melfi, Italy, alongside the Fiat 500X), on the FCA Small Wide 4×4 platform; Trailhawk cars earned a genuine Trail Rated badge with Active Drive Low's 20:1 crawl ratio, a factory lift, skid plates, and tow hooks. The 2019 facelift added LED lighting inspired by the Wrangler, a larger grille, Apple CarPlay/Android Auto, and replaced the 1.4T with the 1.3L GSE-T4; 2022 made the 8.4" Uconnect 4C standard and dropped the 2.4 in the US; 2023 made 4WD standard before US discontinuation. Optional My Sky dual removable roof panels gave it Wrangler-style open-air ability unique in the subcompact class.

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: Modest but loyal following — owners like the character, boxy styling, and the Trailhawk's surprising trail ability for a subcompact ("the baby Jeep that actually trails"). It never developed a tuning culture; the 1.4T/6-speed manual cars are the connoisseur pick. Discontinuation after 2023 was met with little protest given the reliability record and aging platform.

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Rivals: Honda HR-V, Mazda CX-30, Subaru Crosstrek, Hyundai Kona, Kia Seltos, Chevrolet Trailblazer. Renegade won on styling, character, My Sky roof, and (Trailhawk) real trail hardware, but trailed the entire class on reliability history, powertrain refinement (948TE), and resale; the Crosstrek beat it at its own outdoorsy game with better durability and standard AWD value.

jeep-wagoneer.subtitle: Full-size body-on-frame luxury SUV: 2022–2026 (WS; Wagoneer and upmarket Grand Wagoneer, each in standard and long-wheelbase "L" form; built at Warren Truck Assembly on a Ram 1500 DT-derived frame). V8s dropped after 2023 (Wagoneer) / 2024 (Grand Wagoneer) in favor of the 3.0L Hurricane twin-turbo I6. The Wagoneer nameplate is dropped after 2025 — the Grand Wagoneer carries the line alone for 2026 with a base Hurricane SO. Wagoneer S (2024+), a battery-electric midsize crossover on STLA Large, is covered in the sub-block below.

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: Wagoneer: 5.7L HEMI (EZH) OHV V8 with eTorque 48V mild hybrid (2022–2023); 3.0L Hurricane twin-turbo DOHC I6, standard output (2023–2025; 2026 Grand Wagoneer base). Grand Wagoneer: 6.4L (392) HEMI OHV V8 (2022–2023); 3.0L Hurricane twin-turbo I6, high output (2022–2024); 3.0L Hurricane HO uprated (2025+). All timing chain. (Note: Wikipedia's infobox lists a 3.6L Pentastar for 2021–2024 — no US-market Wagoneer was sold with the Pentastar; likely Ram-platform infobox carryover. Excluded pending OEM confirmation.)

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: 8-speed ZF 8HP75 "TorqueFlite 8" automatic across all engines and years.

horsepower.label: Horsepower
horsepower.text: Wagoneer 5.7 eTorque: 392 hp @ 5,600 rpm. Hurricane SO: 420 hp @ 5,200 rpm. Grand Wagoneer 6.4: 471 hp @ 6,000 rpm. Hurricane HO (2022–2024): 510 hp @ 5,700 rpm. Hurricane HO (2025+): 540 hp @ 6,200 rpm.

torque.label: Torque
torque.text: 5.7 eTorque: 404 lb-ft @ 3,950 rpm. Hurricane SO: 468 lb-ft @ 3,500 rpm. 6.4: 455 lb-ft @ 4,400 rpm. Hurricane HO (2022–2024): 500 lb-ft @ 3,500 rpm. Hurricane HO (2025+): 521 lb-ft @ 3,500 rpm.

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: Wagoneer: RWD standard, 4WD optional. Grand Wagoneer: 4WD standard. 4WD systems: Quadra-Trac I (full-time, single-speed); Quadra-Trac II (two-speed transfer case with low range and hill-descent control, standard on Series III); Quadra-Drive II (adds rear electronic limited-slip differential, Grand Wagoneer). Quadra-Lift air suspension available (Grand Wagoneer) with up to 10 in ground clearance and 24 in water fording.

dimensions.label: Dimensions
dimensions.text: WB 123.0 in (standard) / 130.0 in (L); L 214.7 in / 226.7 in (L); W 83.6 in (with mirrors); H 75.6 in. Curb: 5,960–6,420 lb standard wheelbase; 6,068–6,704 lb (L).

mpg.label: Fuel economy (MPG)
mpg.text: EPA (fueleconomy.gov) — 2022: Wagoneer 5.7 2WD 16/22/18, 4WD 15/20/17; Grand Wagoneer 3.0TT 2WD 15/21/17, 4WD 14/20/17. 2025: Wagoneer 3.0TT 2WD 17/24/20, L 2WD 17/24/20, 4WD 16/23/19, L 4WD 16/23/19; Grand Wagoneer 4WD 14/20/17.

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: Weak early record with heavy recall and complaint volume — NHTSA-based analysis counts 332 complaints and 16 recalls across 2022–2025, and quality-control concerns have been widely reported (over 100,000 unsold units as of December 2024). NHTSA opened a 2024 investigation into 2022 5.7 eTorque trucks stalling/shifting to park at low speed (80+ complaints), overlapping an April 2023 PCM stall recall covering ~131,000 Ram/Wagoneer vehicles. Used-car guides advise avoiding 2022 and preferring 2023–2024 builds. Bright spot: IIHS 2024 Top Safety Pick — the only one of the three bestselling full-size SUVs to earn it (small overlap Good, updated side Good, updated moderate overlap Marginal).

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: (1) 2022 5.7 eTorque low-speed stalling / unexpected shift-to-park — subject of an April 2023 PCM recall (~131k units incl. Ram) and a separate 2024 NHTSA defect investigation. (2) Thermostat drive-pin binding complaints in NHTSA filings (cooling-system failures). (3) 2022 Grand Wagoneer brake-failure-after-pothole complaints (law-firm documented; sudden pedal loss). (4) Broad launch-quality problems: electrical/infotainment glitches (multi-screen Uconnect 5 stack), trim and fit issues — 16 recalls total 2022–2025. (5) Hurricane I6: too new for a statistically meaningful pattern; monitor per VIN. (6) Market-side: sluggish sales, >100k unsold units (Dec 2024), high transaction prices criticized by analysts.

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: Oil per oil-life monitor, up to 10,000 mi/12 mo; 5W-20 (5.7), 0W-20 (Hurricane — verify grade per owner's manual). Spark plugs: 100,000 mi (5.7/6.4); Hurricane interval per owner's manual (verify — not yet independently confirmed). Coolant: OAT, 10 yr/150,000 mi. 8HP75: "fill for life" normal duty; service fluid & filter ~60–80k under towing/severe duty. Air suspension (Quadra-Lift): inspect compressor/dryer at service; budget for strut replacement at 80–120k on high-mile trucks. Brake fluid every 2–3 years. All engines timing chain (no belt).

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: 0–60 (manufacturer/C&D-MT instrumented figures per Wikipedia powertrain table): Wagoneer 5.7: 6.7 sec; Hurricane SO: 5.4 sec (MT yearlong test); GW 6.4: 5.4 sec; Hurricane HO (2022–2024): 4.7 sec (C&D); HO (2025): 4.6 sec (MT comparison); 2026 base: 5.3 sec. Max towing 10,000 lb (Wagoneer properly equipped — best-in-class claim); Grand Wagoneer ~9,800 lb (verify per VIN/config).

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: Minimal enthusiast aftermarket — these are luxury tow/family rigs, not trail builds. Activity is limited to leveling kits, 22–24" wheel/tire swaps, ceramic/tint/PPF, and trailer accessories; Quadra-Lift complicates lifts. No mainstream engine tuning for the Hurricane yet (shared with Ram 1500 Hurricane, so support may follow the truck market).

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: The WS (2022+) revives the Wagoneer nameplate (absent since the 1991–1993 SJ-based Grand Wagoneer) on a Ram 1500 DT-derived body-on-frame chassis with an independent rear suspension adapted from the Ram's coil-spring design, FCA's first application of the 3.0L Hurricane twin-turbo I6 in a Jeep, and a tech-heavy cabin: up to 75 in of total screen area, Uconnect 5 (10.1" Wagoneer / 12" GW), passenger interactive display, and McIntosh audio (19-speaker 950W or 23-speaker 1,375W — first McIntosh automotive systems). The 2023 L models added a foot of wheelbase; 2025 uprated the HO Hurricane to 540 hp; for 2026 the Wagoneer name is dropped and the Grand Wagoneer absorbs the line with a 420-hp base engine. Designed by Ralph Gilles; Warren Truck Assembly, Michigan.

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: Little enthusiast energy — buyers are luxury-SUV cross-shoppers, not Jeep loyalists, and the "premium extension" branding (no Jeep badges at launch) kept it at arm's length from the core community. Resale has been soft against Escalade/Navigator. Hurricane HO trucks earn respect for effortless speed ("quickest body-on-frame SUV short of a supercharged Escalade-V"), and the IIHS Top Safety Pick is a real differentiator. Tuner culture: effectively none.

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Rivals: Cadillac Escalade, Lincoln Navigator, Chevrolet Tahoe/Suburban, GMC Yukon/Denali, Ford Expedition, Toyota Sequoia, Infiniti QX80, BMW X7, Mercedes GLS. Wagoneer/Grand Wagoneer lead on towing (10,000 lb), screen tech, and the Hurricane HO's power-per-dollar, and the 2024 IIHS award beats the Escalade/Navigator on safety testing; they trail on brand prestige, launch reliability record, dealer experience, and resale. The 2026 consolidation under Grand Wagoneer simplifies the pitch but concedes the ~$60k entry point.

kia-k5.subtitle: Midsize sedan — 2015 (TF 2nd gen, facelift) · 2016–2020 (JF 3rd gen) · 2021–2026 (DL3 4th gen, renamed K5)

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: - 2015 (TF): 2.4L Theta II G4KJ GDI I4 — 2,359 cc, NA; 2.0L Theta II G4KH turbo GDI I4 — 1,998 cc. - 2016–2020 (JF): 2.4L G4KJ GDI I4 (base); 1.6L Gamma G4FJ turbo GDI I4 — 1,591 cc (LX 1.6T / EX); 2.0L G4KH turbo GDI I4 (SX). - 2021–2024 (K5 DL3): 1.6L Gamma II turbo GDI I4 — 1,598 cc, CVVD (LX/LXS/GT-Line/EX); GT: 2.5L Smartstream G2.5 T-GDI (G4KP) turbo I4 — 2,497 cc, GDI+MPI dual injection. - 2025–2026 (K5 refresh): 2.5L Smartstream G4KR NA GDI+MPI I4 — 2,497 cc (LXS/EX/GT-Line); GT keeps the 2.5T G4KP.

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: - TF/JF: 6-speed torque-converter automatic (A6MF1 family; 2.0T uses the heavier A6LF1 family) with Sportmatic; JF 1.6T uses 7-speed dry dual-clutch (D7UF1). - K5: 1.6T/2.5 NA — 8-speed torque-converter automatic (A8LF1); GT — 8-speed wet dual-clutch (D8LF1). No manuals.

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: FWD only on all Optimas (TF/JF). K5 is FWD standard; on-demand AWD offered only on GT-Line (2021+). GT is FWD-only with sport-tuned suspension and larger 13.6-in. front brakes.

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: The JF/TF Theta II cars are the core of Kia's engine-defect history (see Known issues) — that drags the generation's reputation despite otherwise solid chassis/electronics. Consumer Reports and J.D. Power consistently scored the gasoline K5 much better than the Theta-era Optima; the 2025 K5 carries an 84/100 J.D. Power Quality & Reliability score. IIHS awarded Top Safety Pick/TSP+ to most Optima (2016+) and K5 model years (verify exact year/headlight combo at iihs.org).

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: - Theta II 2.0T/2.4 GDI connecting-rod bearing wear → engine seizure/fire: NHTSA recall 17V224000 (618,160 units, machining debris; covers 2011–2014 Optima/Sorento, 2011–2013 Sportage); Knock Sensor Detection System (KSDS) product-improvement campaign; settled US class actions (Engine I & II) covering 2011–2019 Optima with lifetime engine warranty if the KSDS update is installed. Nov 2020 NHTSA consent orders fined Hyundai/Kia a combined $210M over recall handling. - Engine-compartment fire recalls: 21V137 (HECU short, 2017–2021 Sportage/Cadenza) and the Sept 2023 HECU/ABS "park outside" recall covering 2011–2015 Optima and 2010–2013-era Kias; 20V750-series fire recalls also covered earlier Forte/Sorento/Optima Hybrid. - JF 1.6T 7-speed dry DCT (D7UF1): low-speed judder/hesitation complaints — clutch service history matters. - K5 GT 8DCT (D8LF1): overheating/harsh-shift complaints under hard use; fluid service critical. - Smartstream 2.5 NA: oil-consumption complaints and class-action filings (piston ring/oil drain-back design); monitor oil level between services.

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: Oil/filter 7,500 mi normal / 3,750 mi severe (GDI engines — Kia US schedule); use oil meeting API SP / ILSAC GF-6 per manual. All engines here are timing-chain — no belt service. Iridium spark plugs ~100,000–105,000 mi (verify per MY; turbo GDI severe schedule is shorter). ATF: "fill-for-life" on the sticker but Kia severe-service schedule calls for replacement ~60,000 mi; dry 7DCT and wet 8DCT need fluid/clutch inspections per severe schedule. Long-life coolant first change ~120,000 mi (some schedules 90,000 — verify per MY). Brake fluid: inspect annually, dealer-typical replacement every 3 yrs — OEM interval Not verified — needs OEM source.

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: 0–60: Optima 2.4 ~7.5 s; Optima SX 2.0T 6.7–6.8 s (instrumented tests); K5 1.6T 7.8 s (MotorTrend, EX); K5 GT 5.2–5.3 s, 13.7–13.8 s quarter-mile (Car and Driver, 2021). K5 GT is the factory performance trim: 290 hp, e-LSD-equipped, sport-tuned suspension, 13.6-in. brakes, 19-in. wheels.

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: The K5 GT 2.5T has real tuner support — piggyback modules (RaceChip, STILLEN TruControl), intakes, downpipes, and intercoolers; JB4-type tunes push well past 330 hp. Optima 2.0T (G4KH) shares tuning DNA with the Veloster N/Sonata turbo community — flash tunes and bolt-ons are common. Coilovers and sway bars from the Hyundai/K-sport aftermarket (BC Racing, Eibach, H&R) fit both generations.

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: TF and JF ride on Hyundai-Kia's YF/JF midsize architecture (MacPherson strut front, multi-link rear) shared with the Sonata. The DL3 K5 moved to the N3 platform: lower, wider, longer wheelbase (+1.8 in. vs JF), more high-strength steel, and available AWD for the first time in this nameplate. Key powertrain tech: CVVD (continuously variable valve duration) on the 1.6T and dual GDI+MPI injection on the 2.5T; the 8DCT in the GT was developed in-house to handle the 2.5T's 311 lb-ft.

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: The Optima SX/SXL 2.0T was a respected "sleeper" in its day; the K5 GT has a genuine following as the value alternative to the Sonata N Line and even the BMW 330i (Kia's own AMCI testing benchmarked it against the 330i). Forum communities (K5GT.org, Kia-Forums) are active on tune/wheel fitment. Non-GT cars are treated as appliance transport — clean, reliable transportation but not hobby cars.

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Direct rivals: Honda Accord, Toyota Camry, Hyundai Sonata, Nissan Altima, Mazda6 (through 2021). The Optima/K5 matches the Sonata mechanically; vs. Accord/Camry it typically offers more power-per-dollar (GT) and longer warranty (10-yr/100k powertrain), but Theta-era engine history means any used Optima needs recall/KSDS verification before purchase. Driving feel is tuned softer than Mazda6/Accord but the GT is legitimately quick.

kia-forte.subtitle: Compact sedan (Forte5 hatchback through 2017) — 2015–2018 (YD 3rd gen) · 2019–2024 (BD 4th gen; replaced by K4 for 2025)

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: - 2015–2016 (YD): 1.8L Nu G4NB MPI I4 — 1,797 cc (LX); 2.0L Nu G4NA GDI I4 — 1,999 cc (EX). Forte5 SX: 1.6L Gamma G4FJ turbo GDI. - 2017–2018 (YD facelift): 2.0L Nu MPI I4 — 147 hp (LX/S); 2.0L GDI I4 — 164 hp (EX). - 2019–2024 (BD): 2.0L Nu MPI I4 — 1,999 cc, 12.5:1 CR (all non-GT trims); GT: 1.6L G4FJ turbo GDI I4 — 1,591 cc.

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: - YD: 6-speed manual or 6-speed automatic (A6MF1); Forte5 SX turbo: 6MT or 6AT. - BD: IVT — Kia's chain-driven CVT (C0GF1) standard on 2.0 MPI trims (6MT offered on early FE); GT: 6-speed manual or 7-speed dry dual-clutch (D7UF1).

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: FWD only, all years/trims. GT adds multi-link rear suspension (vs. torsion beam on other BD trims), larger 12.0-in. front brakes, 18-in. wheels.

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: The strongest reliability story in this data pack — the Nu 2.0 MPI is a simple port-injected engine with no GDI carbon issues and no Theta bearing history. 2010–2013 Fortes were swept into the 20V750-series fire-risk recall (2.0 Nu/2.4 Theta MPI), and the 2010–2013 era appears in the Sept 2023 HECU "park outside" recall, but 2014+ BD/YD cars are largely recall-light. Consumer Reports has rated the BD Forte at or above segment average for reliability (verify per model year at consumerreports.org).

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: - Steering coupler (MDPS rubber coupling) wear → clicking/knocking felt through the wheel — widespread on YD cars; cheap part, labor-intensive fix. Well documented on Kia forums and in TSB coverage. - IVT (C0GF1) on 2019+: early-build shift-shock/jerk complaints; Kia issued TSBs and software updates. Verify fluid condition on high-mile units. - GT 7-speed dry DCT (D7UF1): judder/hesitation in stop-and-go; clutch packs are wear items. - Nu engines: occasional EVAP purge valve and catalytic converter (P0420) complaints; nothing systemic like the Theta.

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: Oil/filter 7,500 mi normal / 3,750 mi severe. All engines timing-chain. Iridium plugs ~100,000 mi (1.6T GDI: shorter per severe schedule — verify per MY). IVT fluid: Kia lists no normal-service interval; severe schedule ~60,000 mi replacement — strongly recommended. 6AT/7DCT: inspect per schedule, severe-service fluid change ~60,000 mi. Coolant ~120,000 mi first change (verify per MY). Brake fluid: inspect annually; OEM fixed interval Not verified — needs OEM source.

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: 0–60: 2.0 MPI IVT ~8.1–8.7 s (Edmunds tested 8.7); Forte GT 6.4–6.7 s (2020 DCT 6.7, 2022 6.4 per compiled instrumented tests). GT is the only performance trim: 201 hp, sport suspension, independent rear, summer-tire option. Forte5 SX (1.6T, 2015–2017) was the earlier hot variant: 6.5–6.7 s.

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: The GT shares its 1.6T (G4FJ) with the Veloster Turbo/Elantra N Line, so that whole catalog applies: intake, cat-back exhaust, intercooler, and JB4/custom flash tunes taking the engine toward 230+ hp. Popular chassis mods: rear sway bar (transforms torsion-beam cars), lowering springs/coilovers, 18x8 wheels. The Forte GT is one of the last compacts available with a manual — that alone gives it cult status.

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: YD rides on the Hyundai-Kia HD compact platform (Elantra MD); BD moved to the K3 platform with a stiffer body, more ADAS content, and the switch from a conventional 6AT to the chain-type IVT for economy trims. The GT's conversion to a multi-link rear axle is the key chassis differentiator within the BD range. All engines are aluminum DOHC 16V with dual CVVT.

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: The Forte GT is a well-kept secret in the budget-sport-compact scene — same powertrain family as the Veloster Turbo with a usable back seat and an available 6MT. Aftermarket support is borrowed wholesale from Hyundai's turbo 1.6 ecosystem. Non-GT Fortes have near-zero enthusiast presence; they're valued as cheap, durable commuters.

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Rivals: Honda Civic, Toyota Corolla, Hyundai Elantra, Mazda3, VW Jetta. The Forte generally undercuts Civic/Corolla on price and beats them on warranty; the Nu 2.0 MPI is arguably the most durable engine in the segment, though down on power and refinement vs. the 1.5T Civic. GT vs. Civic Si/Jetta GLI: slower and less polished, but thousands cheaper.

kia-sorento.subtitle: Midsize 3-row SUV (2-row available 2016–2018) — 2015 (XM 2nd gen) · 2016–2020 (UM 3rd gen) · 2021–2026 (MQ4 4th gen)

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: - 2015 (XM): 2.4L Theta II G4KJ GDI I4 — 2,359 cc (LX); 3.3L Lambda II G6DH GDI V6 — 3,342 cc. - 2016–2018 (UM): 2.4L G4KJ GDI (L/LX); 2.0L G4KH turbo GDI (EX/SX-Limited); 3.3L G6DH V6. - 2019–2020 (UM facelift): 2.4L G4KJ (L/LX); 3.3L G6DH V6 (all other trims; 2.0T dropped). - 2021–2026 (MQ4): 2.5L Smartstream G4KR GDI+MPI I4 — 2,497 cc, 13.0:1 (LX/S); 2.5L Smartstream G4KP T-GDI I4 — 2,497 cc, GDI+MPI (EX/SX/SX Prestige); HEV: 1.6T GDI hybrid (227 hp combined); PHEV: 1.6T plug-in (261 hp combined).

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: - XM/UM 2016–2018: 6-speed automatic (A6MF1 for I4, A6LF1 for 2.0T/V6). - UM 2019–2020: 2.4 keeps 6AT; V6 gets 8-speed automatic (A8LF1). - MQ4: 2.5 NA — 8-speed automatic (A8LF1); 2.5T — 8-speed wet dual-clutch (D8LF1); HEV/PHEV — 6-speed automatic with integrated e-motor.

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: FWD or torque-on-demand AWD with locking center differential (all gasoline trims, XM/UM). MQ4: 2.5 NA FWD or AWD; 2.5T FWD or AWD (X-Line AWD-only, 8.2-in. clearance); HEV FWD/AWD; PHEV AWD-only. X-Pro (2024+) is the off-road trim.

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: Mixed by generation. Theta II 2.4 cars (2012–2019) are inside the class-action/KSDS umbrella (lifetime engine warranty applies if updated); the 2017 Sorento alone carried 5 NHTSA recalls. The 3.3 V6 (G6DH) is the durable pick of the UM generation. MQ4 is improved — 82/100 J.D. Power Q&R on the 2023 Sorento Hybrid. Sept 2023 HECU "park outside" recall covers 2011–2014 Sorento.

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: - Theta II 2.4/2.0T rod-bearing failure and fire risk — recalls 17V224000, KSDS campaign, Engine I/II class actions (2012–2019 Sorento covered). - 20V750 (Dec 2020): fire risk on 2012–2013 Sorento 2.4 MPI — fuel/oil leak or engine damage. - SC172 (Dec 2018): high-pressure fuel pipe improperly torqued during Theta engine replacements → fuel leak; affects 2012–2017 Sorento previously re-engined. - MQ4 8DCT (D8LF1): low-speed creep/shift harshness complaints; clutch fluid service essential. - Smartstream 2.5 NA oil consumption (class-action filings, piston-ring design) — check level regularly on 2021+ 2.5 cars.

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: Oil/filter 7,500 mi normal / 3,750 severe (Theta/Lambda era); 2021+ Smartstream — verify per MY manual (Kia moved some models to longer normal intervals). All engines timing-chain (G4KJ, G4KH, G6DH, G4KR, G4KP — no belts). Iridium plugs ~100,000 mi NA / earlier on GDI turbo per severe schedule (verify). ATF: no normal interval; severe ~60,000 mi. 8DCT fluid per severe schedule. AWD transfer case/rear diff: inspect, severe-service fluid changes. Coolant ~120,000 mi first change (verify per MY). Brake fluid: inspect; OEM fixed interval Not verified — needs OEM source.

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: 0–60: XM/UM V6 AWD 7.1–7.4 s; UM 2.4 ~9.1 s; MQ4 2.5T AWD 6.0–6.3 s (fastest Sorento ever); MQ4 2.5 NA ~9.5 s (Kia internal 0–100 km/h 9.5 s FWD); HEV ~7.2–8.6 s. No factory performance trim; X-Line/X-Pro are appearance/off-road packages. Kia-published top speed 131 mph (2.5T).

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: Minimal hardcore tuning — owners focus on the X-Line/X-Pro formula: all-terrain tires (235/65R17 swaps), 1–1.5-in. lift/leveling spacers, roof racks, skid plates, and light bars. The 2.5T (G4KP) accepts the same piggyback tunes as the K5 GT (RaceChip/STILLEN) for meaningful gains. Towing is the big mod driver: 3,500 lbs with tow package (MQ4), up to 5,000 lbs on V6 AWD (UM).

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: XM was conventional unibody; UM brought Kia's "Iso-Structure" high-strength-steel body (53% AHSS), longer wheelbase (+3.1 in.), and the 2.0T option. MQ4 rides on the N3 platform shared with the K5/Santa Fe: +1.4-in. wheelbase, first Sorento hybrid/PHEV, dual-injection Smartstream engines, and an 8DCT for the turbo. The HEV/PHEV use a 1.6T with a 6AT and integrated motor rather than a CVT-style e-transaxle.

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: Not an enthusiast vehicle in the tuner sense, but the UM V6 and MQ4 X-Pro have a solid overlanding/family-adventure following — lift kits and AT tires are the community norm. The 2.5T's 311 lb-ft surprises people; forums note it will outrun many "sporty" crossovers. Respected as a tow-rig-for-people-who-hate-minivans.

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Rivals: Hyundai Santa Fe, Toyota Highlander, Honda Pilot, Ford Explorer, Nissan Pathfinder (MQ4 straddles compact/midsize: also cross-shopped vs. CR-V/RAV4). Sorento's edge: available 2.5T punch, warranty, and price under Highlander/Pilot; weaker residual values and the Theta-era baggage hurt used-car trust on 2016–2019 four-cylinders. V6 UM cars are the sweet spot for a shop customer wanting durability.

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: - 2015–2016 (SL): 2.4L Theta II G4KJ GDI I4 — 2,359 cc (LX/EX); SX: 2.0L G4KH turbo GDI I4 — 1,998 cc. - 2017–2022 (QL): 2.4L G4KJ GDI (LX/EX); SX Turbo: 2.0L G4KH turbo GDI. - 2023–2026 (NQ5): 2.5L Smartstream G4KR GDI+MPI I4 — 2,497 cc, 13.0:1 (all gas trims); HEV: 1.6T hybrid (227 hp combined); PHEV: 1.6T plug-in (261 hp combined).

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: - SL/QL: 6-speed automatic (A6MF1 for 2.4; A6LF1-family for 2.0T), FWD or AWD. - NQ5: 8-speed automatic (A8LF1); HEV/PHEV: 6-speed automatic with integrated e-motor.

horsepower.label: Horsepower
horsepower.text: - SL: 2.4 — 182 hp @ 6,000 (LX FWD; 180 hp EX AWD); 2.0T — 260 hp @ 6,000 (2015 SX listed 260; 2016 SX 260 ULEV). - QL: 2.4 — 181 hp @ 6,000; 2.0T — 240 hp @ 6,000 FWD / 237 hp AWD. - NQ5: 2.5 NA — 187 hp @ 6,100; HEV — 227 hp combined; PHEV — 261 hp combined.

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: FWD standard, on-demand AWD optional on virtually all trims (all gens). NQ5 X-Line/X-Pro/X-Pro Prestige are AWD-only with 8.3-in. ground clearance and all-terrain tires (X-Pro).

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: QL/SL four-cylinders carry the Theta II story (2011–2019 Sportage in the class actions; lifetime engine warranty with KSDS installed). NHTSA data shows 819 owner complaints filed across Sportage generations, with engine dominating (556). The 2017–2021 Sportage was subject to recall 21V137 — a park-outside HECU fire-risk recall covering 379,931 Sportage/Cadenza vehicles. NQ5 is materially better: 84/100 J.D. Power Q&R for the Sportage Hybrid. NQ5 earned IIHS Top Safety Pick recognition in multiple years (verify per MY at iihs.org).

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: - Theta II 2.4/2.0T rod-bearing failure/seizure — recalls 17V224000 (2011–2013 Sportage), KSDS campaign, class actions through 2019; same symptoms: knock, oil light, stall. - 21V137 (March 2021): HECU electrical short → engine-compartment fire, 2017–2021 Sportage without Smart Cruise Control; "park outside" consumer alert issued by NHTSA. - SC172 (2018): fuel-pipe leak check after Theta engine replacements (2011–2018 Sportage). - QL AWD couplings and 6AT torque-converter shudder complaints at high mileage (forum-documented; service fluid early). - NQ5 2.5 NA oil consumption complaints/class-action filings (Smartstream piston-ring issue) — monitor level.

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: Oil/filter 7,500 mi normal / 3,750 severe (Theta era); 2023+ Smartstream — verify per MY manual. All timing-chain — no belt service. Iridium plugs ~100,000 mi (2.0T shorter per severe schedule — verify). ATF: no normal interval, severe ~60,000 mi. AWD rear coupling/diff fluid: inspect, severe-service replacement. HEV/PHEV: same engine schedule plus hybrid-system coolant checks. Coolant ~120,000 mi first change (verify per MY). Brake fluid: inspect; OEM fixed interval Not verified — needs OEM source.

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: 0–60: SL/QL 2.4 ~8.8–9.3 s; QL SX Turbo 6.7 s FWD / 7.0 s AWD; NQ5 2.5 AWD ~8.5 s (independent tests — exact instrumented figure Not verified — needs OEM source); NQ5 HEV ~7.5–8 s (approximate). No factory performance trim; X-Pro is the off-road flagship with 17-in. AT tires and multi-terrain modes. Towing: 2,000 lbs (SL/QL), 2,500 lbs (NQ5 gas).

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: Light-tuner presence: SX Turbo (G4KH) accepts the usual Theta turbo bolt-ons and piggyback tunes. NQ5 X-Pro owners do mild overlanding builds — AT tires, roof baskets, 1-in. spacers. Wheels/tires and cosmetic mods dominate; serious performance builds are rare because the aftermarket points people to the K5 GT/Stinger instead.

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: SL shared its platform with the Hyundai Tucson (ix35); QL added a stiffer structure and the 240-hp SX Turbo. NQ5 is built on the N3 platform (with K5/Sorento/Tucson) and the US gets the long-wheelbase body — +7.1 in. overall and +3.4 in. wheelbase vs. QL, yielding class-leading rear legroom and cargo space (39.6 cu ft behind row 2). Smartstream 2.5 uses dual GDI+MPI injection and a high 13.0:1 compression ratio; hybrids pair the 1.6T with a conventional 6AT.

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: The Sportage has never been a tuner darling — its following is pragmatic. The SX Turbo has a small cult for being a genuinely quick compact SUV (quicker than a base Stinger 2.0T to 60 in some tests), and the NQ5 X-Pro is popular in the soft-roading community. Forum sentiment: buy the NQ5 or a V6-free post-2020 QL and check recall status first.

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Rivals: Honda CR-V, Toyota RAV4, Hyundai Tucson, Mazda CX-5/CX-50, Ford Escape, Chevy Equinox. NQ5 wins on space, tech-per-dollar, warranty, and hybrid MPG (43 combined FWD); it trails RAV4/CR-V on resale and on the 2.5's refinement. For a used QL, the SX Turbo is the driver's pick but carries Theta risk — documented KSDS/recall completion is mandatory diligence.

kia-telluride.subtitle: Midsize 3-row SUV — 2020–2026 (single generation; facelift 2023 with X-Line/X-Pro trims added). Not sold before 2020.

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: 3.8L Lambda II (G6DN) GDI V6 — 3,778 cc, 13.0:1 CR, aluminum block/head, DOHC dual CVVT. Sole engine across all years/trims.

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: 8-speed torque-converter automatic (A8LF1 family), Sportmatic; no manual, no DCT.

horsepower.label: Horsepower
horsepower.text: 291 hp @ 6,000 rpm (all years/trims).

torque.label: Torque
torque.text: 262 lb-ft @ 5,200 rpm (all years/trims).

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: FWD standard; active on-demand AWD optional on all trims. 2023+ X-Line/X-Pro are AWD-only (X-Pro adds 18-in. all-terrain tires and raised ride height). Tow rating 5,000 lbs; 2023+ X-Pro rated 5,500 lbs (widely reported in Kia press materials — confirm per VIN).

dimensions.label: Dimensions
dimensions.text: 196.9 in. L × 78.3 in. W × 68.9–69.3 in. H; 114.2 in. WB; 8.0 in. ground clearance; curb 4,112–4,482 lbs (FWD–AWD). 7- or 8-passenger.

mpg.label: Fuel economy (MPG)
mpg.text: 2020–2022: FWD 20/26/23, AWD 19/24/21. 2023+ (facelift): FWD 20/26/22, AWD 18/24/20 (per fueleconomy.gov listings — confirm per MY).

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: Strongest reliability reputation in the Kia SUV line — 2020 World Car of the Year, NHTSA 5-star overall ratings, IIHS Top Safety Pick/TSP+ in multiple years (verify per MY at iihs.org). The G6DN V6 is conventional GDI with no Theta-style defect history. Reputation is dented by a series of non-powertrain recalls (below) and heavy windshield-crack complaint volume rather than mechanical failures.

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: - Tow-hitch harness recalls (2022): debris/moisture on the harness circuit board → inoperative trailer brake lights (~85,000 vehicles) and fire risk (~36,500 vehicles); "park outside" guidance on the fire campaign. - 24V407 (2024): front power-seat motor overheating from stuck seat-slide knob → fire risk; 462,000+ vehicles (2020–2024). - Windshield cracking with no impact point — very high NHTSA complaint volume (hundreds of complaints for 2020–2021 MY alone); class-action litigation filed. Also sunroof glass breakage complaints. - Various early recalls: incorrect spare tire, blank instrument cluster/LCD, occupant-detection malfunction, incorrect seat belt assemblies (2020–2022 MY). - Owner-reported: high-beam performance complaints, cold-temperature engine squeal (belt/pulley), driveline vibration at highway speed (forum-documented).

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: Oil/filter 7,500 mi normal / 3,750 severe; G6DN is timing-chain — no belt service. Iridium plugs ~100,000 mi (verify per MY). ATF: no normal interval; severe-service replacement ~60,000 mi. AWD transfer case/rear diff: inspect, severe-service fluid changes. Coolant ~120,000 mi first change (verify per MY). Brake fluid: inspect; OEM fixed interval Not verified — needs OEM source. GDI-only fuel system — intake-valve carbon cleaning is a legitimate 60k–90k mi service.

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: 0–60: 7.1–7.4 s (instrumented tests, SX AWD). No factory performance trim — X-Line/X-Pro (2023+) are off-road appearance/capability packages. Top speed electronically limited (Not verified — needs OEM source).

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: Overlanding-style builds dominate: all-terrain tires on 18s, 1–1.5-in. lift/leveling kits, skid plates, roof racks, light bars. The X-Pro factory package absorbed much of this demand. No meaningful engine tuning scene for the NA 3.8.

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: Purpose-built unibody on the N3-related large-SUV architecture shared with the Hyundai Palisade; built in West Point, Georgia. Iso-Structure high-strength-steel body, MacPherson strut front / multi-link rear, self-leveling rear suspension available on upper trims. The 2023 facelift added revised styling, dual 12.3-in. screens, and the X-Line/X-Pro sub-trims without powertrain changes.

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: Enthusiast interest is of the family-adventure/overland variety, not performance — the Telluride is the community default answer for "3-row that isn't a minivan." Strong owner loyalty and high resale; the X-Pro has a genuine following. No tuner support to speak of.

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Rivals: Hyundai Palisade (mechanical twin), Toyota Highlander/Grand Highlander, Honda Pilot, Ford Explorer, Mazda CX-90, Chevy Traverse. Telluride wins on value, warranty, and cabin execution; the V6 is durable but down on refinement and economy vs. turbo/hybrid rivals (no hybrid offered). Driving feel is comfort-tuned — softer than CX-90, quieter than Explorer.

kia-soul.subtitle: Subcompact boxy crossover/hatch — 2015–2019 (PS 2nd gen) · 2020–2025 (SK3 3rd gen). Discontinued after 2025 — production ended October 2025, no 2026 model year (confirmed by Edmunds and Automotive News).

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: - 2015–2019 (PS): 1.6L Gamma G4FG GDI I4 — 1,591 cc (Base); 2.0L Nu G4NA GDI I4 — 1,999 cc (+/!); 2017–2019 Soul ! (Turbo): 1.6L G4FJ turbo GDI I4 — 1,591 cc. Soul EV (2015–2019, battery-electric) sold in CARB states. - 2020–2025 (SK3): 2.0L Nu MPI I4 — 1,999 cc, 12.5:1 CR (all trims from 2023); GT-Line 1.6T (2020–2022 only): G4FJ turbo GDI.

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: - PS: 6-speed manual (Base) or 6-speed automatic (A6GF1 family); 1.6T: 7-speed dry dual-clutch (D7UF1). - SK3: IVT chain-driven CVT (C0GF1) standard; 6MT on early LX (through ~2022); 1.6T: 7DCT (D7UF1).

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: FWD only, all generations/trims. No AWD ever offered on US Soul.

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: Good overall for a budget car — the Nu engines have no Theta-style defect umbrella. Main blot: recall 19V120000 (2019, campaign SC176) covering all 2012–2016 Souls with the 1.6L GDI — catalytic converter overheating → engine damage/fire risk, with an ECU update remedy. 2014–2015 Soul was included in the 20V750-series fire-risk recall; 2011–2013 Soul in the Sept 2023 HECU "park outside" recall. 3rd gen (2020+) with the 2.0 MPI/IVT is recall-light and owner-complaint volume is modest.

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: - 19V120000: 1.6 GDI catalyst overheat/engine damage (2012–2016) — verify ECU update completed. - Nu 2.0: catalytic-converter failure (P0420) is a common high-mileage repair; cats are also a top theft target on this model — worth advising customers. - Steering coupler (MDPS rubber coupling) clicking — same wear pattern as other Kia/Hyundai compacts of the era (PS gen). - IVT (C0GF1) on 2020+: early shift-quality TSBs/software updates; fluid service on severe schedule recommended. - 1.6T dry 7DCT: judder/hesitation in traffic; clutch packs are wear items. - Soul EV: high-voltage battery recalls affected early Kia EVs in some markets — verify US VIN at nhtsa.gov/recalls.

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: Oil/filter 7,500 mi normal / 3,750 severe. All engines timing-chain. Iridium plugs ~100,000 mi (1.6T shorter per severe schedule — verify per MY). IVT/6AT/7DCT fluids: no normal interval listed; severe ~60,000 mi — IVT fluid service strongly advised. Coolant ~120,000 mi first change (verify per MY). Brake fluid: inspect; OEM fixed interval Not verified — needs OEM source.

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: 0–60: 1.6/2.0 PS ~8.4–10 s; Soul ! Turbo (2017–2019) 6.4–6.5 s (2017 instrumented test: 6.4 s); SK3 2.0 IVT ~8.3 s; SK3 GT-Line 1.6T ~6.4 s (2020 test). Factory sporty variants: Soul ! / GT-Line Turbo (2017–2022) with 201 hp, 18-in. wheels, larger brakes.

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: The 1.6T Soul shares the Veloster Turbo's G4FJ, so intakes, exhausts, and piggyback/flash tunes carry over — a quirky budget-sleeper. Cosmetic culture is strong (wraps, wheels, roof racks). Rear sway-bar upgrade is the common chassis mod on torsion-beam cars.

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: PS rode on the Hyundai-Kia PB small-car platform; SK3 moved to the K3 architecture shared with the Forte — stiffer body, IVT for efficiency, and a brief 1.6T/7DCT performance option. Boxy packaging yields 62.1 cu ft max cargo in a 165-in. footprint. All trims MacPherson strut front / coupled torsion-beam rear.

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: The Soul has genuine cult status as a design icon ("the toaster," "hamster car") — one of the most recognizable shapes on the road, hence owner affection despite humble hardware. The Turbo has a small sleeper following. Discontinuation after 2025 (17-year US run) is already boosting used interest in clean 1.6T cars.

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Rivals: Hyundai Kona, Nissan Kicks, Chevy Trax, Toyota Corolla Cross, Honda HR-V. The Soul's edge was space efficiency, price, and character; it loses to Kicks/Trax on MPG and to Kona on available AWD (never offered on Soul). 2.0 MPI durability is a used-buy plus vs. small turbo rivals.

kia-ev6.subtitle: Battery-electric compact/midsize crossover — 2022–2026 (E-GMP dedicated EV platform, 800V class; facelift 2025 with US production in Georgia, NACS port, larger battery)

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: Permanent-magnet AC synchronous motors, single (RWD) or dual (AWD): - Light (58.0 kWh standard range, 2022 and 2024+): single rear motor. - Light Long Range/Wind/GT-Line (77.4 kWh, 2022–2024): single rear motor (RWD) or dual motor (AWD). - GT (2023–2024): dual high-output motors, 77.4 kWh. - 2025+ refresh: 63.0 kWh (Light) / 84.0 kWh (Light LR, Wind, GT-Line, GT); GT output increased. - Battery: 697V lithium-ion (NCM pouch cells, SK On), liquid-cooled; 10-yr/100k-mi battery warranty.

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: Single-speed gear reduction unit, 10.65:1 constant ratio. No multi-gear transmission.

horsepower.label: Horsepower
horsepower.text: Light SR RWD — 167 hp; Wind/GT-Line RWD — 225 hp (168 kW); Wind/GT-Line AWD — 320 hp (239 kW); GT AWD (2023–2024) — 576 hp (430 kW); 2025+ GT — 601 hp (with reported ~641 hp GT-mode overboost — confirm per MY Kia press materials).

torque.label: Torque
torque.text: RWD — 258 lb-ft; AWD — 446 lb-ft; GT — 545 lb-ft (2023–2024 OEM spec).

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: RWD (single rear motor) or AWD (dual motor). GT is AWD-only with e-LSD, electronically controlled suspension (ECS), 15.0-in. front brakes, 21-in. wheels.

dimensions.label: Dimensions
dimensions.text: 184.3–184.8 in. L × 74.0–74.4 in. W × 60.8 in. H; 114.2 in. WB (same as Telluride); 6.1 in. ground clearance; curb 4,255–4,502 lbs (Wind/GT-Line), GT 4,795 lbs. Frunk + 24.4 cu ft rear cargo.

mpg.label: Fuel economy (MPG)
mpg.text: EPA MPGe (2023 OEM figures): RWD 134 city / 101 hwy / 117 combined; AWD Wind 120/98/109, GT-Line AWD 106/86/96; GT 85/74/79. EPA range: RWD 310 mi; AWD 282 (Wind) / 252 (GT-Line); GT 206 mi. 2025+ refresh (84 kWh): RWD 319 mi, AWD 295 mi (Wind) / 270 (GT-Line), GT 231 mi; Light 240 mi. Charging: 800V architecture, 10–80% in 18 min on a 350-kW DC fast charger (240 kW peak), 10.9 kW OBC (~7h10m on 240V/48A); 2025+ gains native NACS port (non-GT).

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: Mixed — the drivetrain itself (motors, battery) has few failure reports, but the ICCU/12V electrical issue (below) is the defining reliability story and drove below-average predicted-reliability scores at Consumer Reports. Battery degradation reports are modest; pack warranty is 10 yr/100k mi with 70% capacity floor. Multiple ICCU-related recall campaigns; check VIN.

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: - ICCU (Integrated Charging Control Unit) failure: transient high voltage/thermal stress damages internal transistors and blows the ICCU fuse → 12V battery stops charging → warnings, then loss of motive power while driving. Kia recall 24V200 (2024; 2022–2024 EV6) with ICCU software update + inspection/replacement; extended later in 2024 (24V867) after the firmware-only fix proved insufficient. Sibling Hyundai campaigns 24V204/24V868; 200,000+ vehicles across the brands. 12V battery drain/dead-12V complaints are widespread even without full ICCU failure (forum-documented). - Slow AC charging failures/OBC issues and charge-port door faults reported at lower volume. - Recalls for rearview camera/display software on some MYs — verify per VIN.

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: No engine oil, no spark plugs, no timing drive, no multi-gear transmission service. Scheduled items: reduction-gear oil (inspect; replace per severe schedule — verify per MY manual), battery/electronics coolant (inspect; long-life replacement interval per manual — Not verified — needs OEM source), brake fluid (inspect/replace — absorbs moisture despite regen-dominated braking; OEM interval Not verified — needs OEM source), cabin air filter ~every 15k–20k mi, tire rotations ~7,500 mi (EV torque wears tires fast — budget for tires), brake pads last long but calipers/slides need cleaning where road salt is used (regen under-use corrodes rotors). 12V battery: treat as a wear item; AGM replacement and ICCU recall status check at every visit.

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: 0–60 (Kia OEM published figures): RWD 7.2 s; AWD 5.1 s; GT 3.4 s (Car and Driver independently recorded 3.2 s for the 2023 GT). Top speed: 115 mph RWD / 117 AWD / 161 mph GT (OEM). GT adds GT drive mode, drift mode, e-LSD, ECS, and (2025+) a simulated-gear-shift feature.

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: Minimal mechanical tuning (no aftermarket motor/inverter tunes of note). Scene is wheels/tires, lowering springs, aero cosmetics, tint, and track-day brake/tire prep for the GT. V2L (vehicle-to-load) outlet is a popular utility "mod-free" feature for camping/tools. 2025+ NACS port removes the adapter for Tesla Superchargers.

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: First Kia on the E-GMP dedicated EV platform (shared with Hyundai Ioniq 5/6, Genesis GV60): 800V/400V multi-charging capability via the motor/inverter as a boost converter, flat-floor skateboard layout with a 114.2-in. wheelbase, 5-link rear suspension, Mobis iEB integrated electric brake booster with strong regen blending, and standard heat-pump availability on upper trims. 2025 refresh moved US assembly to West Point, Georgia, enlarged the battery to 84.0 kWh, added NACS, ccNC infotainment with OTA, and revised styling.

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: The EV6 GT has real respect — it out-accelerates the Mustang Mach-E GT and challenges the Ioniq 5 N (its sibling) at a lower profile; track-day videos and drag content built its rep quickly. Owner forums are among the most active in the Kia world, dominated by charging strategy, ICCU recall tracking, and 12V-battery management talk.

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Rivals: Hyundai Ioniq 5 (platform twin), Tesla Model Y, Ford Mustang Mach-E, VW ID.4, Toyota bZ4X/Subaru Solterra. EV6's 800V charging (18-min 10–80%) beats everything in class except its E-GMP siblings; Model Y wins on charging network/software, Mach-E on handling feel. Reliability caveat: any used EV6 needs documented ICCU recall completion — that is the single most important service-history check on this model.

lexus-rx.subtitle: Midsize luxury SUV/crossover, 5-seat (2-row) and RX L (3-row, 2018–2022); generations: 2016–2022 (4th gen, AL20), 2023–2026 (5th gen, ALA10/ALH10)

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: 2016–2022: RX 350 = 3.5L 2GR-FKS naturally aspirated V6 (D-4S port+direct injection, VVT-iW); RX 450h = 3.5L 2GR-FXS Atkinson-cycle V6 + front/rear motor-generators (THS II). 2023–2026: RX 350 = 2.4L T24A-FTS turbocharged I4; RX 350h = 2.5L A25A-FXS Atkinson I4 hybrid (THS II); RX 450h+ = 2.5L A25A-FXS plug-in hybrid (18.1 kWh Li-ion); RX 500h = 2.4L T24A-FTS turbo hybrid (single-motor "Performance Hybrid" system).

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: 2016–2022: 8-speed automatic (UA80E/UA80F "Direct Shift-8") on RX 350; e-CVT planetary hybrid transaxle on RX 450h. 2023–2026: 8-speed automatic on RX 350; e-CVT on RX 350h and RX 450h+; 6-speed automatic on RX 500h.

horsepower.label: Horsepower
horsepower.text: 2016–2022: RX 350 = 295 hp @ 6,300 rpm; RX 450h = 308 hp system total. 2023–2026: RX 350 = 275 hp; RX 350h = 246 hp system; RX 450h+ = 304 hp system; RX 500h = 366 hp system (Lexus Canada rounds to 367).

torque.label: Torque
torque.text: 2016–2022: RX 350 = 267 lb-ft @ 4,700 rpm; RX 450h = 247 lb-ft system. 2023–2026: RX 350 = 317 lb-ft; RX 350h = 233 lb-ft @ 4,300 rpm (engine); RX 500h = 406 lb-ft system; RX 450h+ combined torque not published by Lexus.

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: 2016–2022: RX 350 FWD or AWD (mechanical, Dynamic Torque Control); RX 450h FWD or AWD — AWD hybrid uses a rear-axle motor (E-Four), no driveshaft; RX 350L/450hL (2018–2022) FWD/AWD. 2023–2026: RX 350 FWD or AWD; RX 350h AWD standard (E-Four); RX 450h+ AWD (E-Four); RX 500h DIRECT4 AWD only.

dimensions.label: Dimensions
dimensions.text: 2016–2022: 192.5 in L × 74.6 in W × 67.7 in H, wheelbase 109.8 in; curb ≈ 4,222–4,674 lbs.
