torque.text: L33: 2.5L 180 lb-ft @ 4,000 rpm (2015), 177 lb-ft (2016–2018); 3.5L 251 lb-ft @ 4,400 rpm. L34: 2.5L FWD 180 lb-ft @ 3,600 rpm; 2.5L AWD 178 lb-ft @ 3,600 rpm; VC-Turbo 273 lb-ft @ 4,000 rpm on premium (267 lb-ft regular).

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: L33: FWD only. L34: FWD standard; Intelligent AWD optional with 2.5L (first AWD Altima); VC-Turbo FWD only.

dimensions.label: Dimensions
dimensions.text: L33: length 191.5 in / width 72.0 in / height 57.9–58.1 in / wheelbase 109.3 in / curb 3,108–3,355 lb. L34: length 192.9 in / width 72.9 in / height 56.8–57.4 in / wheelbase 111.2 in / curb 3,212–3,459 lb (KBB/press data by trim).

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: L33 is defined by CVT failures — 2013–2016 Altima covered by a federal class-action settlement with CVT warranty extension (up to 7 yr/84,000 mi). 2013–2018 Altimas recalled four times for secondary hood-latch corrosion (campaign 20V315, ~1.8M vehicles). L34 2.5 (PR25DD) has been comparatively trouble-free; L34 VC-Turbos (2019–2020) are included in NHTSA recall 25V437000 (443,899 vehicles, engine-bearing manufacturing defects).

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: Jatco CVT judder, overheating and premature failure (L33; class action + warranty extension). QR25DE oil consumption at mileage (NHTSA complaints, Nissan TSBs on piston rings). Hood-latch corrosion (recalls 2014–2020). VC-Turbo main/A-link/C-link/L-link bearing failures (recall 25V437000, June 2025). CVT complaints persist on L34 but at much lower rates.

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: Oil/filter 5,000 mi/6 mo (severe 3,750 mi); manual schedule calls tire rotation at 5,000 mi (2.5L). Iridium spark plugs ~105,000 mi. Long-life coolant ~105,000 mi/7 yr first change. CVT fluid (NS-2/NS-3): no fixed normal-service interval — inspect; shop best practice is drain/fill every 60,000 mi (severe-service schedule). Brake fluid replacement every ~2 yr per Nissan schedule. All engines timing chain (no belt service).

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: 0–60: L33 2.5 ~7.8 sec, L33 3.5 V6 ~6.2 sec (Edmunds instrumented tests); VC-Turbo ~6.5 sec (automobile-catalog estimate); L34 2.5 0–60 — Not verified — needs OEM/publication source. No factory performance trim; SR adds sport-tuned suspension/paddles only.

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: Light scene — bolt-ons (intake, cat-back) from Stillen/Megan Racing, lowering springs/coilovers, wheel/tire packages. VC-Turbo ECU tuning is limited by CVT torque capacity; no meaningful turbo upgrade path. 3.5 SR (L33) is the "sleeper" pick.

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: L33 rides on Nissan's D platform with a high-strength-steel unibody and aluminum hood; 2016 facelift brought aero work (active grille shutters) and the retuned CVT. L34 moved to a new, stiffer platform, added available AWD, ProPILOT Assist, and the world-first production variable-compression engine (multi-link actuator varies piston stroke).

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: Regarded as an appliance sedan, not an enthusiast platform; the L33 3.5 V6 has a small sleeper following. Tuner support is thin and concentrated on cosmetic/chassis parts; the CVT discourages power builds.

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Rivals: Honda Accord, Toyota Camry, Mazda6, Hyundai Sonata, Kia K5. Altima typically undercuts on price and matches on space/MPG, but the CVT's reliability record (L33) and resale lag the Accord/Camry; driving feel is mid-pack — quieter and softer than a Mazda6, less refined powertrain than the 2018+ Accord 2.0T.

nissan-rogue.subtitle: Compact crossover; 2015–2020 (T32; facelift 2017, Hybrid 2017–2019), 2021–2026 (T33; 2.5L for 2021, 1.5L VC-Turbo from 2022; 2024 tech refresh; Rock Creek trim added 2025+)

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: T32: 2.5L QR25DE NA I4 (port injection). T32 Hybrid: 2.0L MR20DD + 30 kW electric motor (net 176 hp), 2017–2019. T33: 2.5L PR25DD NA DI I4 (2021 only); 1.5L KR15DDT VC-Turbo turbo I3 (variable compression, 2022–2026).

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: All years: Xtronic CVT (Jatco CVT8 family; EPA designation AV-S8 on T33). Hybrid uses CVT with clutch-pack (no torque converter). No manual.

horsepower.label: Horsepower
horsepower.text: T32 2.5: 170 hp @ 6,000 rpm. T32 Hybrid: 176 hp net. T33 2.5: 181 hp. T33 1.5 VC-Turbo: 201 hp.

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: FWD standard, Intelligent AWD optional on all trims, all generations. Hybrid offered FWD and AWD.

dimensions.label: Dimensions
dimensions.text: T32: length 184.5 in / width 72.4 in / height 66.3–68.5 in / wheelbase 106.5 in. T33: length 183.0 in / width 72.4 in / height 66.5–66.9 in / wheelbase 106.5 in. Curb weight: Not verified — needs OEM source (published figures cluster ~3,400–3,700 lb depending on trim/drivetrain).

mpg.label: Fuel economy (MPG)
mpg.text: T32 FWD 26/33/29, AWD 25/32/27; Hybrid FWD 33/35/34, AWD 31/34/33 (fueleconomy.gov, 2017). T33 2.5 FWD 27/35/30 (CNET/Nissan). T33 1.5T FWD 30/37/33 (SL/Platinum 29/36/32), AWD 28/35/31 (SL/Platinum 28/34/31) (fueleconomy.gov, 2023).

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: T32: CVT class action (Stringer v. Nissan, 2014–2018 Rogue) with powertrain warranty extension to 7 yr/84,000 mi. T33: ~1,304 NHTSA complaints and 19 recall campaigns across 2021–2025 (The Weekly Driver tally) — one of the highest complaint rates in segment, dominated by the 1.5L VC-Turbo: recall 25V437000 (June 2025, engine bearings, 2021–2024), Feb 2026 recall (2023–2025, oil-breakdown bearing seizure), plus a 2024–2025 throttle-body gear recall and airbag-inflator recall 24V154000 (2023 Rogue).

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: T32: CVT judder/failure (class action); AEB false-activation complaints on 2017–2018 models (NHTSA-documented). T33: VC-Turbo engine-bearing failures (two NHTSA recalls + NHTSA investigation), high engine/cooling/fuel-system complaint counts; electronic throttle-body gear failures (recall). The 2021-only PR25DD 2.5L is the lowest-risk T33 powertrain.

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: Oil/filter 5,000 mi/6 mo (severe 3,750 mi). Iridium plugs ~105,000 mi. Long-life coolant first change ~105,000 mi. CVT fluid NS-3 — inspect; 60,000-mi drain/fill is shop best practice, especially on T32. Brake fluid ~every 2 yr per Nissan schedule. All engines timing chain.

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: 0–60: T32 2.5 low-9-sec range (publication tests) — exact figure Not verified — needs OEM/publication source; T33 1.5T ~8 sec (publication tests) — Not verified precisely. Rock Creek (2025+) is an appearance/off-road-trim package, not a power upgrade.

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: Minimal performance scene — mostly wheels/tires, roof racks, mild lift/level kits on T32, hitch and overlanding accessories. No meaningful engine tuning market (CVT + small turbo).

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: T32 moved Rogue onto the Renault-Nissan CMF-CD architecture, adding a third-row option (2014–2017) and the short-lived Hybrid. T33 rides a new CMF platform with Vehicle Motion Control (chassis-integrated brake/torque control), a stronger body, and the segment-unique 1.5L variable-compression three-cylinder. 2024 refresh added Google built-in infotainment.

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: Pure family appliance in the community's eyes — valued for space, seats and safety tech, ignored by tuners. The rare Hybrid has a small fuel-sipper following. T33's VC-Turbo is viewed with skepticism after the bearing recalls.

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Rivals: Toyota RAV4, Honda CR-V, Mazda CX-5, Hyundai Tucson. Rogue matches them on space and value and beats some on MPG (1.5T), but its powertrain durability record is now the segment's weakest — RAV4/CR-V hybrids are the low-risk alternatives; CX-5 is the driver's pick.

nissan-sentra.subtitle: Compact sedan; 2015–2019 (B17; facelift 2016, SR Turbo/NISMO 2017–2019), 2020–2025 (B18), 2026 (all-new 9th gen)

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: B17: 1.8L MRA8DE NA I4; 1.6L MR16DDT turbo I4 (SR Turbo, NISMO). B18 & 2026: 2.0L MR20DD NA DI I4.

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: B17: 6-speed manual (S, SR Turbo, NISMO) or Xtronic CVT (Jatco CVT7). B18: Xtronic CVT (6MT on 2020 S only, then dropped). 2026: retuned Xtronic CVT only, with new Sport mode (SV/SR/SL).

horsepower.label: Horsepower
horsepower.text: B17 1.8: 130 hp (124 hp in some CVT ratings). B17 1.6T: 188 hp. B18/2026 2.0: 149 hp @ 6,400 rpm.

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: FWD only, all generations. No AWD Sentra sold in the US.

dimensions.label: Dimensions
dimensions.text: B17: length 182.1 in / width 69.3 in / height 58.9 in / wheelbase 106.3 in / curb 2,848–2,943 lb (1.8 CVT; turbo/NISMO trims heavier). B18: length 182.7 in / width 71.5 in / height 56.9 in / wheelbase 106.8 in / curb 3,036–3,084 lb. 2026 (9th gen): Not verified — needs OEM source (dimensions not yet published in spec-sheet form).

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: B17: 2013–2017 Sentra CVT covered by class-action litigation (Martinez v. Nissan, final approval 2023) with CVT warranty extensions. B18: materially better — Sentra ranked top Compact Car in the J.D. Power 2025 U.S. Initial Quality Study (per Nissan). NHTSA complaint volume on B18 is low; airbag-inflator recall 24V154000 covers 2023–2024 Sentra.

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: B17: CVT judder/slipping/failure (class action); premature CVT wear is the dominant shop ticket on 2013–2017 cars. B18: high-mileage timing-chain stretch and CVT longevity are flagged by independent engine analysts (owner-mechanic reports, not TSB-level) — treat as watch items, not confirmed defects. 2026: too new for meaningful data.

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: Oil/filter 5,000 mi/6 mo (severe 3,750 mi). Iridium plugs ~105,000 mi (MR20DD; MR16DDT similar long-life plug). Coolant long-life ~105,000 mi first change. CVT fluid NS-2/NS-3 — 60,000-mi drain/fill recommended on B17 given CVT history. Brake fluid ~every 2 yr. All engines timing chain (interference).

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: 0–60: 2026 Sentra 8.7 sec, 1/4-mile 16.7 @ 86 mph (MotorWeek instrumented test). B17 SR Turbo/NISMO 0–60: Not verified — needs OEM/publication source (publication estimates cluster in the low-7s). NISMO (2017–2019) was the factory performance trim: tuned suspension, 18-in wheels, aero, 215/45 tires — same 188 hp as SR Turbo.

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: B17 SR Turbo/NISMO has the only real tuner base — shared MR16DDT with Juke means intakes, downpipes, cat-backs and ECU tunes exist; coilovers (e.g., HIRO) for B18; wheel/tire upgrades common. CVT cars should avoid aggressive tunes.

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: B17 sat on Nissan's V platform with a twist-beam rear axle. B18 moved to the CMF-C/D architecture — 2 in lower and wider, with a multi-link independent rear suspension replacing the beam axle, a big rigidity and ride-quality step. The 2026 car keeps the MR20DD but adds 6% more body rigidity, a retuned CVT with Sport mode, acoustic glass and available ProPILOT Assist.

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: SE-R/Spec V nostalgia keeps the nameplate warm, but modern Sentras are commuter cars; the B17 NISMO is the only recent trim with cult interest, and its tuner support piggybacks on the Juke 1.6T aftermarket. B18 is respected as a value play, not a build platform.

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Rivals: Honda Civic, Toyota Corolla, Hyundai Elantra, Mazda3, Kia K4. Sentra undercuts on price and offers class-leading front legroom and seats, but its NA 2.0/CVT powertrain trails rivals' turbo and hybrid options on both pace and MPG; B18+ build quality now rates better than its reputation (IQS class win), while B17 CVT history still hurts resale.

nissan-frontier.subtitle: Midsize pickup; 2015–2021 (D40; new 3.8L V6/9AT powertrain for 2020–2021), 2022–2026 (D41)

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: D40 (2015–2019): 4.0L VQ40DE NA V6 (port injection); 2.5L QR25DE NA I4 (4x2 King Cab only, through 2019). D40 (2020–2021) & D41 (2022–2026): 3.8L VQ38DD NA DI V6 (93% new parts vs. 4.0L, integrated exhaust manifolds).

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: D40 2015–2019: 5MT (2.5L), 6MT (V6 4x2), or 5-speed automatic. 2020–2021: all-new 9-speed automatic only (manuals discontinued). D41: 9-speed automatic (similar to Titan's unit, per Nissan; supplier code not verified). No CVT ever used.

horsepower.label: Horsepower
horsepower.text: 2.5L: 152 hp @ 5,200 rpm. 4.0L V6: 261 hp @ 5,600 rpm. 3.8L V6: 310 hp @ 6,400 rpm (best-in-class claim at 2020/2022 launch).

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: RWD (4x2) standard; part-time shift-on-the-fly 4WD (2HI/4HI/4LO) optional; PRO-4X (4x4 only) adds electronic locking rear diff; PRO-X (2022+, 4x2 Crew Cab) is the 2WD off-road-trim counterpart.

dimensions.label: Dimensions
dimensions.text: D40: wheelbase 125.9 in (139.9 in LWB) / length 205.5 in (219.4 in LWB) / width 72.8 in / height ~70.1 in / curb ~4,100–4,624 lb by configuration (Crew samples 4,443–4,624 lb; base King Cab 2.5 4x2 below range — exact figure needs OEM source). D41: wheelbase 126.0 in (139.8 in LWB) / length 210.2 in (224.1 in LWB) / width 73.0–74.7 in / height 71.6–72.9 in / curb 4,451–4,773 lb (Nissan spec sheet).

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: D40 is regarded as one of Nissan's toughest products — simple proven powertrain, fully boxed frame; Consumer Reports flagged high expected reliability on the newest models (per Motor Verso). Chronic D40 watch items: frame corrosion in salt states (dealers call it the #1 killer), timing-chain guide wear at high mileage, and the radiator/ATF-cooler cross-contamination ("SMOD") issue concentrated on 2005–2010 automatics — still worth pressure-testing on any D40. D41 early build issues are recall-level, not pattern failures: 9-speed parking-pawl rollaway recall (180,176 trucks, 2020–2022 Frontier/Titan) and airbag-inflator recall 24V154000 (2024 Frontier).

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: D40: SMOD radiator/trans-cooler failure (mainly pre-2012 autos — verify cooler on any automatic D40); timing-chain guide/tensioner rattle on VQ40DE at 100k+; frame rust-through in rust belt; leaf-spring and secondary air-injection niggles. 2020+: parking pawl may not engage in Park (recall; advise parking brake). No systemic VQ38DD engine defect documented as of mid-2026.

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: VQ38DD: 0W-20, 5.3 qt w/filter; platinum-tipped plugs ~105,000 mi; coolant 12.7 qt capacity. Oil/filter 5,000 mi/6 mo (severe 3,750 mi). 4WD transfer case/front diff fluids per severe-service schedule when towing/off-road. All engines timing chain. ATF: Nissan Matic-spec service under severe use; exact OEM interval — Not verified — needs OEM source (consult ESM by VIN).

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: 0–60: 2020 Frontier 3.8 ~8.0 sec, 2021 ~7.4 sec (autofiles compilation); D41 PRO-4X low-7s in publication tests — precise figure Not verified. Max tow 6,720 lb (D41, properly equipped); max payload 1,610 lb. PRO-4X: Bilstein shocks, e-locker, skid plates, all-terrain tires.

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: One of Nissan's strongest aftermarket scenes: lift/level kits and 33-in tires, winch bumpers, skid plates, bed racks/rooftop tents, regears, and Stillen supercharger support for the VQ40DE; NISMO factory off-road accessories offered on D41. Long-travel suspension kits exist for desert builds.

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: D40 rides the F-Alpha fully boxed ladder frame shared in concept with the first-gen Titan, with double-wishbone front / leaf-sprung solid-axle rear. The 2020–2021 trucks were a rolling powertrain prototype for the D41: the DI 3.8L and 9-speed landed a year before the new body. D41 keeps the same 126.0-in wheelbase and frame architecture but adds hydraulic cab mounts (−80% road vibration), retuned hydraulic steering (+16% ratio), bigger stabilizer bars and a modern interior.

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: The D40 has a loyal budget-truck following (ClubFrontier community) — cheap, simple, durable, hugely modifiable; values rose as people discovered it. The D41 PRO-4X is respected as a no-nonsense off-roader and one of the last NA-V6 midsize trucks; tuner support is chassis/overland-focused rather than power-focused.

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Rivals: Toyota Tacoma, Ford Ranger, Chevy Colorado/GMC Canyon, Jeep Gladiator. Frontier's pitch is value and simplicity — the only naturally aspirated V6 left in segment vs. rivals' turbo fours, typically the lowest price, solid reliability; it trails the GM trucks and Ranger on refinement, towing tech and cabin polish, and the Tacoma on resale.

nissan-pathfinder.subtitle: 3-row midsize SUV; 2015–2020 (R52; facelift 2017; no 2021 model year), 2022–2026 (R53; Rock Creek from 2023; refresh for 2026)

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: R52 2015–2016: 3.5L VQ35DE NA V6 (port injection). R52 2017–2020 & R53 2022–2026: 3.5L VQ35DD NA DI V6 (11.0:1, mirror-bore coating). (A 250-hp net hybrid existed for 2014 only — outside the 2015+ window.)

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: R52: Xtronic CVT (Jatco CVT8 family; D-Step logic added 2017). R53: all-new 9-speed automatic with paddle shifters (replaced the CVT).

horsepower.label: Horsepower
horsepower.text: R52 2015–2016: 260 hp. R52 2017–2020: 284 hp @ 6,400 rpm. R53: 284 hp @ 6,400 rpm; Rock Creek 295 hp @ 6,400 rpm on premium fuel.

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: R52: FWD or 4x4-i selectable 4WD. R53: FWD standard; Intelligent 4WD with direct-coupling clutch and 7-position Drive & Terrain Mode selector (Standard/Sport/Eco/Snow/Sand/Mud-Rut/Tow); Hill Descent Control on 4WD.

dimensions.label: Dimensions
dimensions.text: R52 2015–2016: length 197.2 in / width 77.2 in / height ~69.5 in / wheelbase 114.2 in / curb 4,149–4,486 lb. R52 2017–2020: length 198.5 in / width 77.3 in / height 69.5 in / curb 4,292–4,662 lb. R53: length 197.7 in / width 77.9 in / height 69.7–70.9 in / wheelbase 114.2 in / curb 4,317–4,625 lb (Nissan spec sheet).

mpg.label: Fuel economy (MPG)
mpg.text: R52 2017–2020: 20/27/22 2WD; 19/26 4WD (Nissan); 2015–2016 rated the same 20/27 2WD, 19/26 4WD (EPA carryover per Nissan's 2017 release). R53: 21/26/23 2WD; 21/27/23 4WD (Platinum 4WD 20/25/22) (Nissan/EPA).

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: R52: 2013–2017 Pathfinders were swept into Nissan's CVT class-action/warranty-extension programs (84 mo/84,000 mi) over CVT judder; 2013–2016 models recalled June 2022 for secondary hood-latch corrosion (322,671 US vehicles). R53: ~295 NHTSA complaints 2022–2025 (The Weekly Driver), top areas brakes, engine, electrical — far below R52 CVT-era levels; airbag-inflator recall 24V154000 covers 2024 Pathfinder. The 9-speed swap removed the single biggest failure point.

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: R52: CVT shudder/judder and torque-converter failure (class actions, multiple TSBs — the model's defining defect); hood-latch corrosion recall; radiator fan and liftgate strut complaints secondary. R53: brake-system complaints (premature wear/feel), scattered electrical issues; the 9-speed rollaway recall that hit Frontier/Titan does NOT apply to Pathfinder (Nissan limited it to pickups). Rock Creek's off-road tires/trims add no known mechanical liabilities.

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: VQ35DD: 0W-20, 5.1 qt w/filter; iridium plugs ~105,000 mi; coolant 2.5 gal capacity. Oil/filter 5,000 mi/6 mo (severe 3,750 mi). R52 CVT fluid NS-3 — 60,000-mi drain/fill strongly advised given the judder history. R53 9AT fluid service per severe schedule — exact OEM interval Not verified — needs OEM source. All engines timing chain. 2025+ models include Nissan Maintenance Care (3 oil changes/2 yr).

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: 0–60: R52 2019 SV FWD ~6.4 sec, 1/4-mile 14.9 @ 93 mph (Motor Matchup); R53 0–60 — Not verified — needs OEM/publication source (publications put it in the high-6s). Max towing: 5,000 lb (R52 2013–2016), 6,000 lb (R52 2017+ and R53, properly equipped, SAE J2807). Rock Creek: 5/8-in lift, off-road-tuned suspension, all-terrain tires, tubular roof rack.

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: Light aftermarket — all-terrain tires, mild lifts, roof racks and towing accessories dominate; Rock Creek serves as the factory "build." No meaningful engine-tuning scene on either generation.

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: R52 marked Pathfinder's shift from body-on-frame truck to unibody crossover (shared D-platform bones with Murano/Maxima) — better packaging and MPG, but paired to the problem-prone CVT. R53 is a clean-sheet unibody with ~50% more high-strength steel, stiffer suspension mounts (+28% front roll stiffness), dual-pinion EPS, and the CVT replaced by a conventional 9-speed automatic; 8-passenger seating standard, 6,000-lb tow.

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: Enthusiasts revere the R50/R51 truck-based Pathfinders and largely wrote off the R52 as a soft-roader with a bad transmission. The R53 partially rehabbed the nameplate — the 9AT and Rock Creek trim brought back some credibility — but it remains a family hauler, not a build platform.

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Rivals: Toyota Highlander, Honda Pilot, Kia Telluride, Hyundai Palisade, Ford Explorer. Pathfinder undercuts Telluride/Palisade on price and beats them on max towing (6,000 vs 5,000–5,500 lb), and the 9AT/V6 combo is honest and proven; it trails the Koreans on cabin polish and the Highlander hybrid on MPG, and the R52's CVT reputation still shadows used-car values.

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: Z52: 3.5L VQ35DE NA V6 (sequential multi-port injection, 10.3:1). 2025–2026: 2.0L KR20DDET VC-Turbo turbo I4 (variable compression 8.0:1–14.0:1, DI+port; shared with Altima/Infiniti QX50 family, detuned to 241 hp vs. 268 hp in QX50).

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: Z52: Xtronic CVT (Jatco CVT8 family; EPA designation AV-S7). 2025–2026: conventional 9-speed automatic (same transmission family as Pathfinder R53) — the CVT is gone.

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: Z52: FWD standard, Intelligent AWD optional. 2025–2026: FWD (SV) or Intelligent AWD; AWD standard on SL and Platinum per dealer spec sheets.

dimensions.label: Dimensions
dimensions.text: Z52: length 192.8 in / width 75.4 in / height 67.8 in / wheelbase 111.2 in / curb 3,768–4,037 lb (FWD S to AWD Platinum). 2025–2026: wheelbase 111.2 in / length ~192 in / width 78.0 in (+2.6 in) / curb ~4,200–4,500 lb (press/review figures); exact height and per-trim curb weights — Not verified — needs OEM source.

mpg.label: Fuel economy (MPG)
mpg.text: Z52: 21/28/24, both FWD and AWD (fueleconomy.gov, 2018); some 2019+ trims rated 20/28/23 (Edmunds). 2025–2026: 21/27/23 for both FWD and AWD (EPA figures published via Car Confections; Nissan had estimated 21/27/23 pre-launch).

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: Z52 is a core member of the Nissan CVT litigation family: Beaver v. Nissan (2015–2018 Murano + 2016–2018 Maxima, ~520,000 vehicles, final court approval July 21, 2025) extended CVT warranty coverage to 84 months/84,000 miles (Nissan campaign bulletin, October 3, 2025). The VQ35DE engine itself is a durable, well-understood unit. Separate safety recall 16V636000 (2015–2017 Murano): ABS actuator pump seal can leak brake fluid onto a circuit board — fire risk. 2025+ generation: too new for pattern data.

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: Z52: CVT hesitation, judder and premature failure (class action + 84/84 warranty extension — the dominant shop ticket); ABS actuator brake-fluid leak/fire recall; low 1,500-lb tow rating reflects CVT thermal limits. 2025–2026: the KR20DDET engine family has a documented bearing-defect recall on other models (25V437000 covers 2019–2020 Altima, 2021–2024 Rogue, QX50/QX55) — Murano is NOT on that campaign as of mid-2026, but shops should monitor for bearing-noise complaints given the shared engine family.

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: Oil/filter 5,000 mi/6 mo (severe 3,750 mi). Iridium plugs ~105,000 mi. Long-life coolant ~105,000 mi first change. Z52 CVT fluid NS-3 — no fixed normal interval; 60,000-mi drain/fill is shop best practice given litigation history. Brake fluid ~every 2 yr. Both engines timing chain.

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: 0–60: Z52 mid-7-second range in period publication tests — exact figure Not verified — needs OEM/publication source; 2025 VC-Turbo ~8 sec (dealer-published figure, unverified by major outlet). Max towing 1,500 lb both generations. No performance trims.

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: Essentially none beyond wheels/tires, window tint and hitch accessories; no engine-tuning market (CVT on Z52, niche VC-Turbo on new gen).

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: Z52 rides Nissan's D platform (shared bones with R52 Pathfinder/Maxima) with a style-first body; the 2019 facelift added Nissan Safety Shield 360 and revised styling. The 2025 4th gen uses a reworked version of the outgoing chassis (per SAE/Mobility Engineering) but swaps the entire powertrain concept: VC-Turbo four + 9-speed automatic replacing the V6 + CVT, plus dual 12.3-inch displays, available massaging/ventilated seats and "Invisible Hood View" camera tech.

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: No enthusiast following — the Murano is a design-led, comfort-first near-luxury crossover bought for its seats, styling and quiet ride. Z52s are cheap used but the CVT reputation scares informed buyers; the 2025 redesign's 9-speed answers that criticism directly.

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Rivals: Honda Passport, Chevy Blazer, Hyundai Santa Fe, Toyota Crown Signia/Venza, Ford Edge (discontinued). Murano historically won on style and seat comfort while trailing on cargo space and drivetrain trust (CVT); Passport/Blazer tow and drive better. The 2025+ car closes the transmission gap but keeps a complex engine and no hybrid option, where Toyota and Hyundai rivals now offer one.

nissan-kicks.subtitle: Subcompact crossover; 2018–2024 (P15, 1st gen; facelift 2021), 2025–2026 (2nd gen; first AWD option)

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: P15: 1.6L HR16DE NA I4 (port injection). 2025–2026: 2.0L MR20DD NA DI I4 (shared with Sentra).

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: Both generations: Xtronic CVT only (Jatco CVT7/JF015E family on P15). No manual offered in the US.

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: P15: FWD only (no AWD ever offered). 2025–2026: FWD standard; Intelligent AWD optional — a Kicks first; ground clearance 8.4 in on the new gen.

dimensions.label: Dimensions
dimensions.text: P15: length 169.1 in / width 69.3 in / height 62.4 in (63.3 in on 2022) / wheelbase 103.1 in / curb 2,639–2,744 lb. 2025–2026: length 171.9 in / width 70.9 in / height 64.0–64.2 in / wheelbase 104.6 in (FWD), 104.9 in (AWD) / curb weight — Not verified — needs OEM source (roughly 300 lb heavier than P15 per launch coverage).

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: P15's simple port-injected 1.6L has no systemic engine defect pattern; watch items are CVT-related complaints at mileage (same Jatco CVT7 family as Versa — Kicks was not named in the big CVT class actions, but the hardware is related). Recalls: 2021 Kicks included in EPS torque-sensor recall (11,824 Versa/Kicks units — loss of power-steering assist); 2023–2024 Kicks included in driver airbag-inflator recall 24V154000; a 2025–2026 door-latch recall (~26,000 vehicles incl. Kicks) is reported in trade press — verify campaign number at nhtsa.gov. 2nd gen too new for pattern data.

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: P15: CVT judder/shudder complaints at higher mileage (owner/NHTSA reports — no class action specific to Kicks); electric power steering assist loss (recall above); modest passing power is the everyday complaint, not a defect. 2025–2026: no documented pattern failures as of mid-2026.

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: Oil/filter 5,000 mi/6 mo (severe 3,750 mi). Iridium plugs ~105,000 mi. Long-life coolant ~105,000 mi first change. CVT fluid NS-2 — 60,000-mi drain/fill recommended given CVT7 family history. Brake fluid ~every 2 yr. Both engines timing chain.

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: 0–60: P15 roughly 10-sec range — precise figure Not verified — needs OEM/publication source. 2025 2.0: ~9.0 sec 0–100 km/h (publication estimate). SR is a cosmetic/appearance trim, not a power upgrade.

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: Cosmetic only — wraps, wheels, roof baskets; effectively zero performance aftermarket for either generation.

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: P15 is built on Nissan's Versa-derived V platform — light (~2,650 lb), simple, cheap to run; the 2021 facelift refreshed styling, added disc rear brakes on upper trims and standard Safety Shield 360. The 2025 2nd gen moves to a larger, more SUV-like package (+3 in length, +~300 lb), adopts the Sentra's 2.0L MR20DD, adds available AWD with terrain modes, dual 12.3-inch displays (upper trims) and available ProPILOT Assist.

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: None — the Kicks is a budget urban runabout prized for price, MPG and parkability. The community treats it as a first car/commuter; the only "cult" angle is its value-per-dollar reputation among practical owners.

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Rivals: Honda HR-V, Chevy Trax/Trailblazer, Hyundai Kona/Venue, Kia Soul, Toyota Corolla Cross. P15 Kicks undercut everything on price and beat most on MPG but was FWD-only, slow, and light on refinement; the 2025+ gen fixes AWD availability and power but trades away some MPG. HR-V and Corolla Cross remain the resale/reliability benchmarks; Trax is the value benchmark.

ram-1500.subtitle: Full-size half-ton pickup. 2015–2018 (4th gen, DS platform; continued as "1500 Classic" 2019–2024), 2019–2024 (5th gen, DT platform), 2025–2026 (DT facelift — Hurricane I6 replaces HEMI; 5.7L HEMI returns for 2026)

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: 2015–2018 (DS / Classic): 3.6L Pentastar V6 (ERB, naturally aspirated, DOHC VVT); 5.7L HEMI V8 (EZH, NA, MDS cylinder deactivation, VVT); 3.0L EcoDiesel V6 (VM Motori L630, DOHC 24v turbodiesel) 240 hp/420 lb-ft. 2019–2024 (DT): 3.6L Pentastar V6 eTorque (48V belt motor-generator mild hybrid, standard); 5.7L HEMI V8 (EZH) with or without eTorque; 3.0L EcoDiesel Gen 3 (VM L630, 260 hp/480 lb-ft, 2020–2023); 6.2L supercharged HEMI V8 "Hellcat" (TRX only, 2021–2024). 2025–2026: 3.6L Pentastar eTorque (base); 3.0L Hurricane twin-turbo I6 standard-output (GME-T6); 3.0L Hurricane twin-turbo I6 High-Output (540 hp); 5.7L HEMI V8 eTorque re-added for 2026.

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: All 1500s in scope use ZF-sourced TorqueFlite 8-speed automatics: 845RE behind the 3.6L V6; 8HP70 behind EcoDiesel and 5.7L applications; 8HP75 high-capacity unit behind HEMI/eTorque V8 and the TRX. No manual transmission offered 2015–2026.

horsepower.label: Horsepower
horsepower.text: 3.6L Pentastar: 305 hp @ 6,400 rpm (all years). 5.7L HEMI (with or without eTorque): 395 hp @ 5,600 rpm. EcoDiesel: 240 hp (2014–2019 DS/Classic), 260 hp @ 3,600 rpm (2020–2023 Gen 3). TRX 6.2L supercharged: 702 hp @ 6,100 rpm. Hurricane SO: 420 hp; Hurricane HO: 540 hp.

torque.label: Torque
torque.text: 3.6L Pentastar: 269 lb-ft @ ~4,175–4,800 rpm. 5.7L HEMI: 410 lb-ft @ 3,950 rpm. EcoDiesel: 420 lb-ft (DS) / 480 lb-ft (Gen 3). TRX: 650 lb-ft @ 4,800 rpm. Hurricane SO: 469 lb-ft; Hurricane HO: 521 lb-ft.

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: Rear-wheel drive (4x2) standard on most trims; part-time 4WD optional across the range (Borg Warner transfer cases; auto-4WD full-time mode on upper trims). Rebel, TRX (2021–2024), and RHO (2025+) are 4WD only. Hurricane HO models are 4x4 standard.

dimensions.label: Dimensions
dimensions.text: DS/Classic (2015–2024): length 229 in (Crew Cab 5'7") – 237.9 in (Crew Cab 6'4"); width 79.4 in; height ~74.6–77.2 in (air suspension adds height options); wheelbase 140.5 in (Crew 5'7") / 149.5 in (Crew 6'4"); curb weight ~4,750–5,700 lb depending on cab/engine/4x4. DT (2019–2026): Quad Cab 6'4": length 228.9 in, wheelbase 140.5 in; Crew Cab 5'7": length 232.9 in, wheelbase 144.6 in; Crew Cab 6'4": wheelbase 153.5 in; width 82.1 in; height 77.5–77.7 in (standard suspension; up to ~79.6 in with air suspension Off-Road 2 setting); curb weight ~4,800–5,800 lb; TRX ~6,350 lb.

mpg.label: Fuel economy (MPG)
mpg.text: DS (EPA city/highway/combined): 3.6L 4x2 17/25/20, 4x4 16/23/19; 5.7L 4x2 15/22/17, 4x4 15/21/17; EcoDiesel 4x2 20/27/23, 4x4 19/26/22 (2014–2016 figures revised downward after EPA/CARB emissions-settlement reflash — check fueleconomy.gov for the exact year). DT 2019+: 3.6L eTorque 4x2 20/25/22, 4x4 19/24/21; 5.7L HEMI 4x2 15/22/17, 4x4 15/21/17; HEMI eTorque 4x2 17/23/19; EcoDiesel (2020+) 4x2 22/32/26, 4x4 21/29/24. 2025+: 3.6L eTorque 4x2 20/25/22 (HFE 4x2 20/26/23); Hurricane SO 4x2 18/25/21, 4x4 17/24/19; Hurricane HO 4x4 15/21/17 (RHO similar). TRX: 10/14/12.

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: Mixed reputation by generation. The DS (2015–2018) is mechanically simple and durable in Pentastar/HEMI form, but the 5.7L has a well-documented lifter/camshaft failure pattern ("HEMI tick") and EcoDiesel trucks carry recall and settlement baggage (see Known issues). The DT won MotorTrend Truck of the Year 2019, 2020, 2021 (and 2025 for the facelift) and Ram scored strongly in J.D. Power initial-quality and APEAL studies, but 2019 first-year trucks had above-average electrical/infotainment complaints and Consumer Reports owner-survey data for DT trucks flags in-car electronics and electrical accessories as trouble spots. The 2022 Ram 1500 earned an IIHS Top Safety Pick and a 5-star NHTSA overall crash rating for the crew cab. NHTSA recall-count totals per model year: Not verified — needs OEM source (use nhtsa.gov/recalls per VIN for customer vehicles).

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: (1) 5.7L HEMI lifter roller seizure scoring the camshaft — "HEMI tick" progressing to misfire; affects DS and DT trucks, debated incidence (owner-forum estimates run high-teens percent); also broken exhaust-manifold bolts mimicking the tick. (2) EcoDiesel EGR cooler thermal-fatigue cracking/fire risk — October 2019 recall of ~107,000+ trucks (2014–2019 Ram 1500 EcoDiesel), followed by a 2025 class-action settlement with extended warranty and reimbursement terms. (3) EcoDiesel emissions: EPA/CARB/FCA settlement covered 2014–2016 Ram 1500 EcoDiesel (required reflash; reduced advertised MPG). (4) eTorque 48V battery pack / MGU faults on 2020–2024 DT trucks — stop/start unavailable warnings, power loss. (5) DT air suspension failures (compressor, valve block, cold-weather faults) and Uconnect screen/software issues. (6) DS air suspension and TIPM-related electrical faults on earlier trucks.

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: Gas engines: oil change per oil-change-indicator, max ~10,000 miles/12 months (many owners/techs run 5,000–7,500 on HEMIs to mitigate lifter wear); 5W-20 (5.7L) / 0W-20 (3.6L) full synthetic. Spark plugs: ~100,000 miles (iridium, gas engines). Coolant: 10-year/150,000-mile OAT antifreeze. ZF 8-speed: factory fill is "lifetime," but severe-service guidance and independent specialists recommend fluid/filter service ~60,000 miles. Brake fluid: inspect at service intervals; no fixed factory interval widely published — Not verified — needs OEM source. All engines are timing-chain designs (no timing belt service). EcoDiesel: oil ~10,000 miles max; fuel filter per schedule (verify by year — Not verified — needs OEM source); timing belt on the VM L630 (~150,000-mile replacement is widely cited — verify against owner manual for the exact year).

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: 5.7L HEMI crew 4x4 ~6.4–6.5 s 0–60 (press tests). TRX: 4.5 s 0–60 and 12.9 s quarter-mile @ 108 mph (Ram factory); Car and Driver measured 3.7 s 0–60 and 12.3 s @ 110 mph. 2025 RHO (Hurricane HO): 4.6 s 0–60 factory claim. Performance trims: Rebel (off-road), TRX (2021–2024, 702 hp), RHO (2025+, 540 hp), Warlock (value off-road appearance). Sport/Black Express street trims for 2026.

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: Supercharger kits are the big-ticket item: ProCharger (+140 hp on 93 octane, 2019–2023 5.7L) and RIPP (3.6L V6, DS/Classic). TRX is a major Hennessey platform (Mammoth 1000, 1,000+ hp). Common bolt-ons: cold-air intakes (K&N, aFe, S&B), cat-back exhausts, DiabloSport/handheld tuning, leveling/lift kits with 33–35 in tires (Rebel/TRX accept 35s), EcoDiesel ECM tunes for MPG. Factory performance variants: TRX, RHO, Rebel GT.

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: The fourth-generation DS (2009–2018, sold as Classic through 2024) introduced the class-exclusive coil-spring 5-link rear suspension and optional 4-corner air suspension, plus the first 8-speed automatic in the segment (2013). The fifth-generation DT (2019) moved to a new frame using ~98% high-strength steel with roughly a 225-lb weight reduction, added the eTorque 48V mild-hybrid system to V6 and V8 engines, active grille shutters/air dam, and the 12-inch Uconnect 5 portrait screen. The 2025 facelift replaced the HEMI V8 with the twin-turbo Hurricane I6 family (heavier but more powerful and efficient), added the luxury Tungsten trim and a standard-output Hurricane across mid-range trims; customer backlash brought the 5.7L HEMI back as an option for 2026.

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: The 1500 community is large and split by powertrain: HEMI loyalists (who drove the 2026 HEMI return), TRX owners who treat the truck as a collectible super-truck, Rebel off-roaders, and EcoDiesel MPG devotees. The Classic body is popular as a value platform for lifts and superchargers because of cheap, well-understood hardware. Tuner support (DiabloSport, HP Tuners, Hennessey, ProCharger, RIPP) is among the deepest of any half-ton.

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Main rivals are the Ford F-150 and Chevrolet Silverado/GMC Sierra 1500; the Toyota Tundra is the reliability benchmark. The Ram's coil/air-spring rear suspension gives it the best ride and the DT the best interior in the segment, and used pricing is typically softer than a comparable F-150. The trade-off is the HEMI lifter issue and EcoDiesel recall history, which drag its long-term reliability reputation below the Tundra's.

ram-2500.subtitle: Full-size 3/4-ton heavy-duty pickup. 2015–2018 (4th gen HD), 2019–2024 (5th gen HD), 2025–2026 (5th gen facelift — Cummins HO + TorqueFlite HD 8-speed)

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: 2015–2018: 5.7L HEMI V8 (NA, VVT, standard on 2500 SRW); 6.4L HEMI V8 (NA, 16-valve, VVT, MDS Fuel Saver cylinder deactivation, cooled EGR); 6.7L Cummins ISB 6.7 turbodiesel I6 (Bosch CP3 injection pump, VGT turbo) in two outputs by transmission. 2019–2024: 6.4L HEMI V8 (gas standard); 6.7L Cummins ISB turbodiesel I6 (Bosch CP4.2 pump 2019+, single 370 hp/850 lb-ft output on the 2500 — the Aisin-backed High-Output version was 3500-only). 2025–2026: 6.4L HEMI V8 (405 hp); 6.7L Cummins High-Output turbodiesel I6 (430 hp/1,075 lb-ft) — first time the HO diesel is offered in the 2500.

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: 2015–2018: 6-speed 66RFE automatic (5.7L and 6.4L gas); 6-speed G56 manual or 6-speed 68RFE automatic (Cummins; manual discontinued after 2018). 2019–2024: 8-speed TorqueFlite 8HP75-LCV automatic (6.4L gas); 6-speed 68RFE automatic (Cummins). 2025–2026: 8-speed ZF 8HP75 (gas) and new TorqueFlite HD 8-speed automatic (ZF PowerLine-based) behind the Cummins HO, replacing the 68RFE.

horsepower.label: Horsepower
horsepower.text: 2015–2018: 5.7L HEMI 383 hp @ 5,600 rpm; 6.4L HEMI 410 hp @ 5,600 rpm; 6.7L Cummins 350 hp @ 2,800 rpm (G56 manual) or 370 hp @ 2,800 rpm (68RFE). 2019–2024: 6.4L 410 hp @ 5,600 rpm; Cummins 370 hp @ 2,800 rpm. 2025–2026: 6.4L 405 hp; Cummins HO 430 hp.

torque.label: Torque
torque.text: 2015–2018: 5.7L 400 lb-ft @ 4,000 rpm; 6.4L 429 lb-ft @ 4,000 rpm; Cummins 660 lb-ft @ 1,400–1,500 rpm (manual) or 800 lb-ft @ 1,600–1,700 rpm (68RFE). 2019–2024: 6.4L 429 lb-ft; Cummins 850 lb-ft @ 1,700 rpm. 2025–2026: 6.4L 429 lb-ft; Cummins HO 1,075 lb-ft (available in every gear per Ram).

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: Rear-wheel drive or part-time 4WD (Borg Warner 44-46/44-47 transfer cases; solid AAM front axle on 4x4). Power Wagon is 4WD only with front/rear electronic locking differentials, electronic sway-bar disconnect, and a 12,000-lb winch (4th gen; 5th gen similar with updated spec).

dimensions.label: Dimensions
dimensions.text: 2015–2018: length ~231–259 in by cab/bed; width 79.4 in; height ~77–80.6 in; wheelbase 140.5–169.5 in; curb weight ~6,000–7,000 lb. 2019–2026: length 232–260.8 in (Crew Cab 6'4" = 238.2 in class; Crew Cab 8' / Mega Cab configurations to ~260 in); width 79.5 in (mirrors excluded; ~83.5 in with standard mirrors per some spec sheets); height 78–80.9 in; wheelbase 149 in (Crew 6'4") / 160.5 in (Crew 8'); curb weight ~6,500–7,600 lb (2023 Limited 4x4 diesel example: 7,281 lb).

mpg.label: Fuel economy (MPG)
mpg.text: Not EPA-rated — 2500-series trucks exceed the 8,500-lb GVWR threshold for EPA fuel-economy labeling, so no official city/highway/combined figures exist. Owner/instrumented-test real-world figures (Cummins 4x4 crew): roughly 14–17 mpg combined; 6.4L gas: roughly 10–14 mpg. Treat as anecdotal, not official.

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: The 6.7L Cummins engine itself has a strong long-life reputation (B-series design lineage with many high-mileage examples), but the truck around it carries known weak points (CP4 pump, front-end wear, 68RFE under load — see Known issues). J.D. Power spec/quality data and owner surveys put the Ram HD line mid-pack for the segment; the 2025 redesign (PowerLine 8-speed replacing the 68RFE) directly addresses the most-criticized component. IIHS does not crash-test HD pickups; NHTSA ratings are limited. NHTSA recall-count totals per model year: Not verified — needs OEM source.

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: (1) Bosch CP4.2 high-pressure fuel-pump failure on 2019–2020 6.7L Cummins — metal-debris contamination can destroy the fuel system; NHTSA recall 21V-880 covered ~222,000+ Ram 2500/3500 trucks with a pump replacement. Aftermarket CP3 conversions are common insurance on tuned trucks. (2) Cummins emissions settlement: December 2023 federal settlement requiring Cummins to recall/repair 2013–2019 Ram 2500/3500 6.7L trucks (~630,000 vehicles) over emissions-control software; $1.675B civil penalty. (3) Front-end steering/suspension wear — "death wobble" shimmy complaints tied to track bar, ball joints, tie-rod ends and steering linkage on solid-axle 4x4 trucks; multiple TSBs and a steering-linkage recall on 2019-era HDs. (4) 68RFE automatic: torque-converter shudder, valve-body and shift-quality complaints, and premature failure when tuned/deleted without transmission upgrades (2015–2024 Cummins). (5) Exhaust-manifold bolt breakage and lifter/cam wear on 5.7L/6.4L HEMIs (less common on 6.4 than 5.7 but documented). (6) DEF/SCR and EGR system faults on emissions-intact diesels.

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: 6.7L Cummins (per Ram schedule): engine oil and filter 15,000 miles/6 months/500 hours (7,500 miles/6 months severe; 10W-30 or 5W-40 CK-4 for 2019+, 15W-40 CJ-4/5W-40 CK-4 earlier; 12 qt capacity); fuel filters (engine- and chassis-mounted) 15,000 miles/12 months; crankcase ventilation filter 67,500 miles (2007.5–2018) / 75,000 miles (2019+); coolant flush 150,000 miles/120 months (10-year OAT); 68RFE fluid/filters 120,000 miles normal / 60,000 miles severe (ATF+4); transfer case 60,000 miles normal / 30,000 severe; front/rear differential fluid 15,000–20,000 miles under severe service; valve-lash check 150,000 miles (through 2018; not required 2019+). Gas engines (5.7L/6.4L): oil per indicator, max ~10,000 miles/12 months; spark-plug interval on HD gas engines: Not verified — needs OEM source; all engines use timing chains.

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: No factory 0–60 figures are published for HD trucks; instrumented press tests of 2019+ Cummins crew-cab 4x4s land around 8 seconds, but a specific verified number is Not verified — needs OEM/instrumented source. Specialty trims: Power Wagon (off-road flagship, lockers/winch/disconnecting sway bar), Rebel HD (2023+, off-road trim with available Cummins), Warlock (2026 value off-road trim). Max towing ~20,000 lb (2025+ 2500 diesel); 2019+ 2500 max towing ~14,000–20,000 lb by configuration.

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: The Cummins aftermarket is enormous: EFI Live and HP Tuners ECM/TCM tuning (mild tow tunes to 100+ hp gains), cold-air intakes, 4–5 in exhausts, CP3 pump conversions, and built 68RFE transmissions (valve bodies, converters, billet shafts — ATS, BD, Revmax, SunCoast). 35-in tires fit at stock height on 18/20-in wheels; leveling kits and 2.5–6 in lifts are standard practice. Emissions deletes exist but carry federal EPA enforcement risk — shops should not perform them. Power Wagon owners commonly add bumpers, airbags/compressor kits, and 35–37 in tires.

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: The fourth-generation HD (2010–2018) rides on a boxed steel frame with a solid AAM front axle and, from 2014, a class-exclusive 5-link coil-spring rear suspension on the 2500 with optional rear air suspension — the reason it rides better than leaf-sprung rivals. The 2019 fifth generation brought a new high-strength-steel frame, an aluminum hood, the ZF 8HP75 8-speed for gas engines, and updated Cummins cooling/EGR hardware along with the switch from the proven CP3 to the CP4.2 fuel pump. The 2025 facelift finally paired the High-Output Cummins (430 hp/1,075 lb-ft) with a ZF PowerLine-derived TorqueFlite HD 8-speed, ending the 68RFE era and adding a 10-year/100,000-mile powertrain warranty on 2026 models.

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: The 2500 is the default platform for the diesel performance community: "million-mile Cummins" lore, drag and sled-pull builds, and a deep towing culture. The Power Wagon has a genuine cult following as the most capable factory off-road HD sold. Stock-height 4th-gen 6.4L trucks are seen as the budget sweet spot; 2019+ owners trade CP4-pump anxiety stories and CP3-swap receipts on forums.

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Rivals are the Ford F-250 Super Duty (6.7L Power Stroke, 10-speed, up to 500 hp/1,200 lb-ft HO) and the Chevrolet Silverado/GMC Sierra 2500HD (6.6L Duramax L5P, Allison-branded 10-speed). The Ram wins on ride quality (coil/air rear) and the Cummins inline-6's simplicity and reputation, but until 2025 its 68RFE trailed both rivals' transmissions badly and its diesel output trailed Ford's HO. Value on used 4th-gen trucks is strong; reliability perception favors the Cummins powertrain but not the chassis electronics around it.

ram-3500.subtitle: Full-size 1-ton heavy-duty pickup (SRW and DRW "dually"). 2015–2018 (4th gen HD), 2019–2024 (5th gen HD), 2025–2026 (5th gen facelift — Cummins HO standard + TorqueFlite HD 8-speed)

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: 2015–2018: 5.7L HEMI V8 (standard, SRW only); 6.4L HEMI V8 (NA, 16v, MDS); 6.7L Cummins ISB turbodiesel I6 (CP3 pump through 2018) in three calibrations by transmission, including the Aisin-backed High Output exclusive to the 3500. 2019–2024: 6.4L HEMI V8; 6.7L Cummins standard-output (68RFE); 6.7L Cummins High Output (Aisin AS69RC, 3500-only; CGI engine block; CP4.2 pump 2019–2020, reverted to CP3 for 2021). 2025–2026: 6.4L HEMI V8 (405 hp); single 6.7L Cummins HO calibration (430 hp, new Bosch CP8 pump, glow plugs replace grid heater, grey cast iron block returns).

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: 2015–2018: 6-speed 66RFE (5.7L/6.4L gas); 6-speed G56 manual or 6-speed 68RFE (standard-output Cummins); 6-speed Aisin AS69RC (High Output Cummins, 3500 only). Manual dropped after 2018. 2019–2024: 8-speed 8HP75-LCV (6.4L gas); 68RFE (standard-output Cummins); AS69RC (HO). 2025–2026: ZF 8HP75 (gas); TorqueFlite HD 8-speed (ZF PowerLine 8) for the Cummins HO — single transmission across the diesel line.

horsepower.label: Horsepower
horsepower.text: 2015: 5.7L 383 hp @ 5,600 rpm; 6.4L 410 hp @ 5,600 rpm; Cummins 350 hp (G56) / 370 hp (68RFE) / 385 hp HO (AS69RC), all @ 2,800 rpm. 2016–2018: same except HO stays 385 hp. 2019–2020: 6.4L 410 hp; Cummins 370 hp (SO) / 400 hp (HO). 2021–2024: Cummins 370 hp / 420 hp HO. 2025–2026: 6.4L 405 hp; Cummins HO 430 hp @ 2,800 rpm.

torque.label: Torque
torque.text: 2015: 5.7L 400 lb-ft @ 4,000 rpm; 6.4L 429 lb-ft @ 4,000 rpm; Cummins 660 lb-ft (G56) / 800 lb-ft (68RFE) / 865 lb-ft HO. 2016–2017: HO raised to 900 lb-ft. 2018: HO raised to 930 lb-ft. 2019–2020: 850 lb-ft (SO) / 1,000 lb-ft @ 1,800 rpm (HO — first production pickup to reach 1,000 lb-ft). 2021–2024: 850 / 1,075 lb-ft. 2025–2026: HO 1,075 lb-ft @ 1,800 rpm.

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: RWD (SRW and DRW) or part-time 4WD (Borg Warner transfer cases; solid AAM front axle). DRW (dual rear wheel) available on Crew and Mega Cab long-bed models; Mega Cab is DRW-capable with the 6'4" box. No factory full-time AWD.

dimensions.label: Dimensions
dimensions.text: Length ~232–260.8 in by cab/bed; wheelbase 149–169.5 in; width 79.5 in (SRW; DRW dually fender width ~96 in class — exact DRW width Not verified — needs OEM source); height ~77.8–80.9 in; curb weight ~6,600–8,400 lb (diesel DRW crew at the top of the range; exact per configuration varies — treat as approximate). Max payload 7,680 lb (2019+); max towing 35,100 lb (2019–2020 HO DRW), up to 37,090 lb gooseneck (2021+ HO DRW).

mpg.label: Fuel economy (MPG)
mpg.text: Not EPA-rated — 3500-series trucks exceed the 8,500-lb GVWR threshold for EPA fuel-economy labeling; no official city/highway/combined figures exist. Real-world owner/press figures (HO Cummins DRW 4x4): roughly 12–16 mpg; treat as anecdotal, not official.

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: Mirrors the 2500 story: the Cummins inline-6 has a strong long-life reputation (B10 life ~250,000 miles per Cummins), and the Aisin AS69RC behind the HO engine is considered the most durable transmission in the HD segment, with a shorter 30,000-mile factory fluid interval. Weak points: 2019–2020 CP4.2 fuel pump (recall 21V-880 retrofits CP3), 68RFE behind standard-output trucks, solid-axle front-end wear ("death wobble"), and the 2013–2019 emissions-settlement recall (~630,000 Ram 2500/3500 trucks, $1.675B Cummins civil penalty, December 2023). IIHS does not test 1-ton pickups; NHTSA recall counts per year Not verified — needs OEM source.

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: (1) CP4.2 HPFP failure on 2019–2020 Cummins — NHTSA recall 21V-880, CP3 retrofit; catastrophic fuel-system contamination when it fails ($10,000+ repairs). (2) 2013–2019 Cummins emissions settlement (software recall). (3) Death wobble / track bar / ball joint / steering linkage wear on 4x4 trucks, worst on lifted or big-tire builds. (4) 68RFE converter shudder and valve-body failures under load or tuning. (5) Aisin AS69RC is robust but has documented torque-converter and overheating complaints when towing at max GCWR in heat — fluid service discipline matters. (6) HEMI exhaust-manifold bolts and lifter tick on gas trucks. (7) Grid-heater bolt failures ingested into the engine on pre-2025 Cummins (widely documented in the Cummins community; 2025+ uses glow plugs instead).

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: Same schedule family as the 2500: Cummins oil/filter 15,000 miles/6 months/500 hours (7,500 severe); fuel filters 15,000 miles; CCV filter 67,500 miles (–2018) / 75,000 miles (2019+); coolant 150,000 miles/10 years (OAT); 68RFE fluid 120,000 normal / 60,000 severe (ATF+4); **Aisin AS69RC fluid/filter 30,000 miles/24 months (Mopar ASRC fluid)**; transfer case 60,000/30,000; differentials 15,000–20,000 miles severe; valve lash 150,000 miles (–2018; not required 2019+ hydraulic lifters). Gas engines: oil per indicator, max ~10,000 miles/12 months; HD gas spark-plug interval Not verified — needs OEM source. All engines timing chains/gears, no belts.

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: No factory 0–60 published; a 4x4 HO diesel crew runs roughly 7.5–8.5 s in press testing — specific figure Not verified — needs OEM/instrumented source. No factory performance trims; capability trims instead: Power Wagon is 2500-only (not offered as 3500), Limited/Longhorn luxury trims, Tradesman work spec. Headline capability: 37,090-lb max gooseneck towing and 7,680-lb max payload (2021+ HO DRW).

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: The 3500 is the sled-pull/drag-build platform of choice: compound-turbo kits, built AS69RC/68RFE transmissions, EFI Live/HP Tuners calibrations, 1,000+ hp street builds are routine in the community. Work-truck mods dominate elsewhere: gooseneck/5th-wheel hitches, air-ride helper systems, dually wheels/tires, auxiliary fuel tanks. Emissions deletes carry federal EPA enforcement risk — shops should not perform them.

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: The 3500 differs from the 2500 in rear suspension: leaf springs (not coils) for maximum payload, with supplemental rear air suspension optional from 2014. The HO Cummins/Aisin AS69RC pairing (2013+) created the top-output tier of the segment, reaching 1,000 lb-ft in 2019 — a first for a production pickup. The 2019 redesign added a CGI engine block (−60 lb) and hydroformed high-strength frame; the 2025 facelift standardized the HO diesel with a ZF PowerLine 8-speed, new CP8 injection pump, glow-plug cold-start, and a return to a grey-iron block for NVH/rigidity.

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: Respected as the towing king of the Ram line; the Aisin-equipped HO trucks are the sought-after used buys precisely because the transmission tolerates stock and mild-tune torque. DRW long-bed Cummins trucks anchor the RV and hotshot-hauling communities. Value retention on clean HO/Aisin trucks is strong.

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Rivals: Ford F-350 Super Duty (6.7L Power Stroke HO 500 hp/1,200 lb-ft, 10R140 10-speed, max tow ~38,000 lb class) and Chevrolet Silverado/GMC Sierra 3500HD (Duramax L5P 470 hp/975 lb-ft, Allison 10-speed, max tow 36,000 lb). The Ram counters with the inline-6 Cummins' simplicity and B10-life reputation, best-in-class ride when unloaded (coil front/link rear), and competitive max tow; Ford leads on paper output and aluminum-body weight savings, GM on transmission refinement versus the pre-2025 68RFE.

ram-promaster.subtitle: Full-size front-wheel-drive commercial cargo van (Fiat Ducato-based), sold 2014–2026 in 1500/2500/3500 series; scope here 2015–2026. Single generation with a 2023 powertrain/interior refresh.

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: 2015–2016: 3.6L Pentastar V6 (ERB, NA, gasoline, standard) or 3.0L EcoDiesel I4 (Fiat F1C-family DOHC turbodiesel, 174 hp/295 lb-ft, optional — discontinued in the US after 2016). 2017–2022: 3.6L Pentastar V6 only (280 hp/260 lb-ft). 2023–2026: next-generation 3.6L Pentastar V6 (276 hp/250 lb-ft). ProMaster EV (battery-electric) added 2024+ — outside the 2015–2026 ICE scope but note for the site.

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: 2015–2016: 6-speed 62TE automatic (Pentastar) or 6-speed M40 single-clutch automated manual (EcoDiesel only). 2017–2022: 6-speed 62TE automatic. 2023–2026: TorqueFlite 9-speed automatic (948TE family) — note a 2026 class-action alleges the 2022–2023 9-speed calibration uses only 7 of 9 ratios (litigation pending, unproven).

horsepower.label: Horsepower
horsepower.text: 3.6L Pentastar: 280 hp (2015–2022); 276 hp (2023+, next-generation calibration). 3.0L EcoDiesel I4: 174 hp (2015–2016 only).

torque.label: Torque
torque.text: 3.6L Pentastar: 260 lb-ft (2015–2022); 250 lb-ft (2023+). 3.0L EcoDiesel: 295 lb-ft (2015–2016 only).

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: Front-wheel drive only — class-exclusive among full-size US vans (Transit and Sprinter are RWD/AWD); no AWD option. Unibody (body-integral) construction, transverse engine.

dimensions.label: Dimensions
dimensions.text: Wheelbases 118 / 136 / 159 in (159 in extended on 3500); overall length ~195–250 in by wheelbase/extension; roof heights: low/standard (~90 in), high (~99–101 in), and Super High Roof added 2023; cargo volume up to ~463 cu ft (pre-2023 high roof) and ~520 cu ft (2023+ Super High Roof); cargo width 75.6 in between walls (best-in-class claim); curb weight ~4,700–5,400 lb; max payload 4,680 lb; max tow 6,910 lb (2023 figures). Exact per-configuration numbers vary — confirm against the build sheet.

mpg.label: Fuel economy (MPG)
mpg.text: Not EPA-rated — ProMaster's GVWR (8,550–9,350 lb) places it in Class 2b/3, above the EPA fuel-economy labeling threshold; no official city/highway/combined figures exist. Do not publish MPG numbers for this van; real-world fleet reports (~14–18 mpg gas, low-20s diesel) are anecdotal.

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: Fleet-duty reputation is adequate: the Pentastar/62TE combination is long-serving and well understood, and FWD eliminates driveline complexity. The 2015–2016 EcoDiesel + M40 automated manual is the known weak pairing (see Known issues) and was dropped. Commercial vans are not rated by IIHS/Consumer Reports in the consumer sense; NHTSA recall counts per year Not verified — needs OEM source.

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: (1) M40 automated-manual (2015–2016 diesel): clutch actuator and shift-control faults, jerky low-speed behavior, expensive hydraulic unit replacements — the diesel was discontinued in the US after 2016. (2) 62TE: solenoid-pack and valve-body failures, harsh shifts at mileage; fluid discipline critical. (3) 948TE 9-speed (2023+): shift-quality complaints; June 2026 class action alleges false advertising (only 7 ratios used) — pending, not adjudicated. (4) Pentastar: rocker-arm/tick failures and oil-filter-housing cooler leaks (common across 3.6L applications). (5) Sliding-door track/latch wear and cargo-area corrosion are the common fleet complaints.

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: Pentastar: oil per indicator, max ~10,000 miles/12 months, 0W-20; spark plugs ~100,000 miles; coolant 10-year/150,000-mile OAT; timing chain (no belt). 62TE/948TE: factory "lifetime" fill claims exist but fleet specialists service fluid ~60,000 miles; EcoDiesel I4 (if encountered): oil per indicator, timing-belt engine — belt interval Not verified — needs OEM source. Brake fluid: no fixed published interval — Not verified — needs OEM source.

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: 0–60 ~8.1 s (2023 Pentastar estimate, dealer-published). No performance trims; configurations are vocational: Cargo Van, Window Van, Crew Van (2023+, 3-passenger second row), Cutaway/Chassis Cab (3500). Max payload 4,680 lb; max tow 6,910 lb.

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: This is an upfit platform, not a tuning platform: shelving/partitions (Adrian Steel, Ranger Design), ladder racks, HVAC/refrigeration conversions, wheelchair lifts, and the huge #vanlife camper-conversion scene (FWD low floor and nearly vertical walls make it the favorite US camper base after the Sprinter). Suspension helper kits and all-terrain tires are the only common mechanical mods.

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: The ProMaster is the third-generation Fiat Ducato (2006–present global platform) federalized for the US and built in Saltillo, Mexico. Its transverse FWD unibody layout yields the lowest load floor in the class and near-90° cargo walls. The 2023 refresh replaced the 62TE with the TorqueFlite 9-speed, revised the Pentastar (276 hp), added Uconnect 5, a third (Super High) roof option, and standard backup camera with available digital rearview mirror and 360° cameras; a BEV variant (Amazon launch customer) followed.

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: No performance community, but a massive camper-conversion following — ProMaster builds dominate budget vanlife because of the cheap, simple, FWD powertrain and square cargo box. The 2015–2016 diesel vans are avoided by converters due to the M40 gearbox.

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Rivals: Ford Transit (RWD/AWD, 3.5L V6 or 3.5L EcoBoost, 10-speed) and Mercedes-Benz Sprinter (RWD/AWD, diesel I4, higher price). The ProMaster undercuts both on price, has the widest/straightest cargo box and lowest step-in height, but lacks AWD, has lower max payload than a Transit 350, and its 6-speed (pre-2023) was a generation behind rivals' transmissions.

ram-promaster-city.subtitle: Compact front-wheel-drive commercial van (Fiat Doblò-based), 2015–2022 only — discontinued after 2022 with no direct US replacement (nameplate announced to return ~2027 on a Citroën Jumpy/Fiat Scudo basis). Single generation.

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: 2.4L Tigershark MultiAir2 I4 (ED6-family, NA, SOHC 16v with MultiAir electro-hydraulic intake valve actuation) — single engine for the entire run.

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: 9-speed 948TE (ZF 9HP48-family) automatic, transverse. Single transmission for the entire run.

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: Front-wheel drive only; no AWD ever offered. Unibody, transverse powertrain.

dimensions.label: Dimensions
dimensions.text: Wheelbase 122.4 in; length ~187.5 in; width ~72.1 in (body); height ~74 in; curb weight ~3,500 lb; cargo volume 131.7 cu ft; max payload 1,890 lb; max tow 2,000 lb. (Figures per manufacturer/press spec sheets; confirm per configuration.)

mpg.label: Fuel economy (MPG)
mpg.text: EPA-rated (unlike its big brother — ProMaster City is under the 8,500-lb GVWR threshold): 21 city / 28–29 highway / 24 combined, depending on model year and body (cargo vs wagon).

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: Mediocre-to-fair. The 948TE 9-speed is the defining trouble spot (shared with Jeep Cherokee/Renegade), and the 2.4L Tigershark has documented oil-consumption complaints in FCA-wide applications. NHTSA recall counts per year Not verified — needs OEM source. IIHS did not rate this van class; no meaningful CR sample.

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: (1) 948TE 9-speed: harsh/erratic shifting, shift flare, valve-body and torque-converter complaints; multiple software updates across FCA applications. (2) 2.4L Tigershark oil consumption (piston ring/PCV related) documented across Cherokee/Renegade/ProMaster City fleet use — check oil level at every service. (3) Sliding-door and rear barn-door hardware wear in delivery duty. (4) MultiAir actuator ("brick") failures on the 2.4 are documented FCA-wide — oil-quality and interval sensitive.

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: Oil per indicator, max ~10,000 miles/12 months (0W-20; MultiAir systems are oil-critical — many techs shorten to 5,000–7,500); spark plugs ~100,000 miles; coolant 10-year/150,000-mile OAT; 948TE fluid: "lifetime" per factory but 60,000-mile service is standard fleet practice; timing chain (no belt). Brake fluid: no fixed published interval — Not verified — needs OEM source.

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: 0–60 ~9–10 s (press estimates; exact figure Not verified — needs OEM/instrumented source). No performance trims: Tradesman cargo, SLT, and 5-passenger Wagon variants only. Capability: 1,890-lb payload, 2,000-lb tow.

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: Pure vocational upfit: shelving, partitions, roof racks, small camper/moto conversions. No meaningful tuning aftermarket for the 2.4/948TE in this application.

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: A rebadged second-generation Fiat Doblò cargo van built in Turkey (Tofaş) and federalized for the US from the 2015 model year. It was the first application of the ZF 9-speed (948TE) in a US commercial van and the only FCA compact van, slotting under the Ducato-based ProMaster. Ram discontinued it after 2022 as compact-van demand collapsed (Transit Connect and NV200 exited the US market in the same period).

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: Minimal enthusiast scene; appreciated by small fleets and urban couriers for car-like maneuverability and low running costs. The small DIY camper community likes it as a cheap, efficient mini-vanlife base.

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Rivals: Ford Transit Connect (2.0L/2.5L, also discontinued US 2023), Nissan NV200/Chevrolet City Express (discontinued), Mercedes Metris (larger). The ProMaster City offered the most power (178 hp) and payload (1,890 lb) of the compact set with competitive MPG, but the 948TE's behavior and the Tigershark's oil appetite trail the Transit Connect's powertrains for refinement and durability reputation.

ram-trx.subtitle: High-performance off-road variant of the 5th-generation (DT) Ram 1500 Crew Cab 5'7", model years 2021–2024 (incl. 2024 Final Edition). Supercharged "Hellcat" halo truck. (Announced to return as a 777-hp SRT model for 2027 — outside this pack's 2015–2026 scope.)

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: 6.2L supercharged HEMI V8 "Hellcat" (pushrod 16v, 2.4L twin-screw supercharger, dual-path induction with hood scoop) — single powertrain for the entire run.

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: 8-speed TorqueFlite 8HP95 high-torque automatic (ZF 8HP95 variant, the strongest 8HP used in a Stellantis truck); steering-wheel paddle shifters, launch control.

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: Full-time 4WD only — BorgWarner 48-13 active transfer case, Dana 60 rear axle with electronic locking differential.

dimensions.label: Dimensions
dimensions.text: Length 232.9 in (Crew Cab 5'7"); width 88.0 in (8 in wider than a standard 1500 via composite fender flares); height ~80.9 in; wheelbase 144.6 in; ground clearance 11.8 in; curb weight ~6,350 lb; approach/breakover/departure 30.2° / 21.9° / 23.5°; 35-in Goodyear Wrangler Territory AT tires on 18-in wheels (beadlock-capable optional).

mpg.label: Fuel economy (MPG)
mpg.text: EPA 10 city / 14 highway / 12 combined; 33-gallon fuel tank.

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: Too limited-volume and too new for meaningful CR/J.D. Power samples; the Hellcat engine family has a solid durability record in Dodge applications, but the TRX's desert-duty use means check suspension, frame, and cooling on used examples. NHTSA recall counts per year Not verified — needs OEM source. Premium fuel required.

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: (1) Supercharger idler/bearing noise and belt wear. (2) Bilstein Black Hawk e2 adaptive shock leaks/rebuilds on hard-used trucks. (3) High-rate drivetrain wear (axle shafts, CV boots) on jumped or tuned trucks. (4) Brake wear is accelerated at 6,350 lb curb weight; 15-in front rotors are consumables on track/desert use. (5) 8HP95 is robust but fluid services are commonly recommended at 30,000–60,000 miles under severe use — factory severe-service interval Not verified — needs OEM source.

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: Oil per indicator (0W-40 full synthetic, 8.3 qt Hellcat spec) — severe-use owners run 3,000–5,000 miles; spark plugs ~60,000 miles class (supercharged HEMI; exact TRX interval Not verified — needs OEM source); coolant 10-year/150,000-mile OAT; 8HP95 fluid severe service ~30,000–60,000 miles (flagged above); transfer case/differentials under severe schedule; supercharger belt inspection per schedule. Timing chain (no belt).

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: Factory: 0–60 mph 4.5 s, 0–100 10.5 s, quarter-mile 12.9 s @ 108 mph, top speed 118 mph (tire-limited). Car and Driver instrumented: 3.7 s 0–60, 12.3 s @ 110 mph — fastest gas pickup they had tested. Tow 8,100 lb; payload ~1,310 lb.

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: Hennessey Mammoth 900/1000 packages (up to 1,012 hp) are the signature TRX build; also pulley/tune packages (775–850 hp on stock blower), long-travel rear kits, 37-in tire conversions with re-gear (4.56+), and bed-mounted chase racks. Factory special editions: Launch Edition (2021), Sandblast, Lunar, Havoc, and the 2024 Final Edition (4,000 units).

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: The TRX is not a body-kit package: its frame rails are ~75% unique or up-gauged versus a standard DT, with forged aluminum front control arms, 2.6-in Bilstein Black Hawk e2 remote-reservoir adaptive dampers, 13+ in of wheel travel, a Dana 60 rear, and a functional hood scoop feeding a 29-liter airbox. The 8HP95, full-time transfer case, and 11.8-in clearance complete the Baja-runner brief.

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: Instant halo/cult truck — values held near or above MSRP for years, and the 2024 Final Edition sold out immediately. It is the benchmark against which the Ford Raptor R (720 hp) is measured, and the community treats low-mile examples as collectibles. Deep aftermarket (Hennessey, Mopar, whipple-style blower work, long-travel suspension).

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Direct rival: Ford F-150 Raptor R (5.2L supercharged V8, 720 hp/640 lb-ft, 10-speed) — the Raptor R is lighter and edges handling feel; the TRX counters with a stronger 8-speed, plusher ride, and the segment's best interior. Against the standard Raptor (450 hp) it is simply in another league. Its successor-in-spirit within Ram is the 540-hp Hurricane RHO, which is quicker than expected (4.6 s) but lacks the V8 character that defines the TRX's appeal.

ram-rebel.subtitle: Off-road trim level of the Ram 1500 (not a separate model). 2015–2018 (DS), 2019–2024 (DT; EcoDiesel added 2020), 2025–2026 (DT facelift, Hurricane-powered). A Rebel HD variant of the 2500 was added for 2023. Condensed section — all 15 fields kept.

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: 2015–2018: 3.6L Pentastar V6 (305 hp) or 5.7L HEMI V8 (395 hp). 2019–2024: 3.6L Pentastar eTorque (standard), 5.7L HEMI with optional eTorque, 3.0L EcoDiesel (260 hp, 2020–2023). 2025–2026: 3.0L Hurricane twin-turbo I6 standard-output (420 hp) becomes the Rebel's engine; 3.6L eTorque base availability varies by year — confirm per model year.

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: 8-speed TorqueFlite automatic throughout: 845RE (V6) / 8HP70-8HP75 (HEMI, EcoDiesel) / 8HP75 (Hurricane). No manual.

horsepower.label: Horsepower
horsepower.text: 305 hp (3.6L) / 395 hp (5.7L) / 260 hp (EcoDiesel) / 420 hp (Hurricane SO, 2025+).

torque.label: Torque
torque.text: 269 lb-ft (3.6L) / 410 lb-ft (5.7L) / 480 lb-ft (EcoDiesel) / 469 lb-ft (Hurricane SO).

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: 2015–2018 DS Rebel: 4x2 or 4x4. 2019+ DT Rebel: 4WD only (part-time with low range; electronic locking rear differential standard).

dimensions.label: Dimensions
dimensions.text: Shares the host 1500 body: DS crew cab 5'7" — 229 in length, 79.4 in width, ~140.5 in wheelbase, ~1-in factory lift/air-suspension ride height; DT crew cab 5'7" — 232.9 in length, 82.1 in width, 144.6 in wheelbase; 33-in all-terrain tires (LT275/70R18 class). Rebel HD (2500, 2023+): crew cab 6'4", 238.2 in class length.

mpg.label: Fuel economy (MPG)
mpg.text: Slightly below equivalent non-Rebel trims due to tires/lift: 2015–2018 HEMI 4x4 ~15/21/17 class; DT HEMI 4x4 ~15/21/17, EcoDiesel 4x4 21/29/24, V6 eTorque 4x4 19/24/21; 2025+ Hurricane Rebel ~17/23/19 class — exact Rebel-specific EPA figures by year: partially Not verified — needs OEM source (use fueleconomy.gov per configuration).

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: Same as host generation (see Ram 1500 section): HEMI lifter tick applies, EcoDiesel recalls apply (2020–2023 Rebels included), eTorque 48V faults apply. Air-suspension-equipped Rebels add compressor/valve-block failure points. NHTSA recall counts per year Not verified — needs OEM source.

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: Host-powertrain issues (HEMI lifter/cam, exhaust-manifold bolts; EcoDiesel EGR cooler recall; eTorque 48V battery). Rebel-specific: air suspension compressor failures (DS standard, DT optional), 33-in tire wear/alignment sensitivity, e-locker actuator faults reported on DT trucks.

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: Identical to the host Ram 1500 schedules (gas: oil ≤10,000 miles/12 months, plugs ~100,000, OAT coolant 10-yr/150,000, timing chains; ZF 8-speed fluid service ~60,000 recommended). Air-suspension trucks: inspect dryer/compressor and lines at service.

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: 0–60 ~6.4–6.5 s (HEMI DT 4x4 class); 2025+ Hurricane Rebel ~5.3–5.6 s class (SO estimates; exact Not verified — needs OEM/instrumented source). Not a speed truck: hardware is Bilstein dampers, skid plates, tow hooks, hill-descent control, 33-in AT tires, e-locker. Rebel HD (2023+): 6.4L HEMI or 370-hp Cummins 2500-based off-road trim.

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: Popular mild-build platform: leveling kits to clear 35s, cat-back exhausts, cold-air intakes, bed racks/rooftop tents; the 2025+ Hurricane SO responds well to tunes (Hurricane aftermarket is young but growing). Factory cousins: Warlock (cheaper), RHO (much faster, 540 hp).

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: The 2015 Rebel was the first factory off-road trim of the DS 1500, pairing standard 4-corner air suspension (raised ride height) with Bilstein monotubes and 33-in Toyo Open Country A/Ts. The 2019 DT Rebel moved to coil springs with Bilsteins and optional air suspension, adding a standard electronic rear locker; the 2025 facelift gave it the 420-hp Hurricane SO.

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: The Rebel is the volume off-road Ram — the "Raptor-lite" buyers choose for daily drivability and price. Strong used demand; the community generally prefers it over the TRX/RHO for actual trail use (narrower, lighter, cheaper to feed).

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Cross-shops: Ford F-150 Tremor and Chevrolet Silverado Trail Boss. The Rebel matches the Trail Boss on hardware, trails the Tremor on payload/tuning depth, but rides better than both (coil/air rear) and typically prices lower on the used market.

subaru-outback.subtitle: Midsize crossover wagon (SUV-styled from 2026), 2015–2019 (5th gen, BS) • 2020–2025 (6th gen, BT) • 2026 (7th gen)

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: 2015–2019 (BS): FB25 2.5L DOHC DI naturally aspirated flat-4 (175 hp); EZ36 3.6L DOHC naturally aspirated flat-6 (256 hp). 2020–2025 (BT): FB25 2.5L DI NA flat-4 (182 hp); FA24F 2.4L DI turbo flat-4 on XT/Wilderness (260 hp). 2026 (7th gen): FB25 2.5L NA flat-4 (180 hp); FA24 2.4L turbo flat-4 on XT/Wilderness (260 hp). All engines timing-chain driven — no timing belts.

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: Lineartronic CVT only, all years. TR580 chain-CVT behind the 2.5L; TR690 high-torque CVT behind the 3.6R (2015–2019) and 2.4 XT/Wilderness (2020+). No manual offered in this period.

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: Symmetrical full-time AWD standard on every trim, every year — no FWD variant sold in the US. Active torque vectoring standard from 2015. Dual-function X-Mode on Wilderness and most upper trims.

dimensions.label: Dimensions
dimensions.text: 2015–2019 (BS): L 189.6–189.9 in / W 72.4 in / H 66.1–66.5 in (with roof rails) / WB 108.1 in / curb 3,593–3,946 lb. 2020–2025 (BT): L 191.3–191.9 in / W 73.0 in / H 66.1–66.9 in (Wilderness 66.9) / WB 108.1 in / curb 3,634–3,946 lb. 2026 (7th gen): exact US dimensions **Not verified — needs OEM source** (Subaru press materials confirm a taller, boxier body; publish from media.subaru.com spec sheet).

mpg.label: Fuel economy (MPG)
mpg.text: 2015–2019 2.5i: 25 city / 33 hwy / 28 comb (EPA, per Edmunds spec listing); 3.6R: 20/27/22. 2020–2025 2.5: 26/33/29; 2.4 XT: 23/30/26 (2020–2022), 22/29/25 (2023–2025); Wilderness: 22/26/24. 2026: EPA figures **Not verified — needs OEM source** (check fueleconomy.gov 2026 listings).

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: BS generation: mixed — strong structure/safety (IIHS Top Safety Pick+ most years) but marred by the FB oil-consumption class action era and CVT complaints; Consumer Reports rated later BS years average. BT generation: average-to-above-average CR predicted reliability; NHTSA 5-star overall ratings. Recall counts vary by model year — pull by VIN at nhtsa.gov/recalls rather than quoting a generation total.

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: (1) FB25 excessive oil consumption, worst on 2015 production — class-action era (Yaeger/Cohen v. Subaru settlements; warranty extension to 8 yr/100k on covered VINs; TSB 02-157-14R). (2) Valve spring recall WTW-78 on early 2016 production — broken valve spring can kill the engine. (3) Lineartronic CVT shudder/judder — 2015 models covered by CVT warranty extension to 10 yr/100k (TSB 16-107-17R); 2019–2020 covered by TSB 16-155-25; select 2020s recalled under WRK-21. (4) Parasitic battery drain — In re Subaru Battery Drain class settlement, MY2015–2020 Outback (DCM/telematics). (5) Rear wheel bearing hum, typically 60–120k mi (shop pattern). (6) 2020+ 11.6-in Starlink screen freezes/connectivity complaints. (7) Denso low-pressure fuel pump recall/settlement covering certain 2018–2020 Outbacks. (8) Thermo-control-valve failure class action alleging overheating risk on 2019+ FB/FA engines. (9) Windshield stress-crack complaints/litigation on BS-era cars.

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: Oil/filter 0W-20 synthetic every 6,000 mi / 6 mo (FB/FA). CVT fluid: officially "inspect" — in practice drain-and-fill with genuine Subaru CVTF (TR580) or High-Torque CVTF (TR690) every 30–60k, especially if towing. Subaru Super Coolant (blue): first change 11 yr/137,500 mi, then 5.5 yr/68,750 mi. Spark plugs 60,000 mi. Timing chains — no belt service. Brake fluid every 30,000 mi / 30 mo. Front/rear diff gear oil inspect 30k.

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: 0–60: BS 2.5i ~9.1–9.3 s; 3.6R 7.1 s; BT 2.5 8.5–8.7 s; BT XT 6.1–6.3 s (instrumented-test compilations). Performance trims: Onyx Edition XT, Touring XT, and the off-road-focused Wilderness (2022+, 9.5-in clearance, geared CVT final drive, all-terrain tires). No STI variant.

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: Common community mods: 1–2 in lift kits (ADF, RalliTEK, LP Aventure), all-terrain tires on +1/+2 wheels, skid plates, roof racks/tents, cat-back exhausts on XT, and Cobb Accessport Stage 1/2 tunes on FA24F XT (large gains; CVT torque limits respected). Wilderness is effectively the factory "pre-modded" variant.

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: The BS (2015) was an evolution of the Legacy wagon on the pre-SGP architecture, swapping the EJ253 for the cleaner, more efficient FB25 and going CVT-only. The BT (2020) moved to the Subaru Global Platform — ~70% stiffer structure — dropped the EZ36 six for the Ascent-sourced FA24F turbo, and added the 11.6-in portrait infotainment and EyeSight 4.0.
