The 2026 seventh gen restyles the car into a conventional SUV silhouette, revises the 2.5 to 180 hp/178 lb-ft, and adopts the WRX's dual-pinion electric power-steering rack.

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: The Outback is the adventure-wagon default: huge owner forums (subaruoutback.org), deep off-road aftermarket, and a strong high-mileage culture. The 3.6R has a small cult following as the last six-cylinder; the XT is the tuner pick. Enthusiasts tolerate the CVT in exchange for space, clearance, and AWD.

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Rivals: Toyota RAV4, Honda CR-V/Passport, Mazda CX-50, Ford Edge/Bronco Sport. Outback wins on standard AWD, clearance, and wagon utility at a value price; it trails RAV4 Hybrid on fuel economy and, in BS years, on powertrain refinement/reliability history. Driving feel is soft and secure rather than sporty.

subaru-forester.subtitle: Compact SUV, 2015–2018 (4th gen, SJ) • 2019–2024 (5th gen, SK) • 2025–2026 (6th gen, SL, incl. Hybrid)

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: 2015–2018 (SJ): FB25 2.5L DOHC NA flat-4 (170 hp, port-injected); FA20F (a.k.a. FA20DIT) 2.0L DI turbo flat-4 in 2.0XT (250 hp). 2019–2024 (SK): FB25 2.5L DI NA flat-4 only (182 hp). 2025–2026 (SL): FB25 2.5L DI NA (180 hp); Forester Hybrid (on sale spring 2025 as a 2025 model, continues 2026): 2.5L Atkinson-cycle Boxer + Toyota-based series-parallel hybrid system, 194 hp combined.

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: SJ: 6-speed manual (base 2.5i, early years) or Lineartronic CVT — TR580 (2.5i), TR690 high-torque (2.0XT). SK/SL: CVT only (TR580 family; 7- or 8-speed manual mode on upper trims/Wilderness). Hybrid: e-CVT-style planetary hybrid transaxle integrated with mechanical AWD.

horsepower.label: Horsepower
horsepower.text: FB25 (SJ): 170 hp @ 5,800 rpm. FA20F XT: 250 hp @ 5,600 rpm. FB25 DI (SK): 182 hp @ 5,800 rpm. FB25 (SL): 180 hp. Hybrid: 194 hp combined (gas engine alone 162 hp).

torque.label: Torque
torque.text: FB25 (SJ): 174 lb-ft @ 4,100 rpm. FA20F XT: 258 lb-ft @ 2,000–5,200 rpm. FB25 DI (SK): 176 lb-ft @ 4,400 rpm. FB25 (SL): 178 lb-ft. Hybrid: traction-motor torque 199 lb-ft; combined system torque not published by Subaru — **Not verified — needs OEM source**.

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: Symmetrical full-time AWD standard on all trims, all years. Hybrid uses a mechanically linked AWD system (not e-AWD) per Subaru. X-Mode on Premium-and-up; dual-function X-Mode on Wilderness.

dimensions.label: Dimensions
dimensions.text: 2015–2018 (SJ): L 180.9–181.9 in / W 70.7 in / H 66.4–68.2 in / WB 103.9 in / curb 3,252–3,624 lb. 2019–2024 (SK): L 182.1 in / W 71.5 in / H 67.5 in / WB 105.1 in / curb 3,449–3,589 lb. 2025–2026 (SL): L ~183.3 in / W ~72.0 in / WB 105.1 in / curb ~3,500–3,750 lb — exact height/weight by trim **partially verified; confirm on media.subaru.com spec sheet**.

mpg.label: Fuel economy (MPG)
mpg.text: SJ 2.5i CVT: 24/32/27 (2015–2016), 26/32/28 (2017–2018); 2.5i 6MT: 22/29/24; 2.0XT: 23/28/25. SK 2.5: 26/33/29; SK Wilderness: 25/28/26. SL gas: 26/33/29 (base/Premium; upper trims on 19-in wheels ~25/32/28 — verify by trim at fueleconomy.gov). SL Hybrid: 35 city / 34 hwy / 35 comb (EPA, per Edmunds first-look), up to ~581 miles of range.

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: The SJ is regarded as durable but sits in the FB oil-consumption window for 2014–2015 builds; 2016–2018 improved. SK is one of Subaru's stronger reliability performers in Consumer Reports owner surveys (above average most years; CR named Forester a repeated Top Pick). IIHS Top Safety Pick+ across most of the range. Verify recall counts by VIN at nhtsa.gov/recalls.

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: (1) FB25 oil consumption — TSB 02-157-14R explicitly covers 2011–2014 Forester CVT and 2015 manual-transmission cars; class-action warranty extensions applied. (2) Lineartronic CVT — 2014–2015 SJ covered by the 10 yr/100k CVT warranty extension (TSB 16-107-17R). (3) Parasitic battery drain — class settlement covers MY2015–2020 Forester. (4) Denso low-pressure fuel pump recall/settlement — certain 2019–2020 SK. (5) Thermo-control-valve class action (2019+ FB25). (6) Spontaneous windshield-crack complaints on SJ/SK (litigation filed; widely documented). (7) Rear wheel bearing wear — recurring shop pattern on SJ.

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: Oil/filter 0W-20 every 6,000 mi / 6 mo (turbo XT: keep strictly on schedule). CVT fluid drain-and-fill every 30–60k with genuine Subaru CVTF (TR580) / High-Torque CVTF (TR690 on XT). Subaru Super Coolant first change 11 yr/137,500 mi. Spark plugs 60,000 mi (FA20F included). All engines timing-chain — no belt. Brake fluid every 30,000 mi / 30 mo. Hybrid: follow hybrid-system coolant/inverter checks in the SL schedule.

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: 0–60: SJ 2.5i ~8.8–9.0 s; SJ 2.0XT 6.3 s (the last turbo Forester and the enthusiast pick); SK 2.5 ~8.3 s; SL gas ~8.3–8.6 s; SL Hybrid ~8.1 s (MotorWeek instrumented). Performance trims: 2.0XT (SJ), Sport (SK/SL, appearance/chassis tune), Wilderness (2022+, 9.2-in clearance, 3,500-lb tow rating).

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: Lift kits (1–2 in), all-terrain tires, skid plates and rally armor, roof baskets, LED pods; SJ XT responds well to Accessport Stage 1+ tunes, downpipes, and intercooler upgrades. The SJ XT has the strongest tuner support of any non-WRX Subaru SUV; SK/SL owners mostly modify for overlanding rather than power.

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: The SJ (2014–2018) introduced direct injection to the Forester line via the FA20DIT turbo and paired the CVT with X-Mode. The SK (2019) moved to the Subaru Global Platform with a major rigidity/crash-structure gain, standardized EyeSight, and dropped both the turbo and the manual. The SL (2025) is an SGP evolution with a stiffer body, WRX-derived dual-pinion steering, and Subaru's first Toyota-collaboration series-parallel hybrid that keeps a mechanical driveshaft to the rear axle.

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: The SJ 2.0XT is a cult "sleeper" — WRX-adjacent power in a family SUV — with full aftermarket support. Later Foresters are viewed as appliance-grade but honest; the Wilderness earns genuine respect in the overlanding community. Owner communities (subaruforester.org) are large and technically detailed.

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Rivals: Toyota RAV4, Honda CR-V, Mazda CX-5/CX-50, Hyundai Tucson. Forester counters with standard AWD, best-in-class visibility, and (from 2025) a credible hybrid; it historically trailed RAV4/CR-V on powertrain polish, and the CVT is the long-term durability watch item versus Toyota's conventional automatics.

subaru-crosstrek.subtitle: Subcompact crossover, 2015–2017 (1st gen; "XV Crosstrek" through 2015, renamed Crosstrek for 2016) • 2018–2023 (2nd gen) • 2024–2026 (3rd gen)

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: 2015–2017: FB20 2.0L DOHC NA flat-4, port-injected (148 hp); 2014–2016 Crosstrek Hybrid: FB20 + 13-hp integrated motor (~160 hp combined). 2018–2023: FB20 2.0L DI (152 hp); 2021–2023 Sport/Limited add FB25 2.5L DI (182 hp); 2019–2023 Crosstrek Hybrid = plug-in (FB20-based Toyota THS-II PHEV, 148 hp total). 2024–2026: FB20 152 hp (2024–2025 base/Premium); FB25 182 hp (Sport/Limited/Wilderness); for 2026 all gas trims move to a 180-hp 2.5L, and a new Crosstrek Hybrid (series-parallel, 194 hp) joins.

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: 2015–2017: 5-speed manual or Lineartronic CVT (TR580). 2018–2023: 6-speed manual (2.0 base/Premium) or CVT; 2.5 CVT-only. 2024+: CVT only (8-speed manual mode w/ paddles on upper trims). PHEV and 2026 Hybrid: planetary e-CVT hybrid drive.

horsepower.label: Horsepower
horsepower.text: FB20 PFI (2015–2017): 148 hp @ 6,200 rpm. FB20 DI (2018+): 152 hp @ 6,000 rpm. FB25 (2021+): 182 hp @ 5,800 rpm. PHEV (2019–2023): 148 hp combined (engine alone 137 hp @ 5,600). 2026 gas: 180 hp; 2026 Hybrid: 194 hp combined.

torque.label: Torque
torque.text: FB20 (all): 145 lb-ft @ 4,200 rpm (PFI) / @ 4,000 rpm (DI). FB25: 176 lb-ft @ 4,400 rpm (2021–2023), 178 lb-ft (2024+). PHEV: 134 lb-ft engine + motor assist. 2026 Hybrid: traction-motor 199 lb-ft; combined system torque **Not verified — needs OEM source**.

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: Symmetrical full-time AWD standard on every trim and every powertrain, including both hybrids. X-Mode on most trims; dual-function X-Mode on Wilderness.

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: Consistently Subaru's reliability leader — Consumer Reports named Crosstrek a 2026 Top Pick (subcompact SUV) and owner-survey reliability has run above average for most of the line. IIHS Top Safety Pick+ in most years. NHTSA 5-star ratings on recent gens. Verify recall counts by VIN.

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: (1) FB20 oil consumption — TSB 02-157-14R covers 2012–2013 CVT and 2014–2015 manual cars. (2) Valve-spring recall on early FB/FA production (2013-era builds; verify by VIN — exact campaign scope **needs NHTSA VIN check**). (3) CVT warranty extension 10 yr/100k for 2013–2015 CVT cars incl. 2014–15 Hybrid (TSB 16-107-17R). (4) Starlink infotainment class settlement covering MY2018 models. (5) Denso low-pressure fuel pump recall/settlement — certain 2018–2020 cars. (6) PHEV charge-cable/harness recall (fire risk while charging) on 2019–2022 Crosstrek Hybrid — verify by VIN. (7) Rear wheel bearing wear; windshield-crack complaints on 2nd/3rd gen.

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: Oil/filter 0W-20 every 6,000 mi / 6 mo. CVT drain-and-fill 30–60k with genuine Subaru CVTF. Spark plugs 60,000 mi. Subaru Super Coolant first change 11 yr/137,500 mi. Timing chains — no belt. Brake fluid every 30,000 mi / 30 mo. PHEV/Hybrid: add hybrid coolant and battery-system inspections per schedule; keep the 12V battery healthy (DCM drain reports).

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: 0–60: 2015 2.0 ~10.3 s; 2018 2.0 ~9.0–9.7 s; 2021–2023 2.5 ~7.6–8.2 s; 2024 2.5 ~8.1–8.6 s (MotorWeek 8.6); 2026 Hybrid ~8.0 s expected (not yet independently instrumented at time of writing). "Performance" trims: Sport (2.5, chassis/appearance) and Wilderness (2024+, 9.3-in clearance, 3,500-lb tow). No turbo/STI variant.

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: The Crosstrek is the overlanding starter kit: 1.5–2 in lifts, all-terrain tires (popular 235/75R15 conversions on 1st gen), skid plates, light bars, roof tents. Power mods are rare (little to gain on the FB20); the 2.5 gets mild intake/exhaust work. Wilderness is the factory-built version of the most common build.

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: Gen 1 was an Impreza 5-door with raised suspension, cladding, and 8.7-in clearance — the formula that created the segment. Gen 2 (2018) moved to the Subaru Global Platform with direct injection, far better crash structure, and (briefly) the only PHEV in Subaru history. Gen 3 (2024) is an SGP refresh with a stiffer body, WRX-derived dual-pinion steering, and — for 2026 — the Toyota-collaboration series-parallel hybrid with mechanical AWD.

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: Hugely popular with the outdoor crowd; "battlewagon" builds are a social-media staple. Purists prize the manual (gone after 2023). Tuner support is modest — owners spend on armor and tires, not horsepower. The PHEV is a curiosity valued for HOV access, not performance.

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Rivals: Honda HR-V, Toyota Corolla Cross, Mazda CX-30, Hyundai Kona, Jeep Renegade. Crosstrek's standard AWD, 8.7-in clearance, and top-tier reliability scores beat most rivals for capability-per-dollar; rivals' turbo options (CX-30, Kona) are quicker, and the Corolla Cross Hybrid undercuts it on price.

subaru-impreza.subtitle: Compact sedan (through 2023) and 5-door hatchback (hatch-only from 2024), 2015–2016 (4th gen, GJ/GP) • 2017–2023 (5th gen, GK/GT) • 2024–2026 (6th gen, GU)

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: 2015–2016: FB20 2.0L DOHC NA flat-4, port-injected (148 hp). 2017–2023: FB20 2.0L DI (152 hp). 2024–2026: FB20 152 hp (Base/Sport); FB25 2.5L DI 182 hp (RS). All naturally aspirated, timing-chain driven. (WRX/STI are separate model lines — out of scope.)

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: 2015–2016: 5-speed manual or Lineartronic CVT (TR580). 2017–2023: 5-speed manual (base, through ~2019) or CVT with 7-speed manual mode. 2024+: CVT only — 8-speed manual mode with paddles on Sport/RS.

torque.label: Torque
torque.text: FB20 PFI: 145 lb-ft @ 4,200 rpm. FB20 DI: 145 lb-ft @ 4,000 rpm. FB25 RS: 178 lb-ft @ 4,400 rpm.

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: Symmetrical full-time AWD standard on every trim, every year — the cheapest standard-AWD car sold in the US for much of this period. Active torque vectoring on Sport/RS trims (2024+ RS standard).

dimensions.label: Dimensions
dimensions.text: 2015–2016: sedan L ~180.3–180.5 in, hatch L 173.8 in / W 68.5 in / H 57.3 in / WB 104.1 in / curb ~2,910–3,075 lb. 2017–2023: sedan L 182.1 in, hatch L 175.6 in / W 70.0 in / H 57.3 in / WB 105.1 in / curb ~2,976–3,131 lb. 2024–2026 (hatch only): L 176.2 in / W 70.1 in / H 58.3 in / WB 105.1 in / curb ~3,122–3,250 lb (by trim; RS heaviest).

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: The 5th gen was the first Subaru Global Platform car and earned strong crash scores (IIHS Top Safety Pick+ most years; NHTSA 5-star on 2024 per published ratings). Consumer Reports predicted reliability: average to above average for GK/GT, with 2017–2018 dragged down by infotainment/electrical complaints. Early FB20 cars (through 2015) fall in the oil-consumption TSB window. Verify recall counts by VIN.

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: (1) FB20 oil consumption — TSB 02-157-14R covers 2012–2013 CVT and 2014–2015 manual cars. (2) Valve-spring recall on early FB production (2012–2014-era builds; exact scope **needs NHTSA VIN check**). (3) CVT warranty extension 10 yr/100k on 2012–2015 CVT cars (TSB 16-107-17R). (4) Starlink infotainment class settlement — MY2017–2018 Impreza explicitly covered. (5) Denso low-pressure fuel pump recall/settlement — certain 2018–2020 Imprezas. (6) PCV-valve recall (2019 campaign) on certain 2017–2018 builds — verify by VIN. (7) Occasional rear wheel bearings and windshield-crack complaints, lower incidence than the SUVs.

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: Oil/filter 0W-20 every 6,000 mi / 6 mo. CVT drain-and-fill 30–60k with genuine Subaru CVTF. Spark plugs 60,000 mi. Subaru Super Coolant first change 11 yr/137,500 mi. Timing chains — no belt. Brake fluid every 30,000 mi / 30 mo. Manual-transmission and rear-diff gear oil inspect/replace 30k–60k.

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: 0–60: 2015–2016 ~9.5 s; 2017–2023 CVT ~9.0–9.6 s (MotorWeek 9.6); 2024 RS ~7.8–8.0 s (press estimates; **no instrumented test verified — treat as approximate**). Sport trims add suspension tuning, 18-in wheels, active torque vectoring; the 2024+ RS is the warm variant (2.5L, RS-specific tuning). No factory turbo in this period.

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: Enthusiast mods focus on chassis: sway bars, coilovers, strut braces, WRX wheel/tire swaps (5x100 on pre-2024, 5x114.3 on 2024+ RS), and cat-back exhausts. Engine tuning is limited (no factory turbo; NA tunes add little). Common suspension and appearance parts interchange with Crosstrek of the same generation.

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: The 4th gen (2012–2016) introduced the FB-series engine family — chain-driven, long-stroke, efficiency-focused. The 5th gen (2017) debuted the Subaru Global Platform itself: 70%+ stiffer body, direct injection, first US-built Impreza (Lafayette, Indiana), and a 50% reduction in body roll per Subaru. The 6th gen (2024) drops the sedan, adds the 2.5L RS, stiffens the shell further, and brings the 11.6-in infotainment and dual-pinion steering down from the larger models.

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: The Impreza lives in the WRX's shadow but is respected as the cheapest durable AWD platform; 5-door manuals are sought after as winter beaters and rally-cross starters. The RS nameplate (revived 2024, echoing the 1998–2001 2.5 RS) carries real nostalgia. Tuner support for non-turbo cars is thin — most "builds" are suspension and wheels.

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Rivals: Honda Civic, Toyota Corolla, Mazda3, Hyundai Elantra. The Impreza's card is standard AWD plus top safety scores at a compact price; the Civic and Mazda3 drive sweeter and their base engines are livelier than the FB20, while the RS closes the gap. Resale and AWD capability keep it strong in snow-belt markets like Colorado.

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: FA24F 2.4L DOHC DI twin-scroll-turbo flat-4 (only engine offered), timing-chain driven. The Ascent was the first application of the FA24F.

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: Lineartronic high-torque CVT (HT-TR690 Gen 2, which debuted with the 2019 Ascent) with 8-speed manual mode and paddle shifters. No manual transmission.

horsepower.label: Horsepower
horsepower.text: 260 hp @ 5,600 rpm (all years, all trims).

torque.label: Torque
torque.text: 277 lb-ft @ 2,000–4,800 rpm (all years, all trims).

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: Symmetrical full-time AWD standard on every trim; X-Mode with hill-descent control. Up to 5,000-lb tow rating (Subaru-published) with standard transmission oil cooler on most trims.

dimensions.label: Dimensions
dimensions.text: L 196.8 in / W 76.0 in / H 71.6 in / WB 113.8 in / curb ~4,300–4,600 lb by trim (**exact curb range partially verified — confirm by trim on media.subaru.com spec sheet**).

mpg.label: Fuel economy (MPG)
mpg.text: Base/Premium: 21 city / 27 hwy / 23 comb; Limited/Touring (heavier, 20-in wheels): 20/26/22 — EPA sticker split confirmed by owner/forum sticker data and NRCan figures (11.6/9.0 L/100km ≈ 20/26).

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: Roughest launch of the modern Subaru line — CarComplaints lists 8 NHTSA recalls and 700+ TSBs for the 2019 model year (electrical, infotainment, powertrain software); quality improved markedly from 2021. Consumer Reports scored 2019–2020 below average, later years average. Verify recalls by VIN.

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: (1) 2019 teething problems: infotainment/electrical glitches, multiple early recalls and software campaigns (CarComplaints 2019 TSB/recall counts). (2) Parasitic battery drain — class settlement explicitly covers MY2019–2020 Ascent (DCM/telematics). (3) Denso low-pressure fuel pump recall/settlement — certain 2019–2020 Ascents. (4) Thermo-control-valve failure class action alleging overheating risk on 2019+ FA24 applications. (5) TR690 CVT shudder/valve-body complaints — service with genuine Subaru High-Torque CVTF only; check VIN for CVT campaigns. (6) Windshield stress-crack and rear wheel bearing reports, lower incidence than Outback. (7) Expected long-term DI carbon buildup on intake valves (FA24F is direct-injection only).

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: Oil/filter 0W-20 synthetic every 6,000 mi / 6 mo (turbo + towing: treat 6k as the maximum, not the target). CVT drain-and-fill every 30–60k with genuine Subaru High-Torque CVTF (TR690) — critical on a vehicle that tows. Spark plugs 60,000 mi. Subaru Super Coolant first change 11 yr/137,500 mi. Timing chain — no belt. Brake fluid every 30,000 mi / 30 mo. Front/rear diff oil inspect 30k.

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: 0–60: published instrumented tests cluster around 6.7–7.0 s (**specific figure Not verified in this session — verify against C&D/MotorTrend test before publishing**). No performance trim; Onyx Edition (2023+) is an appearance/trim package, Touring tops the range.

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: Modest scene: mild lifts (1–1.5 in), all-terrain tires, skid plates, roof tents — the Ascent is the overlanding family's answer to a minivan. Cobb Accessport Stage 1 tunes exist for the FA24F but gains are modest and CVT torque capacity is the limiting factor. Most owners mod for towing (brake controllers, weight-distribution hitches) rather than speed.

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: Subaru's largest vehicle ever at launch, built on a stretched Subaru Global Platform at Lafayette, Indiana, replacing the Tribeca. It debuted both the FA24F engine and the Gen-2 high-torque TR690 needed to handle 277 lb-ft and a 5,000-lb tow rating through a CVT. Standard EyeSight and 8-passenger (or 7 with captain's chairs) seating across the range.

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: Not an enthusiast car and doesn't pretend to be — the community is pragmatic owners who tow pop-ups and haul kids. Appreciated as "the big Forester": same mechanical honesty, same service patterns. Tuner support is minimal by design; the overlanding aftermarket (lifts, armor, racks) is where the energy is.

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Rivals: Honda Pilot, Toyota Highlander, Ford Explorer, Kia Telluride/Hyundai Palisade. Ascent undercuts most on price with standard AWD and strong safety scores; it trails Telluride/Palisade on third-row space and cabin polish, and its 2019–2020 reliability history lags the Highlander. For snow-belt families it's often the value pick.

subaru-wrx.subtitle: Compact performance sedan, 2015–2021 (VA, incl. WRX STI through 2021) • 2022–2026 (VB; no STI variant offered)

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: 2015–2021 WRX: FA20F (FA20DIT) 2.0L DI turbo flat-4 (268 hp). 2015–2021 WRX STI: EJ257 2.5L port-injected turbo flat-4 (305 hp 2015–2018; 310 hp 2019–2021; limited S209 341 hp, 2019). 2022–2026: FA24F 2.4L DI twin-scroll-turbo flat-4 (271 hp). All timing-chain driven (EJ257 uses a timing **belt** — 105k service).

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: VA WRX: 6-speed manual or Sport Lineartronic CVT (high-torque TR690-based, 8-speed manual mode). VA STI: 6-speed manual only, with Driver-Controlled Center Differential (DCCD). VB: 6-speed manual or Subaru Performance Transmission (SPT) CVT — Subaru claims 30% faster upshifts / 50% faster downshifts vs. the VA CVT.

horsepower.label: Horsepower
horsepower.text: FA20F WRX: 268 hp @ 5,600 rpm. EJ257 STI: 305 hp @ 6,000 rpm (310 hp from 2019; S209 341 hp). FA24F VB: 271 hp @ 5,600 rpm.

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: Symmetrical AWD, all trims. VA 6MT: viscous-coupling center diff (50:50 nominal); VA CVT: electronically managed multi-plate transfer (rear-biased 45:55 nominal); STI: DCCD driver-adjustable center diff. VB retains both layouts. Active torque vectoring standard.

mpg.label: Fuel economy (MPG)
mpg.text: Per fueleconomy.gov: 2015 WRX 6MT 21/28/24; WRX CVT 19/25/21; STI (2.5L) 17/23/19. 2022 WRX 6MT 19/26/22; CVT 19/25/21. Premium fuel required/recommended on all turbo models.

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: Highly owner-dependent — modified examples dominate the failure statistics. FA20F cars are mechanically sound when stock and maintained; the EJ257 STI carries the known rod-bearing/ringland risk profile, amplified by tuning. Consumer Reports scored the VA below average much of its run; the VB is too new for long-term data. Verify recalls by VIN.

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: (1) EJ257 rod bearing wear and piston ringland failure — the defining STI shop issue, strongly correlated with tuning, detonation, and neglected oil (repair-shop pattern documentation). (2) FA20DIT intake-valve carbon buildup (direct-injection only, no port wash). (3) VA 6MT clutch judder and throw-out-bearing wear; factory rev-hang complaints. (4) Denso low-pressure fuel pump recall/settlement — certain 2019–2020 WRX. (5) Parasitic battery drain — class settlement explicitly covers MY2015–2020 WRX. (6) VB 11.6-in Starlink infotainment freezes/connectivity complaints. (7) VA CVT heat sensitivity under track use — cooler strongly recommended for hard use.

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: VA WRX/STI: 5W-30 full synthetic every 6,000 mi (3–5k for tuned/track cars); VB: 0W-20 per current schedule. Spark plugs 60,000 mi (sooner on tuned cars). STI EJ257: timing **belt** service at 105,000 mi — budget for it. 6MT and rear-diff gear oil every 30k (STI front diff shares trans oil; DCCD fluid checks). Brake fluid every 30k / 30 mo; STI Brembos need proper pad/rotor service, not generic parts. CVT fluid: genuine Subaru High-Torque CVTF, 30–60k.

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: 0–60: VA WRX 6MT ~5.4–5.6 s; VA STI ~5.1–5.2 s (Series.White instrumented 5.2); VB 6MT ~5.4–5.6 s (press-test cluster — **treat as approximate**). Trims: VA — base/Premium/Limited, Series.Gray, Series.White; STI base/Limited, Type RA (2018), S209 (2019, 209 units). VB — base/Premium/Limited/GT (CVT-only, adaptive dampers), TR (2024), tS (2025, STI-tuned suspension/Recaros; no power increase, no DCCD).

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: One of the deepest tuning ecosystems in the industry: Cobb Accessport staged tunes (VB Stage 1 ~280–300 hp; Stage 1+ flex-fuel/E85 ~330–350 hp per tuner-published figures), Ecutek, J-pipes/downpipes, top-mount/front-mount intercoolers, coilovers, sway bars, big-brake kits. EJ257 STI builds routinely go built-block (IAG and peers) with rotated turbo kits. Bolt pattern changed from 5x100 (VA) to 5x114.3 (VB).

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: The VA (2015) split the WRX from the Impreza as a standalone model with the new direct-injected FA20DIT and a stiffer shell; the STI kept the proven EJ257/DCCD formula. The VB (2022) moved to the Subaru Global Platform with the FA24F (electronic wastegate, broader torque), introduced the SPT CVT and GT trim with adaptive dampers, and — controversially — clad the fenders in unpainted composite. STI was discontinued after 2021; Subaru has signaled any future STI would be electrified.

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: This is Subaru's enthusiast core: NASIOC-era forum culture, rally heritage, and a tuner market that supports everything from $500 Accessport maps to six-figure time-attack builds. The manual take rate remains high, and VB values are watched closely since the STI's death made every VA STI — especially Type RA and S209 — a collectible. tS and TR trims are factory answers to the "no STI" gap.

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Rivals: VW Golf GTI/Golf R, Honda Civic Si/Type R, Hyundai Elantra N, Toyota GR Corolla. The WRX's standard AWD and price undercut the Golf R and GR Corolla; the Elantra N and Type R out-handle it on track with sharper steering and better gearboxes; the GTI beats it on interior refinement. It remains the all-weather performance-sedan value pick.

subaru-legacy.subtitle: Midsize sedan, 2015–2019 (6th gen, BN) • 2020–2025 (7th gen, BW) — production ended after the 2025 model year (Subaru announcement, April 2024)

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: 2015–2019 (BN): FB25 2.5L DI NA flat-4 (175 hp); EZ36 3.6L NA flat-6 (256 hp). 2020–2025 (BW): FB25 2.5L DI NA (182 hp); FA24F 2.4L DI turbo (260 hp, Sport/Touring XT). All timing-chain driven.

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: Lineartronic CVT only, all years: TR580 behind the 2.5L; TR690 high-torque behind the 3.6R and XT. No manual in this period.

torque.label: Torque
torque.text: FB25 (BN): 174 lb-ft @ 4,000 rpm. EZ36: 247 lb-ft @ 4,400 rpm. FB25 DI (BW): 176 lb-ft @ 4,400 rpm. FA24F XT: 277 lb-ft @ 2,000–4,800 rpm.

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: Symmetrical full-time AWD standard on every trim, every year — the only midsize sedan in the US with standard AWD for most of this run. Active torque vectoring standard.

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: Safety standout — IIHS Top Safety Pick for 18 consecutive years (Subaru). Mechanically mirrors the Outback: BN 2.5 cars are durable post-oil-consumption-era; U.S. News noted below-class predicted reliability scores for early BW years (2020–2021) driven by infotainment/electrical complaints. Verify recalls by VIN.

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: (1) CVT warranty extension to 10 yr/100k covers 2015 Legacy CVTs (TSB 16-107-17R). (2) Valve spring recall WTW-78 on early 2016 production (shared with Outback; verify by VIN). (3) Parasitic battery drain — class settlement explicitly covers MY2015–2020 Legacy. (4) Denso low-pressure fuel pump recall/settlement — certain 2018–2020 Legacys. (5) 2020+ 11.6-in Starlink screen freezes/connectivity complaints. (6) Thermo-control-valve class action on 2019+ FB/FA applications. (7) Windshield stress-crack and rear wheel bearing reports (shared-platform patterns). Note: the FB oil-consumption TSB 02-157-14R covered 2013–2014 Legacys — 2015+ builds were largely past it.

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: Oil/filter 0W-20 synthetic every 6,000 mi / 6 mo. CVT drain-and-fill every 30–60k — genuine Subaru CVTF (TR580) or High-Torque CVTF (TR690). Spark plugs 60,000 mi. Subaru Super Coolant first change 11 yr/137,500 mi. Timing chains — no belt. Brake fluid every 30,000 mi / 30 mo. EZ36: same schedule; watch chain-tensioner rattle history on high-mile cars.

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: 0–60: BN 2.5i ~9.1–9.5 s; 3.6R ~7.1 s; BW 2.5 ~8.0–8.3 s; BW Touring XT 6.1 s (instrumented compilation). "Performance" trims: 3.6R Limited (BN) and Sport / Touring XT (BW, 2020–2025) — warm rather than sporty.

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: Minimal scene. Owners do wheels, mild suspension, and cosmetic work; the Legacy shares bones with the Outback, so some chassis parts cross over. The old Legacy GT (2005–2009) tuner crowd moved to the WRX long ago — an XT tune via Cobb Accessport exists but few bother.

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: The BN (2015) paired the then-new FB25 with standard EyeSight availability and a much-improved cabin. The BW (2020) moved to the Subaru Global Platform — stiffer structure, better crash performance — and added the FA24F XT, 11.6-in portrait infotainment, and DriverFocus attention monitoring. All US Legacys were built at Subaru of Indiana Automotive; Subaru announced in April 2024 that production ends after the 2025 model year, closing a 35-year, 1.3-million-unit US run as the brand pivots to SUVs and EVs.

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: The Legacy has a loyal, quiet owner base rather than an enthusiast scene — repeat buyers who want an AWD sedan and don't want an SUV. Legacy GT (2005–2009) nostalgia drives most enthusiast conversation; the XT is appreciated as a sleeper. Expect a small values bump on clean final-year cars as Subaru's last sedan.

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Rivals: Toyota Camry, Honda Accord, Nissan Altima, Hyundai Sonata, Kia K5. The Legacy's sole-USP was standard AWD at a base price; rivals offered hybrids and sharper driving dynamics, and the segment's collapse sealed its fate. On the used market it's a high-value, high-safety AWD sedan — relevant inventory for a Colorado shop.

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: Rear permanent-magnet synchronous reluctance drive unit (RWD cars; Tesla drive-unit family codes 3D1/3D3/3D5/3D6); Dual Motor cars add a front AC induction motor. 2017–2023: Long Range RWD single rear motor (~192–211 kW per EV certification data); Dual Motor AWD; Performance dual motor. 2024+ Highland: EPA certification filings list motor ratings of 85 kW front / 191 kW rear (Long Range AWD), 88 kW front / 208 kW rear (Long Range AWD-E, Panasonic pack), 98 kW front / 282 kW rear (Performance "4DU" rear drive unit). Batteries: ~50–55 kWh Standard Range (LFP chemistry in most RWD cars from ~2021), ~75–82 kWh Long Range/Performance (2170-format cells).

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: Single-speed fixed-ratio reduction gear, ~9.0:1 final drive, integrated into each drive unit; no multi-gear transmission, no scheduled drive-unit fluid service.

horsepower.label: Horsepower
horsepower.text: 2017–2023 Long Range RWD ≈271 hp (some sources list 258 hp; varies by model year/software); Dual Motor AWD ≈346 hp; Performance 450 hp (2018–2020) → 455 hp (2021–2023). 2024+ Highland: RWD ≈271 hp; Long Range AWD — not officially published (EPA-filed motors total ~370 hp before battery limits); Performance 510 hp.

torque.label: Torque
torque.text: Long Range RWD ≈298 lb-ft; Dual Motor AWD ≈376 lb-ft; 2018–2023 Performance 471 lb-ft; 2024+ Highland RWD 258 lb-ft (350 Nm); 2024+ Performance ≈547 lb-ft (Tesla-claimed +16% peak torque vs. the outgoing 471 lb-ft unit).

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: RWD standard (single rear motor); AWD via dual-motor Long Range and Performance trims, all years.

dimensions.label: Dimensions
dimensions.text: 2017–2023: length 184.8 in, width 72.8 in (82.2 in w/ mirrors), height 56.8–56.9 in, wheelbase 113.2 in; curb weight up to 4,072 lb (Performance). 2024+ Highland: length 185.8 in, width 72.8 in, height 56.7–56.8 in, wheelbase 113.2 in; curb ≈3,880 lb (RWD) to ≈4,030–4,080 lb (Long Range AWD).

mpg.label: Fuel economy (MPG)
mpg.text: EPA MPGe (city/hwy/combined, fueleconomy.gov): 2017 Long Range 131/120/126. 2022: RWD 138/126/132; Long Range AWD 134/126/131; Performance 118/107/113. 2024: RWD 140/125/132; Long Range RWD 145/128/137; Long Range AWD 137/124/130; Performance 117/107/112. 2025: Long Range RWD 145/128/137; Long Range AWD 133/122/128; Performance 113/104/109.

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: Middling but improving. J.D. Power 2025-model consumer score 84/100; Tesla brand moved from near-last to roughly industry average in the J.D. Power 2025 U.S. Vehicle Dependability Study (202 PP100 industry avg). Recall exposure is high in count but mostly software/OTA: notable physical recalls include 21V00D000 (rear camera harness damaged by trunk lid, ~356k cars, 2017–2020), 23V235000 (front lateral link fasteners), and 22V798000 (rear center seat belt anchor). Largest campaigns (23V838000 Autosteer misuse ~2.03M, 24V051000 warning-light font ~2.19M) were OTA fixes.

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: Front upper control arm ball joints ingest water and creak/squeak loudly — near-universal wear item on early cars (documented by owner communities and parts suppliers). 2019–2021 Fremont build quality: panel misalignment, thin/chip-prone paint (rear door lower edges, sills). Heat pump/octovalve and A/C compressor failures on 2021–2023 cars (recall 22V050000 covered a heat-pump valve/defrost software fault; hardware failures widely documented). Rear camera cable harness failure in trunk lid (recall 21V00D000). Center screen yellow border and MCU failures on pre-refresh cars. NHTSA investigated 2023 power-steering loss (~334k cars, 2,388 complaints) — resolved via recall 25V092000 (OTA, ~376k cars). 12V→16V low-voltage battery and coolant-pump ("VCFRONT") faults reported on higher-mileage cars.

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: No engine oil, spark plugs, timing belt/chain, or transmission service. Tesla schedule: tire rotation every 6,250 miles (or if tread-depth difference ≥2/32 in); brake fluid health check every 4 years (replace if contaminated; do not top off); cabin air filter every 2 years; HEPA filter (where fitted) every 3 years; A/C desiccant bag every 4 years; clean and lubricate brake calipers every 12,500 miles in road-salt regions (regen braking leaves friction brakes underused — caliper slide seizure is common).

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: 0–60 mph: Long Range RWD ~5.1 s; Standard Range Plus ~5.3 s; Dual Motor AWD ~4.4 s; 2018–2023 Performance 3.1 s (with rollout subtracted); 2024+ Highland Long Range AWD ~4.2 s; 2024+ Performance 2.9 s (Tesla claim). Performance trims add Track Mode, upgraded brakes; 2024+ Performance adds adaptive dampers, staggered wheels, and the 510-hp "4DU" rear unit. Factory "Acceleration Boost" software option (~50 hp) was sold for Dual Motor cars.

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: Chassis mods dominate: coilovers, springs, sway bars, camber/toe arms (Mountain Pass Performance, Unplugged Performance, KW, H&R), lightweight 18-in wheels, big-brake kits for track use. Power "tunes" are software-only: Tesla's official Acceleration Boost and third-party Ingenext Ghost/Boost modules (warranty/Supercharging risk, per owner forums). No mechanical powertrain tuning exists — motors and inverters are sealed.

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: Built on Tesla's dedicated EV platform shared with Model Y (Tesla states ~75% parts commonality); mixed steel/aluminum body with the battery as a stressed underfloor member. 2021 model year added the heat pump with "octovalve" thermal module; 2023 saw HW4 computer and ultrasonic-sensor deletion (Tesla Vision). The 2024 Highland refresh brought a stiffer body shell, frequency-selective dampers, new front suspension geometry/bushings, 360° acoustic glass, drag coefficient cut to 0.219 (from 0.225), stalkless steering column (wheel-button turn signals), and a rear passenger screen.

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: The default EV sport sedan; strong time-attack and track-day scene around the Performance trim, and the deepest EV aftermarket chassis catalog of any car. Enthusiasts accept the sparse interior for the powertrain response; the 2024+ Performance (adaptive dampers, 510 hp) is broadly considered the first "complete" enthusiast Model 3. Tuning culture is constrained by Tesla's software lock — most "builds" are suspension/tire/aero.

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Rivals: BMW i4/3 Series, Polestar 2, Hyundai Ioniq 6. Beats all on efficiency, charging network, and acceleration per dollar; trails the BMW on steering feel and cabin quality. Reliability is mid-pack — mechanically simple powertrain, but suspension wear items and build-quality defects generate more shop visits than Japanese sedans.

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: Shares the Model 3 drive units — rear permanent-magnet synchronous reluctance motor (RWD and rear of AWD cars) plus front AC induction motor on Dual Motor trims. Battery: ~75–81 kWh usable on Long Range/Performance (2170 cells; Giga Texas AWD cars from 2022 use 4680 structural pack); LFP chemistry on later RWD/Standard cars. EPA-filed per-motor kW ratings for Juniper trims: Not verified — needs OEM source.

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: Single-speed fixed-ratio reduction gear (~9.0:1) per drive unit; no multi-gear transmission.

horsepower.label: Horsepower
horsepower.text: Tesla does not publish most Model Y outputs. Verified/EPA-adjacent figures: RWD (2021 Standard Range) 271 hp; 2025 Long Range RWD 295 hp @ 7,000 rpm (U.S. News/EPA-format data); Performance ≈455–456 hp (2020–2024); Long Range AWD combined — Not verified — needs OEM source (commonly cited ~384 hp, unverified); 2025–2026 Juniper Performance reported at 510+ hp (UK/EU reporting; US figure not officially published).

torque.label: Torque
torque.text: Long Range RWD (2025) 309 lb-ft; Dual Motor Long Range ≈376 lb-ft; Performance ≈487 lb-ft.

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: RWD (Standard Range 2021, Long Range RWD 2024+, Juniper Long Range RWD); AWD dual motor (Long Range, Performance) — no FWD or 4WD variants.

dimensions.label: Dimensions
dimensions.text: 2020–2024: length 187.0 in, width 75.6 in (83.8 in w/ mirrors), height 63.9 in, wheelbase 113.8 in; curb ≈4,416 lb (2020 Long Range AWD, Edmunds) — trim range roughly 4,200–4,420 lb. 2025+ Juniper: length 188.6–188.7 in, width 75.6 in, height 63.9 in, wheelbase 113.8 in; curb ≈4,568 lb (2,072 kg, AU-spec Long Range AWD; US curb figures Not verified — needs OEM source).

mpg.label: Fuel economy (MPG)
mpg.text: EPA MPGe (city/hwy/combined, fueleconomy.gov): 2022: RWD 140/119/129; AWD (4680) 129/116/123; Long Range AWD 127/117/122; Performance 115/106/111. 2024: RWD 128/112/120; Long Range RWD 128/112/120; Long Range AWD 122/112/117; Performance 111/98/105. 2025 Juniper: Long Range RWD 134/117/125; Long Range AWD 123/111/117; Performance 110/97/104.

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: Below-average-to-middling historically: Consumer Reports-based used-vehicle scoring of 2.5/5 (2020–2024 cars); J.D. Power 2024 Model Y Quality & Reliability 69/100 ("Fair") though owners rate driving experience 81/100. Consumer Reports named it best EV of 2024 on road-test/satisfaction grounds. Physical recalls: 21V912000 (front/rear suspension knuckles may fracture, 826 cars), 20V709000 (upper control arm-to-knuckle bolts), 21V835000 (lateral link fasteners, shared w/ Model 3), 23V385000 (steering wheel fastener), 24V886000 (seat recliner welds); large OTA campaigns (TPMS 24V935000 ~696k; Autosteer 23V838000) also apply.

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: Early-build (2020–2021) panel gaps, tailgate misalignment, and thin, chip-prone paint. Shared-with-Model-3 front upper control arm squeak (water in ball joints). Heat pump/octovalve failures and defrost recall 22V050000 (2020–2022 cars); A/C compressor failures documented 2020–2023. Stiff/harsh ride pre-2023 — Tesla issued a "Comfort Suspension" revision (springs/dampers) in late 2022/early 2023. Fast tire wear (weight + torque), rear hatch water ingress/seal complaints, 2023 power-steering loss investigation resolved via recall 25V092000 (OTA). Suspension knuckle fracture recall (21V912000).

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: Identical Tesla schedule to Model 3: tire rotation every 6,250 miles; brake fluid check every 4 years; cabin air filter every 2 years; A/C desiccant bag every 4 years; brake caliper clean/lubrication every 12,500 miles in salt-belt states; no oil, plugs, belts, or transmission fluid service.

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: 0–60 mph: Long Range AWD 4.8 s (2020–2024) / 4.6 s (2025+ Juniper); Performance 3.5 s (2020–2024) / 3.3 s (2025–2026 Juniper Performance). Top speed 135 mph (Long Range), 155 mph (Performance). 2022 Giga Texas AWD trim briefly offered a 279-mile structural-pack variant (0–60 ~5.0 s).

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: Milder tuning culture than Model 3: lowering springs/coilovers (Mountain Pass, Unplugged), wheel/tire packages, aero and off-road-lite accessories. Official Tesla Acceleration Boost available for Long Range AWD; Ingenext modules circulate on forums with warranty caveats. Juniper Performance (staggered wheels, adaptive damping) is the factory enthusiast variant.

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: Model 3-derived crossover sharing ~75% of parts, but notable manufacturing firsts: single-piece rear giga-cast underbody (from launch), later front casting, and — on 2022 Giga Texas AWD cars — a structural battery pack using 4680 cells. Heat pump standard from launch. HW4 computer from 2023. The 2025 Juniper refresh adds a full-width front light bar, revised suspension tuning and bushings, improved cabin sealing/NVH, ventilated seats, power-folding rear seats, and retains the turn-signal stalk (unlike Highland).

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: The best-selling vehicle in the world (2023) — community skews practical rather than tuner-focused. Performance trim has a following for its 3.5-s pace, and the Model 3 aftermarket carries over almost 1:1. Most enthusiast effort goes into ride-quality fixes (suspension, tires) for pre-2023 cars.

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Rivals: Ford Mustang Mach-E, Hyundai Ioniq 5, VW ID.4, Chevrolet Equinox EV. Class-leading efficiency and the Supercharger network remain the trump cards; Mach-E and Ioniq 5 ride more comfortably, and several rivals now match cabin quality. Reliability trails the Korean EVs but the powertrain itself is durable — most shop work is suspension, HVAC (heat pump), and trim.

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: All variants use 3-phase AC induction motors (2012–2019) — a large rear drive unit (LDU) on RWD/Performance cars, plus a smaller front drive unit on "D" dual-motor cars; from the April 2019 "Raven" update, the front unit became a Model 3-type permanent-magnet synchronous reluctance motor. 2021+ refresh: permanent-magnet motors throughout; Plaid uses three carbon-fiber-sleeve-rotor motors (one front, two rear). Batteries: 70/75/85/90/100 kWh packs using 18650 cells through 2021 (~350–400 V architecture); 2021+ uses a revised ~100 kWh pack. No engine codes exist; drive units are identified by part-number families (LDU/SDU, later 3D-series).

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: Single-speed fixed-ratio reduction gear integrated into each drive unit (~9.7:1 rear on early large drive units); no multi-gear transmission, no clutch, no scheduled gear-oil change.

horsepower.label: Horsepower
horsepower.text: 2015: 85 RWD 362 hp; 85D 376 hp (188 hp per axle; battery-limited combined output quoted at ~417 hp); P85D 691 hp (Tesla-claimed). 2015–2016: 90D ~525 hp (sum of motor ratings; battery-limited figures vary by source); P90D 691 hp (762 hp with Ludicrous). 2016–2018: 75/75D and 100D outputs — Not verified — needs OEM source; P100D 680 hp. 2019–2020 (Raven): Long Range and Performance outputs — Not verified — needs OEM source. 2021+: Long Range (later "AWD") 670 hp; Plaid 1,020 hp.

torque.label: Torque
torque.text: 85D 362 lb-ft (Tesla launch figures); P85D 713 lb-ft; P90D ≈686–713 lb-ft (sources vary); P100D 791 lb-ft; 2021+ Long Range 660 lb-ft; Plaid 1,050 lb-ft.

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: RWD (single rear motor: 70, 85) and AWD dual motor (all "D" trims) 2015–2020; AWD only from 2021 (dual-motor Long Range, tri-motor Plaid with rear torque vectoring).

dimensions.label: Dimensions
dimensions.text: 2015–2020: length 195.7–196.0 in, width 77.3 in (86.2 in w/ mirrors), height 56.5–56.9 in, wheelbase 116.5 in; curb ≈4,320 lb (85 RWD) to 4,647 lb (P85D, Edmunds/KBB) and ~4,900+ lb (P100D). 2021+: length 197.7 in, width 78.2 in, height 56.3 in, wheelbase 116.5 in; curb 4,561 lb (Long Range) to 4,776–4,828 lb (Plaid).

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: The most-recalled Tesla: 39 NHTSA recall orders across 11 years (Model X: 38), per a March 2025 NHTSA-data study — though many were OTA software fixes. Consumer Reports reliability has historically been below average for this model. Major physical recalls: 21V035000 (MCU1 8GB eMMC wear-out, 134,951 cars, 2012–2018), 18V204000 (Bosch steering-rack bolt corrosion, 70,421 cars, 2012–2016), 19V002000 (Takata passenger air-bag inflator, 68,673 cars, 2014–2016), 21V00B000 (frunk secondary latch, 130,441 cars, 2014–2021), 22V818000 (EPAS loss after pothole strikes, 2017–2021). 2021+ cars are materially better but still see air-suspension, door-unlock-in-crash (23V862000), and horn-pad/airbag (25V172000) campaigns.

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: MCU1 eMMC flash wear-out killing the center screen (recall 21V035000 — Tesla admitted all units would eventually fail). Flush door-handle failures (paddle gear/microswitch; multiple handle generations). Early large drive units: rotor bearing/seal failures and "milling" noise — Tesla redesigned the unit mid-2015; pre-revision cars often have multiple drive-unit replacements. Front lower control arm cracking (TSB-19-31-001; Williams v. Tesla class action, chiefly 2013–2014 arms). Air suspension strut leaks and compressor wear. Half-shaft/drive-axle shudder under load on higher-mileage dual-motor cars. Steering-rack bolt corrosion (recall). Takata airbags (recall). On 2021+ cars: yoke rim wear, door-unlock-in-crash software recall, and Plaid stock-brake fade in track use (factory Track Package adds carbon-ceramics).

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: Tire rotation every 6,250 miles; brake fluid health check every 4 years; cabin air filter every 3 years (Model S); HEPA + carbon filter every 3 years (Bioweapon Defense Mode cars); A/C desiccant bag every 3 years (Model S); brake caliper clean/lubricate every 12,500 miles where roads are salted. Air suspension has no scheduled service interval. No oil, plugs, belts, or transmission service.

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: 0–60 mph: 85 RWD 5.4 s; 85D 5.2 s; P85D 3.2 s ("Insane"); P90D Ludicrous 2.8 s; P100D Ludicrous 2.5 s (independently tested at 2.28 s with rollout); 2019 Raven Performance 2.4 s; 2021+ Long Range 3.1 s; Plaid 1.99 s (Tesla claim, rollout subtracted) and ~9.2–9.3 s quarter-mile, 200 mph top speed with Track Package. Ludicrous/Launch modes are the factory "performance trim" equipment across generations.

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: Drag-strip culture dominates the Plaid/Raven cars (slicks, lightweight wheels, weight reduction); Plaid Track Package (carbon-ceramic brakes, forged wheels, 200-mph software unlock) is the notable factory option. Older cars get MCU2 retrofits, suspension refreshes, and aftermarket control arms rather than power mods — output is software-locked. Aftermarket limited-slip-style tuning does not exist; Plaid rear torque vectoring is factory.

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: Tesla's first clean-sheet "skateboard" platform (2012): aluminum-intensive body/chassis with the battery pack as a flat stressed floor member, giving a 116.5-in wheelbase and ~0.24–0.208 Cd across generations. Dual-motor "D" AWD arrived October 2014; the 2016 facelift deleted the nosecone grille; the April 2019 "Raven" update swapped the front induction motor for a Model 3-type permanent-magnet unit, added fully adaptive air suspension, and lifted EPA range to 370 miles. The 2021 "Palladium" refresh brought a new interior (17-in landscape screen, yoke), heat pump, revised ~100-kWh pack, and the tri-motor Plaid with carbon-sleeved rotors enabling sustained high-rpm output.

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: The car that made EVs credible — P85D/P100D "Ludicrous" launches defined the mid-2010s straight-line scene, and the Plaid is the default answer at drag strips (1.99-s claims, 9-second quarter-miles stock). Enthusiast respect for the powertrain is total; respect for build quality is not. Cheap early cars (2013–2016) are tempting but buyers are warned to budget for MCU, drive-unit, door-handle, and air-suspension work.

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Rivals: Lucid Air, Porsche Taycan, Mercedes-Benz EQS, BMW i7. The Model S wins on straight-line speed per dollar, efficiency, and Supercharger access; Lucid beats it on range and chassis polish, Taycan on build quality and repeatable performance, and the Germans on refinement. Reliability is the weak leg — it is the most-recalled Tesla and the oldest design here.

tesla-model-x.subtitle: Mid/full-size SUV with falcon-wing rear doors, 2016–2020 (pre-refresh; "Raven" from April 2019), 2021–2026 ("Palladium" refresh)

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: Shares Model S powertrains. 2016–2019: 3-phase AC induction motors — large rear drive unit plus small front drive unit (dual-motor AWD standard on US cars); 75/90/100 kWh 18650-cell packs. April 2019 "Raven": Model 3-type permanent-magnet front motor, 100 kWh pack. 2021+ refresh: permanent-magnet motors throughout — dual-motor Long Range/AWD (670 hp) and tri-motor Plaid (one front, two rear carbon-sleeve-rotor motors, ~100 kWh revised pack). No engine codes; drive units identified by part-number families (LDU/SDU, 3D-series).

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: Single-speed fixed-ratio reduction gear integrated into each drive unit; no multi-gear transmission; "1-speed direct drive" per EPA/Edmunds listings.

horsepower.label: Horsepower
horsepower.text: 2016–2018: 75D ≈330 hp; 90D ≈417 hp; P90D ≈532 hp (up to ~691 hp with Ludicrous, shared with Model S P90D powertrain); P100D ≈680 hp (shared with Model S P100D powertrain). 2019–2020 (Raven) Long Range/Performance: Not verified — needs OEM source. 2021+: Long Range/AWD 670 hp; Plaid 1,020 hp.

torque.label: Torque
torque.text: 75D ≈387 lb-ft (525 Nm); 90D ≈479 lb-ft (650 Nm). P90D/P100D and all 2021+ trims: Not verified — needs OEM source (Model S siblings quote 713/791 lb-ft and 660/1,050 lb-ft respectively, but Tesla does not publish Model X-specific figures).

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: AWD dual motor standard on all US-market Model X; 2021+ adds tri-motor Plaid AWD with rear torque vectoring. No RWD/FWD variants.

dimensions.label: Dimensions
dimensions.text: 2016–2020: length 198.3 in, width 78.7 in body (89.4 in w/ mirrors), height 66.0 in, wheelbase 116.7 in; curb 5,381 lb (2017 P100D, Edmunds) to 5,531 lb (2019 P100D, Edmunds); lighter trims below that — exact figures Not verified. 2021+: length 198.3–199.1 in (sources vary by model year), width 78.7 in, wheelbase 116.7 in; curb 5,248 lb (2025 Plaid, KBB/U.S. News). Tow rating 4,960–5,000 lb.

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: Second most-recalled Tesla after Model S — 38 NHTSA recall orders (per a March 2025 NHTSA-data study). Consumer Reports documented early-build quality failures in 2016 (falcon doors, trim, electronics) and has consistently rated Model X reliability well below average. Major physical recalls: 21V035000 (MCU1 8GB eMMC wear-out incl. 2016–2018 X), 20V062000 (Bosch steering-rack bolt corrosion, 14,113 cars, 2015–2016), 20V710000 + 24V615000 (roof/spine trim panels bonded without primer can detach, 9,136 cars, 2016), 23V679000 (low-brake-fluid warning, 54,676), 22V843000 (RCM passenger airbag calibration), 22V233000 (side-curtain airbags with windows down), 23V488000 (seat-belt pretensioner anchors), 25V172000 (horn pad/airbag, 2021–2024 S / 2021–2025 X).

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: Falcon-wing door faults are the signature problem: failure to open/close, false obstruction detection, misalignment and water leaks — documented by Consumer Reports (2016), WIRED, and owner forums persisting into later builds. Early third-row seatback weakness (April 2016 recall of ~2,700 early cars). MCU1 eMMC screen death (recall). Roof trim detachment above the windshield header (recalls 20V710000/24V615000). The panoramic windshield — largest in production — is expensive to replace and owner-reported stress cracks are common; verify glass part numbers by VIN. Also: steering-rack bolt corrosion (recall), air-suspension strut leaks, half-shaft shudder on dual-motor cars, auto-presenting front door sensor failures, and second-row "monopost" seat motor faults.

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: Tesla EV schedule: tire rotation every 6,250 miles; brake fluid check every 4 years; cabin air filter every 2 years (Model X); HEPA + carbon filter (Bioweapon Defense Mode) every 3 years; A/C desiccant bag every 4 years (Model X); brake caliper clean/lubricate every 12,500 miles in road-salt regions. No oil, plugs, belts, or transmission fluid service. Falcon-wing door mechanisms have no scheduled service but should be inspected for seal alignment and sensor operation.

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: 0–60 mph: 75D ≈4.9–5.2 s; 90D ≈4.8 s; 100D 4.7 s; P100D 2.9 s (manufacturer figure, Ludicrous); 2019–2020 Raven Performance ≈2.7 s — Not verified — needs OEM source. 2021+: Long Range 3.8 s, top speed 149–155 mph; Plaid 2.5 s (Tesla claim). Ludicrous/Launch mode on Performance/Plaid trims.

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: Minimal powertrain tuning (software-locked). Owners fit 20/22-in wheel swaps (note: 22-in turbines cost ~10 miles of range), suspension links for lowering, PPF/wraps, and towing accessories up to the 5,000-lb rating. The Plaid is the factory halo variant; no meaningful aftermarket power modifications exist.

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: Model S-derived platform stretched into a 116.7-in-wheelbase SUV, launched late 2015 (first US deliveries fall 2015; 2016 model year). Signature hardware: double-hinged falcon-wing rear doors with ultrasonic/capacitive anti-pinch sensing, auto-presenting front doors, and a windshield that extends over the front occupants. Standard HEPA "Bioweapon Defense Mode" filtration. The April 2019 Raven update brought the permanent-magnet front motor and fully adaptive air suspension (371-mile Long Range EPA). The 2021 refresh (Palladium) added the Model S Plaid powertrain, revised ~100-kWh pack with stronger cooling, heat pump, landscape 17-in screen, and yoke (later optional round wheel).

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: Respected as the original fast family EV — a 2.5-s three-row SUV still has no direct ICE analog — but the enthusiast community treats it as a tech showcase rather than a driver's car. Falcon doors are divisive: great party trick, real repair bills. Plaid and cheap used P90D/P100D cars are the cult trims.

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Rivals: Rivian R1S, Lucid Gravity, BMW iX, Mercedes EQS SUV. The Model X is quickest per dollar and has the best charging network; the R1S beats it off-road and on packaging, Lucid Gravity on range/space efficiency, and the Germans on build quality. Worst reliability record in its class — the most-recalled luxury EV — with unique repair exposure (doors, windshield, air suspension) that shops should price accordingly.

tesla-cybertruck.subtitle: Full-size electric pickup, 2024–2026 (single generation; RWD/Long Range, AWD, tri-motor Cyberbeast)

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: Permanent-magnet synchronous drive units; AWD = dual motor (600 hp); Cyberbeast = tri-motor (845 hp; one front, two rear with torque vectoring); 2025+ Long Range RWD = single rear motor (321 hp). Battery: ~123 kWh structural pack of 4680-format cells on an ~800 V architecture (the pack internally splits into lower-voltage sections for 400 V charging compatibility). Motor part codes/per-motor kW: Not verified — needs OEM source.

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: Single-speed fixed-ratio reduction gear per drive unit; no multi-gear transmission; electronic locking differential function on dual-motor/tri-motor trucks.

horsepower.label: Horsepower
horsepower.text: Long Range RWD 321 hp; AWD 600 hp; Cyberbeast 845 hp (all Tesla-published figures).

torque.label: Torque
torque.text: Tesla does not publish motor-shaft torque for Cybertruck; it quotes wheel-torque figures that are not comparable to conventional ratings. Not verified — needs OEM source.

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: RWD (2025+ Long Range, single motor); AWD dual motor; AWD tri-motor (Cyberbeast) with rear torque vectoring. Steer-by-wire with 4-wheel steering (rear steer) standard.

dimensions.label: Dimensions
dimensions.text: Length 223.7 in, width 80.0 in body / 86.6 in folded mirrors / 95.0 in mirrors extended, height 70.5–70.6 in, wheelbase 143.1 in; ground clearance 5.0–17.4 in (air suspension, "Extract" mode). Curb weight 6,603 lb (AWD, Tesla) / 6,843–6,863 lb (Cyberbeast, by model year); Long Range RWD curb — Not verified — needs OEM source. Payload ≈2,500 lb (1-ton class); towing 11,000 lb (AWD/Cyberbeast), 7,500 lb (RWD).

mpg.label: Fuel economy (MPG)
mpg.text: EPA MPGe (city/hwy/combined, fueleconomy.gov, 2025): AWD 85/72/79 (43 kWh/100 mi); Long Range (RWD) 93/72/82 (41 kWh/100 mi); Long Range w/ 20-in wheels 89/67/78 (43 kWh/100 mi). Cyberbeast MPGe: Not listed on the fueleconomy.gov 2025 make page — needs verification. 2024 model-year EPA figures: not present on the fueleconomy.gov 2024 Tesla page — Not verified.

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: The worst launch-quality record in Tesla's lineup: 8 NHTSA recall campaigns in its first 17 months (Nov 2023–Mar 2025), per NHTSA-data analysis — 24V276000 (accelerator pedal pad can dislodge and trap pedal, 3,878), 24V456000 (windshield wiper motor controller failure, 11,688), 24V457000 (trunk-bed sail trim adhesion, 12,150), 24V718000 (rearview camera image delay, 27,185, OTA), 24V832000 (drive inverter fault/loss of drive, 2,431), 24V935000 (TPMS warning light, OTA), 24V051000 (warning-light font size, OTA), 25V170000 (cant rail stainless trim delamination/detachment, 46,096 trucks built Nov 2023–Feb 2025). No IIHS rating as of this writing; NHTSA crash rating — Not verified.

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: Beyond the eight recalls above (pedal pad, wiper motor, sail/cant-rail trim detachment, drive inverter): stainless panels show fingerprints, water spots and surface corrosion specks requiring specific cleaning (owner-documented; Tesla publishes cleaning guidance); adhesive-bonded stainless trim is a recurring detachment theme; early trucks shipped without a functional wiper design margin (single huge blade + recalled motor); real-world range frequently lands below the 340/320-mile ratings, especially towing; widely publicized durability testing (TFLtruck, WhistlinDiesel) broke the rear frame/hitch area under abusive towing — a known discussion point for customers planning heavy trailer use. Frunk and tonneau-cover fitment complaints are common on early builds.

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: Tesla EV schedule applies: tire rotation every 6,250 miles (heavy truck + high torque = fast tire wear; expect aggressive rear wear), brake fluid check every 4 years, cabin air filter replacement (interval per owner's manual — verify by model year), brake caliper clean/lubricate every 12,500 miles in salt regions. No oil, plugs, belts, or transmission fluid service. Steer-by-wire, 48 V system and air suspension have no published service intervals; wiper blade is a long single unit — stock the correct part.

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: 0–60 mph: AWD 4.1 s (top speed 112 mph); Cyberbeast 2.6 s, quarter-mile under 11 s (top speed 130 mph); Long Range RWD ≈6.2–6.5 s (original RWD claim 6.5 s). 17.4-in max ground clearance, 35-in approach / 28-in departure angles, adaptive air suspension with ~12 in of travel. Announced bed-mounted range extender battery (~440–470 mi) — availability status Not verified (reported dropped in 2025).

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: The most-wrapped vehicle in America — PPF/vinyl is nearly universal because raw stainless stains. Common mods: wheel/tire packages (all-terrains), lift/level links for the air suspension, light bars and bed racks, molle panels. Unplugged Performance's "UP Invincible" line (bumpers, skid plates, rock sliders) is the notable off-road program. No powertrain tuning exists; output is software-locked.

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: Clean-sheet truck on Tesla's newest architecture: 30X cold-rolled stainless-steel exoskeleton (no paint; panels can't be stamped conventionally), structural 4680 battery pack, ~800 V high-voltage system enabling 325 kW+ Supercharging, and a 48 V low-voltage system (replacing 12/16 V) that cuts wiring current 4× and enabled "Etherloop" loop wiring. First Tesla with steer-by-wire (no mechanical column; variable ratio) plus rear-wheel steering — Tesla claims a tighter turning circle than Model S. Air suspension, bi-directional "Powershare" charging (9.6 kW+ outlets), and an 18.5-in center screen.

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: Polarizing halo vehicle — huge social-media presence, drag-strip novelty (2.6-s truck), and a real off-road accessory scene, but the community is split between wrap/lifestyle owners and truck traditionalists who question towing range and durability. Aftermarket is accessories-only; nobody is "tuning" these. Early depreciation has been steep, which is feeding a used-market enthusiast niche.

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Rivals: Rivian R1T, Ford F-150 Lightning, Chevrolet Silverado EV, GMC Hummer EV. Cybertruck wins on acceleration, charging architecture (800 V, 325 kW), and sticker shock value; R1T is the better off-roader with a conventional bed/spare, Lightning and Silverado EV tow and work more conventionally, and all rivals offer dealer service networks. Reliability and trim build quality are currently worst-in-class — 8 recalls in 17 months — so shops should expect recall-verification work (VIN check) on every first visit.

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: XV50 facelift: 2.5L 2AR-FE naturally aspirated I4; 3.5L 2GR-FE naturally aspirated V6; Hybrid: 2.5L 2AR-FXE Atkinson-cycle I4 + 2JM motor. XV70: 2.5L A25A-FKS "Dynamic Force" naturally aspirated I4 (D-4S port+direct injection); 3.5L 2GR-FKS naturally aspirated V6; Hybrid: 2.5L A25A-FXS Atkinson I4 + 3NM motor (THS II). XV80: hybrid-only in the US — 2.5L A25A-FXS I4 (5th-gen THS); V6 discontinued.

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: XV50: 6-speed automatic — U760E (I4), U660E (V6); e-CVT (hybrid). XV70: 8-speed UB80E automatic (2.5L), 8-speed UA80E automatic (V6), Aisin T110 e-CVT (hybrid). XV80: Aisin T110 e-CVT only (hybrid).

horsepower.label: Horsepower
horsepower.text: XV50: 2AR-FE 178 hp @ 6,000 rpm; 2GR-FE 268 hp @ 6,200 rpm; hybrid 200 hp combined. XV70: A25A-FKS 203 hp (206 hp XSE/quad exhaust) @ 6,600 rpm; 2GR-FKS 301 hp @ 6,600 rpm; hybrid 208 hp combined. XV80: 225 hp combined FWD / 232 hp combined AWD.

torque.label: Torque
torque.text: XV50: 2AR-FE 170 lb-ft @ 4,100 rpm; 2GR-FE 248 lb-ft @ 4,700 rpm. XV70: A25A-FKS 184 lb-ft @ 5,000 rpm (AWD: 182 lb-ft); 2GR-FKS 267 lb-ft @ 4,700 rpm; hybrid engine 163 lb-ft @ 3,600–5,200 rpm. XV80: engine 163 lb-ft; system torque figure Not verified — needs OEM source.

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: XV50: FWD only. XV70: FWD standard; AWD (with 8-speed, 2.5L only) added for 2020 model year — first AWD Camry since 1991; V6 and TRD are FWD only. XV80: FWD standard; electronic on-demand AWD (rear electric motor) optional on all trims.

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: XV50 facelift is one of the most trouble-free modern Camrys — Consumer Reports rates it above average; J.D. Power ranked the Camry at/near the top of midsize cars in its dependability studies; NHTSA 5-star overall. XV70: Consumer Reports above-average ratings; IIHS Top Safety Pick+ (2018–2023); J.D. Power quality awards for TMMK-built cars. XV80: too new for long-term data; IIHS "Good" in all 2025 crash tests including updated moderate overlap.

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: XV50: 2AR-FE excessive oil consumption was a documented issue on earlier 2AR-FE engines (2010–2014 customer support program); 2015–2017 facelift cars are affected far less but monitor oil level at high mileage. U760E 6-speed: owner reports of torque-converter shudder/harsh shifts addressed via TSB reflashes. XV70: Denso low-pressure fuel pump recall (NHTSA 20V012 and expansions — impeller swells, engine stalls; covers 2018–2019 Camry); UB80E/UA80E 8-speed harsh or "hunting" shift complaints addressed by TSB calibration updates; early A25A-FKS coolant electric water pump failures documented in aftermarket/TSB channels. XV80: no established pattern yet — too new.

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: Oil 0W-20 (XV50/XV70 I4 & V6; XV70 I4 also approves 0W-16 from later years; XV80 spec 0W-16) every 10,000 mi/12 mo (5,000 mi severe). Iridium spark plugs 120,000 mi. Toyota Super Long Life Coolant: first change 100,000 mi, then 50,000 mi. ATF WS "lifetime fill" — inspect; severe-service drain-and-fill ~60,000 mi shop practice. Brake fluid: inspect per schedule; common OEM-dealer replacement interval 2–3 yr/20,000–30,000 mi. All engines timing chain.

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: XV50 V6 ~5.8 s 0–60; I4 ~8.3 s; Hybrid ~7.6 s. XV70: 2.5L ~7.6–7.9 s; V6 ~5.8 s (C&D-tested TRD 5.8 s); Hybrid ~7.4 s. XV80: ~6.8 s (C&D, XSE AWD). Factory performance trim: Camry TRD (2020–2024, XV70) — V6, sport suspension, TRD exhaust, aero kit; XV80 has no performance trim (Nightshade is cosmetic, added 2026).

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: Modest scene: ECU tunes exist for XV70 2GR-FKS and A25A-FKS (AMT Tuning, JD Tuning stage-1 packages); lowering springs/coilovers (TEIN, BC Racing), sway bars, strut braces, 18–19 in wheels; TRD factory parts (springs, exhaust, wheels) carry over from dealer channels. XV50 commonly gets bolt-ons only — intake, cat-back exhaust, wheels.

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: XV50 rides on the Toyota K platform carried from XV40; the 2015 facelift ("big minor change") replaced every exterior panel except the roof and stiffened the body with added spot welds. XV70 moved to TNGA GA-K with a ~30% more rigid body, double-wishbone rear suspension, and the new A25A Dynamic Force engine (14.0:1 compression hybrid, 13.0:1 gas) plus Direct Shift 8-speeds. XV80 retains GA-K with revised dampers and tuning, and converts the US line to 5th-generation THS II hybrid-only with optional electronic AWD.

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: The Camry is respected but not loved — the XV70 TRD is the cult pick ("a V6 sport sedan hiding in plain sight"). Tuner support is thin versus Honda; most activity is wheels/suspension and V6 bolt-ons. XV50 V6 cars are prized as cheap, durable sleepers.

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Rivals: Honda Accord, Nissan Altima, Hyundai Sonata, Kia K5, Mazda6 (to 2021). Against the Accord, the Camry trades some steering feel for a smoother ride and historically stronger long-term reliability scores (Consumer Reports/J.D. Power); the XV70 V6 and XV80 hybrid mpg figures are class-leading, and resale value is consistently top-of-segment.

toyota-corolla.subtitle: 4-door compact sedan (hatchback noted where relevant) — 2015–2019 (E170), 2020–2026 (E210; hatchback from 2019)

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: E170 (US): 1.8L 2ZR-FE Dual VVT-i naturally aspirated I4; LE Eco: 1.8L 2ZR-FAE Valvematic I4. E210 (US sedan): 1.8L 2ZR-FAE I4 (L/LE/XLE, 2020–2022); 2.0L M20A-FKS Dynamic Force I4 (SE/XSE; all trims from 2023); Hybrid: 1.8L 2ZR-FXE Atkinson I4 + motor (THS II). Hatchback (2019+): 2.0L M20A-FKS only. GR Corolla (2023+): 1.6L G16E-GTS turbo I3.

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: E170: K313 CVT (Super CVT-i, 7 simulated ratios), 6-speed EC65 manual (S Plus/50th Anniversary). E210: K120 "Direct Shift" CVT with physical launch gear (2.0L), K313 CVT (1.8L sedan, 2020–2022), 6-speed EG60 intelligent manual (SE sedan 2019–2022, hatch to 2025), e-CVT (hybrid). GR Corolla: 6MT or 8-speed GR-DAT automatic (2025+).

horsepower.label: Horsepower
horsepower.text: E170: 2ZR-FE 132 hp @ 6,000 rpm; 2ZR-FAE (LE Eco) 140 hp @ 6,100 rpm. E210: 2ZR-FAE 139 hp @ 6,100 rpm; M20A-FKS 169 hp @ 6,600 rpm; hybrid 121 hp combined (2020–2022) / 138 hp combined (2023+ AWD versions); GR Corolla 300 hp @ 6,500 rpm.

torque.label: Torque
torque.text: E170: 2ZR-FE 128 lb-ft @ 4,400 rpm; 2ZR-FAE 126 lb-ft @ 3,900 rpm. E210: 2ZR-FAE 126 lb-ft @ 3,900 rpm; M20A-FKS 151 lb-ft @ 4,400 rpm; hybrid engine 105 lb-ft. GR Corolla 273 lb-ft (295 lb-ft from 2025).

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: E170: FWD only. E210: FWD; Hybrid adds electronic on-demand AWD (E-Four, rear motor) as an option from 2023. GR Corolla: GR-FOUR full-time AWD with adjustable torque split.

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: E170: Consumer Reports much-better-than-average reliability across the run; among the most dependable compact sedans ever measured. E210: Consumer Reports above average; IIHS Top Safety Pick (sedan, multiple years); NHTSA 5-star overall (2019+ hatchback). GR Corolla excluded — low-volume performance model.

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: E170: Takata passenger airbag inflator recalls (phased NHTSA campaigns cover 2015–2019 Corolla); minor CVT drone/judder complaints; generally no systemic powertrain faults. E210: 2019 Corolla Hatchback K120 CVT recall — CVT vane wheel (impeller) can detach under high load causing loss of motive power; remedy is transmission replacement (NHTSA, 1 recall on 2019 hatch, ~3,400 units). Some early K120 launch-gear/belt complaints documented; 2020+ sedan largely trouble-free. Denso fuel-pump recall family touches 2019–2021 production.

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: Oil 0W-20 (0W-16 on later M20A/hybrid) every 10,000 mi/12 mo. Iridium plugs 120,000 mi. SLLC coolant 100,000 mi first / 50,000 mi after. CVT fluid FE — inspect; drain-and-fill ~60,000 mi severe service. Brake fluid inspect; 2–3 yr replacement common dealer practice. All engines timing chain. Hybrid: check inverter coolant pump operation at major services.

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: E170: ~9.5–10.0 s 0–60. E210: 2.0L sedan ~8.0 s (C&D); hybrid ~10.3 s; GR Corolla ~4.9 s (C&D). Factory sport trims: SE/XSE (sport suspension), Apex Edition (2021, lowered/stiffened, 6,000 units, 120 with manual), GR Corolla (Core/Circuit/Premium, Morizo Edition 2023).

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: E210 hatch/sedan: coilovers and lowering springs (BC Racing, TEIN), rear sway bars, intakes, cat-back exhausts (MagnaFlow, Borla), wheel/tire packages; K120 CVT discourages power tuning — community tunes focus on throttle response. GR Corolla has a real tuner scene: ECU tunes (COBB Accessport), downpipes, intercoolers, suspension and aero. E170: mostly cosmetic — wheels, tint, audio.

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: E170 sits on Toyota's New MC platform with a 3.9-in-longer wheelbase than E140 and conventional torsion-beam rear suspension. E210 moves to TNGA GA-C (shared with Prius/C-HR), gaining a multilink rear suspension, lower cowl and seating position, and — on 2.0L cars — the K120 Direct Shift CVT whose mechanical launch gear eliminates off-the-line CVT lag. The 2023 refresh standardized the 169-hp M20A-FKS and added hybrid AWD.

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: The base Corolla is respected as an appliance, not a project car — until the GR Corolla, which has genuine cult status (rally-derived AWD, hand-built G16E engine) and strong tuner support. The E210 hatchback with the 6-speed iMT is the budget-enthusiast pick. E170 sedans are valued purely as durable commuters.

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Rivals: Honda Civic, Mazda3, Hyundai Elantra, Nissan Sentra. The Civic and Mazda3 drive better; the Corolla counters with best-in-class hybrid mpg, top-tier reliability scores (Consumer Reports), and cheaper upkeep. GR Corolla undercuts the Civic Type R and VW Golf R on price while matching them on character.

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: XA40 (US): 2.5L 2AR-FE naturally aspirated I4; Hybrid (2016–2018): 2.5L 2AR-FXE Atkinson I4 + front/rear motors. XA50 (US): 2.5L A25A-FKS Dynamic Force I4; Hybrid: 2.5L A25A-FXS I4 (THS II); Prime/Plug-in Hybrid (2021–2025): 2.5L A25A-FXS uprated + 18.1 kWh Li-ion pack. XA60 (2026): hybrid-only in US — 2.5L A25A-FXS (6th-gen THS); plug-in hybrid continues.

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: XA40: 6-speed U760E/F automatic; e-CVT (hybrid). XA50: 8-speed UA80E "Direct Shift" automatic (gas); Aisin T110 e-CVT (hybrid/PHEV). XA60: e-CVT only (hybrid family).

horsepower.label: Horsepower
horsepower.text: XA40: 2AR-FE 176 hp @ 6,000 rpm; hybrid 194 hp combined. XA50: A25A-FKS 203 hp @ 6,600 rpm; hybrid 219 hp combined; Prime 302 hp combined. XA60: 227 hp combined FWD / 236 hp combined AWD (hybrid); PHEV up to ~320 hp combined.

torque.label: Torque
torque.text: XA40: 2AR-FE 172 lb-ft @ 4,100 rpm; hybrid system 206 lb-ft combined. XA50: A25A-FKS 184 lb-ft @ 5,000 rpm; hybrid system 206 lb-ft combined; Prime engine 168 lb-ft @ 2,800 rpm (system figure higher). XA60: system torque Not verified — needs OEM source.

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: XA40: FWD or on-demand AWD (gas); Hybrid AWD standard via rear electric motor (AWD-i). XA50: FWD or AWD — Adventure/Limited get Dynamic Torque Vectoring AWD with rear driveline disconnect; hybrid AWD-i standard; Prime AWD standard. XA60: FWD or E-Four AWD (hybrid).

dimensions.label: Dimensions
dimensions.text: XA40: L 179.9–181.1 in / W 72.6 in / H 65.4–67.1 in / WB 104.7 in; curb 3,417–3,516 lb gas / 3,770–3,935 lb hybrid. XA50: L 179.9–181.1 in / W 73.0 in / H 65.4–66.3 in / WB 105.9 in; curb 3,373–3,616 lb gas / 3,704–3,792 lb hybrid / ~4,300 lb Prime. XA60: Not verified — needs OEM source (LA auto show 2025 reveal; US spec sheet pending at time of writing).

mpg.label: Fuel economy (MPG)
mpg.text: XA40: gas FWD 23/30/26, AWD 22/29/25; Hybrid AWD 34/31/33. XA50: gas FWD 26–27/35/30, AWD 25/33/28 (Adventure/TRD 25/32/27); Hybrid 41/38/40; Prime 94 MPGe combined, 40 mpg combined gas-only, 42-mile EPA EV range. XA60: Not verified — needs OEM source (Toyota targets ~44 mpg combined hybrid per reveal materials; EPA label pending).

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: XA40: Consumer Reports above average; 2016 facelift added TSS-P safety suite; IIHS ratings mixed early (Poor small-overlap driver side 2013–2014, improved to Good for 2015+ after structural changes — note passenger-side small overlap remained Poor). XA50: Consumer Reports recommended; IIHS Top Safety Pick+ (2019+); Euro NCAP 5 stars; J.D. Power compact-SUV dependability leader multiple years. XA60: too new — no data.

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: XA40: IIHS-documented passenger-side small-overlap weakness (2013–2018 body); Takata airbag recalls; hybrid battery cooling-fan clogging on high-mileage examples; Denso fuel-pump recall family touches 2018. XA50: Denso low-pressure fuel pump recall (NHTSA 20V012 and expansions — 2019–2020); 2019–2021 Hybrid fuel-tank/fuel-level-sender customer support program (tank could not be filled to rated capacity — tank/sender replacement covered); roof-rail water-leak TSBs (sealer voids, headliner stains); ESC software update after the 2019 Teknikens Värld moose-test failure (Toyota issued a fix; retested pass January 2020); 8-speed gear-hunting complaints early in the run. XA60: too new.

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: Oil 0W-20 (XA40) / 0W-16 (XA50 A25A; 0W-20 acceptable interim) every 10,000 mi/12 mo. Iridium plugs 120,000 mi. SLLC coolant 100,000/50,000 mi. ATF WS lifetime fill — inspect; hybrid e-CVT uses ATF WS, drain-and-fill ~60,000 mi severe. AWD: rear differential and transfer-case gear oil inspect/replace (Toyota LT GL-5 75W-85). Brake fluid inspect; 2–3 yr replacement common. All engines timing chain.

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: XA40: ~8.3 s 0–60 gas; hybrid 8.1 s (0–100 km/h factory data). XA50: gas ~8.0 s; hybrid ~7.4 s (C&D); Prime 5.5–5.7 s (C&D 5.5 s — second-quickest Toyota of its era behind the GR Supra). Factory off-road trims: Adventure (2017–2025), TRD Off-Road (2020–2024: TRD suspension, all-terrain tires, skid plate), Woodland Edition (2023–2025), GR Sport (Europe).

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: Strong overlanding scene: 1.5–2 in lift kits (ReadyLIFT, Rough Country), all-terrain tires (Falken Wildpeak, BFG KO2) on 17-in wheels, skid plates, roof racks and rooftop tents, rock sliders on TRD/Adventure builds. Power mods are minimal — intakes and cat-back exhausts exist for the A25A; no meaningful turbo/tune path. XA40 builds are mostly mild lift-and-tire packages.

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: XA40 (New MC platform) dropped the side-hinged door and spare tire of the XA30 and added the first US RAV4 Hybrid (2016). XA50 moved to TNGA GA-K with a 57% more rigid unibody, multilink rear suspension, lower-mounted door mirrors for visibility, and world-first Dynamic Torque Vectoring AWD with rear driveline disconnect. XA60 keeps GA-K, goes hybrid-only in the US with 6th-generation THS, and adds an available ~320-hp plug-in hybrid.

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
